# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: The Value Function Ecosystem

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the conceptual relationships among the four core value function objects, the two Bellman equation families, and the downstream control/learning algorithms that consume them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MDP Definition: S, A, P, R, gamma] --> B[Policy pi]
    A --> C[Return Gt]
    B --> D[State Value Vpi of s]
    B --> E[Action Value Qpi of s,a]
    C --> D
    C --> E
    D --> F[Bellman Expectation for Vpi]
    E --> G[Bellman Expectation for Qpi]
    F --> H[Bellman Operator Tpi is gamma Contraction]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Unique Fixed Point Vpi via Banach Theorem]
    I --> J[Policy Evaluation]
    E --> K[Qpi to Vpi Conversion: sum over pi]
    D --> L[Vpi to Qpi Conversion: sum over P]
    K --> M[Optimal Value V star]
    L --> N[Optimal Action Value Q star]
    F --> O[Bellman Optimality for V star]
    G --> P[Bellman Optimality for Q star]
    O --> M
    P --> N
    M --> Q[Greedy Policy pi star of s = argmax of Q star]
    N --> Q
    Q --> R[Value Iteration Algorithm]
    Q --> S[Q Learning Algorithm]
    Q --> T[Deep Q Network DQN]
    D --> U[Advantage Function A pi of s,a = Qpi minus Vpi]
    E --> U
    U --> V[Actor Critic A2C, A3C]
```

> [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Guide**
> - Top region: **Inputs** ŌĆö MDP tuple and policy.
> - Middle region: **Definitions** of $V_\pi$ and $Q_\pi$, plus their Bellman *expectation* equations.
> - Center: **Fixed-point theory** guaranteeing unique solutions.
> - Right side: **Conversion identities** between $V_\pi$ and $Q_\pi$.
> - Bottom region: **Optimality** and the algorithms that arise from the value function machinery.

## 4.2 State-Transition Recursion View

This second diagram illustrates the *recursion* inherent in $V_\pi(s)$: the value of a state decomposes into the immediate reward and the discounted value of the successor state, summed over all stochastic branches.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[State s at time t] -->|action a sampled from pi| A1[Action a]
    A1 -->|transition s prime sampled from P| S1[Next state s prime at t+1]
    S0 -->|immediate reward r| R1[Reward R sub t+1]
    S1 -->|discount gamma| R1
    R1 --> V0[Vpi of s]
    S1 --> V1[Vpi of s prime]
    V1 -->|discount gamma| V0
```

> [!TIP] > **Two-Branch Reading**
> The leftmost arrow chain (top) represents the *stochastic decision-and-transition* path; the bottom chain represents the *value inheritance* from the successor state. Combining both yields the Bellman recursion.

## 4.3 Expectation vs. Optimality ŌĆö Side-by-Side Comparison

This block diagram contrasts the structural difference between a Bellman **Expectation** equation (used in policy evaluation) and a Bellman **Optimality** equation (used in control).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph EXPECT[EXPECTATION EQUATION]
        EX1[Action selector: sum over a weighted by pi of a given s] --> EX2[Environment selector: sum over s prime weighted by P]
        EX2 --> EX3[Output: Vpi of s]
    end
    subgraph OPT[OPTIMALITY EQUATION]
        OP1[Action selector: max over a] --> OP2[Environment selector: sum over s prime weighted by P]
        OP2 --> OP3[Output: V star of s]
    end
    EXPECT --> OPT
    OP3 --> OP4[Action extraction: pi star of s equals argmax of Q star of s,a]
```

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Visual Cue for KTU Answer Scripts**
> In valuation, examiners award explicit marks for *highlighting the operator swap* (sum ŌåÆ max). A diagram like the one above placed in an answer sheet often captures 1ŌĆō2 extra marks because it visually demonstrates mastery of the conceptual transition.

## 4.4 Block Architecture ŌĆö Value Function in a Complete RL Agent

The final schematic shows where value functions sit inside the larger RL agent loop. Although you are not required to write code in PECAT868, the architectural placement is a frequent KTU short-answer / 3-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Environment] -->|state s, reward r| A2[Agent]
    A2 -->|action a| A1
    A2 --> A3[Policy pi of a given s]
    A3 --> A4[Value Estimator: Vpi or Qpi]
    A4 --> A5[Policy Improvement: greedy or epsilon greedy]
    A5 --> A3
    A4 --> A6[Loss Function: Bellman residual]
    A6 --> A7[Optimizer: SGD, Adam]
    A7 --> A4
```

> [!NOTE] > **Why this diagram is a KTU favorite**
> It is a complete picture: the agent senses the environment, estimates a value function from the policy, and uses the value function to improve the policy. Every KTU Module 2 short question can be answered by pointing to one block in this diagram.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model Question]

**Differentiate between a state value function $V_\pi(s)$ and an action value function $Q_\pi(s,a)$. Mention the role of the discount factor $\gamma$ in defining the value function.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | $V_\pi(s)$ | $Q_\pi(s,a)$ |
|--------|------------|---------------|
| Argument | State $s$ only | State-action pair $(s, a)$ |
| Definition | Expected return starting from $s$ and following $\pi$ | Expected return starting from $s$, taking $a$, then following $\pi$ |
| Granularity | Coarser (averages over actions) | Finer (commits to a specific action first) |
| Relation | $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s)\,Q_\pi(s,a)$ | $Q_\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s'} p(s'\mid s,a)[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')]$ |

The **discount factor $\gamma \in [0,1]$** weights the relative importance of immediate vs. future rewards. With $\gamma = 0$ the agent is myopic, and with $\gamma \to 1$ the agent is far-sighted. For infinite-horizon (continuing) MDPs, $\gamma < 1$ is required to keep the cumulative reward finite and to ensure Bellman operator contraction.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Question]

**State the Bellman Optimality Equation for the optimal action value function $Q^*(s, a)$ and explain the role of the $\max$ operator.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$Q^*(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} p(s' \mid s, a)\,\Big[\,r(s, a, s') + \gamma\, \max_{a' \in \mathcal{A}}\, Q^*(s', a')\,\Big]$$

**Role of the $\max$ operator:** It selects the best possible next action $a'$ that the agent can take from the successor state $s'$. The outer sum averages over the environment's stochasticity (transitions and rewards), while the inner $\max$ captures the agent's *optimal choice* at the next step. The $\max$ replaces the policy-weighted sum $\sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')$ that appears in the *expectation* equation ŌĆö this is precisely the structural transformation that distinguishes *evaluation* (under a given policy) from *control* (finding the optimal policy).

---

## 5.2 PART B Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice Option I

**Question A [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Define the state value function $V_\pi(s)$ and the action value function $Q_\pi(s, a)$ for a stationary policy $\pi$ in a finite Markov Decision Process. Derive the **Bellman Expectation Equation for $V_\pi(s)$** step-by-step, clearly stating where the **Markov property** is used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define the **optimal state value function** $V^*(s)$ and the **optimal action value function** $Q^*(s, a)$. Starting from the definition of $Q^*$, derive the **Bellman Optimality Equation** for $Q^*$. Show, with justification, how an **optimal policy** $\pi^*$ is extracted from $Q^*$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A(a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Definition of $V_\pi(s)$ (1 Mark):**

$$V_\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,G_t \;\middle|\; S_t = s\,\right] \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{k}\,R_{t+k+1} \;\middle|\; S_t = s\,\right]$$

**Definition of $Q_\pi(s, a)$ (1 Mark):**

$$Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,G_t \;\middle|\; S_t = s,\; A_t = a\,\right]$$

**Derivation of the Bellman Expectation Equation for $V_\pi(s)$ (5 Marks):**

**Step 1 (0.5 Mark):** Substitute $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ into the definition:

$$V_\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \;\middle|\; S_t = s\,\right]$$

**Step 2 (0.5 Mark):** Apply linearity of expectation:

$$V_\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\,\right] + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\,\right]$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Condition the first expectation on the action $a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)$ and the next state $s' \sim p(\cdot \mid s, a)$:

$$\mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\,\right] \;=\; \sum_{a}\pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s'}p(s'\mid s,a)\,r(s,a,s')$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Apply the same conditioning to the second expectation, and use the **Markov property** ŌĆö the future $G_{t+1}$ given $S_{t+1} = s'$ is independent of the past $(S_t, A_t)$:

$$\mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\,\right] \;=\; \sum_{a}\pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s'}p(s'\mid s,a)\, \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'\,\right]$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Recognize the inner conditional expectation as $V_\pi(s')$:

$$V_\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a}\pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s'}p(s'\mid s,a)\,\Big[\,r(s,a,s') + \gamma\, V_\pi(s')\,\Big]$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Conclude: the **Markov property** was invoked in Step 4, where the conditional distribution of $G_{t+1}$ given the full history collapses to the conditional given $S_{t+1} = s'$ alone. The result is the **Bellman Expectation Equation** for $V_\pi$.

---

### Model Solution to Question A(b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Definition of $V^*$ and $Q^*$ (2 Marks):**

$$V^*(s) \;=\; \max_{\pi}\, V_\pi(s), \quad Q^*(s, a) \;=\; \max_{\pi}\, Q_\pi(s, a) \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S},\; a \in \mathcal{A}$$

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Start from the definition of $Q^*$:

$$Q^*(s, a) \;=\; \max_{\pi}\, Q_\pi(s, a)$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Substitute the Bellman expectation equation for $Q_\pi$:

$$Q^*(s, a) \;=\; \max_{\pi}\; \sum_{s'}p(s'\mid s,a)\,\Big[\,r(s,a,s') + \gamma \sum_{a'}\pi(a'\mid s')\,Q_\pi(s', a')\,\Big]$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** The $\max_\pi$ over a fixed $\pi$-independent expression acts only on $\sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q_\pi(s', a')$. The optimal choice is to put probability $1$ on the action that maximizes $Q^*(s', a')$. Hence:

$$\max_{\pi} \sum_{a'}\pi(a'\mid s')\,Q_\pi(s', a') \;=\; \max_{a'}\,Q^*(s', a')$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Final form:

$$Q^*(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} p(s' \mid s, a)\,\Big[\,r(s, a, s') + \gamma\, \max_{a' \in \mathcal{A}}\, Q^*(s', a')\,\Big]$$

**Extracting $\pi^*$ (1 Mark):** Once $Q^*$ is known, an optimal deterministic policy is recovered by greedy action selection:

$$\pi^*(s) \;=\; \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}}\, Q^*(s, a)$$

This is justified because $Q^*(s, a)$ is precisely the expected return when the agent acts optimally from $(s, a)$ onward; choosing the $a$ with the highest $Q^*$ ensures the highest possible expected return from $s$, which is the very definition of optimality.

---

## 5.3 PART B Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice Option II

**Question B [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of the **Bellman Expectation Equation for $V_\pi(s)$**, define the **Bellman operator** $T_\pi$ and prove that $T_\pi$ is a $\gamma$-contraction in the **sup norm**. State the consequence of this contraction under the **Banach Fixed-Point Theorem** for solving $V_\pi$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 3-state finite MDP with states $\{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, discount factor $\gamma = 0.5$, and $s_3$ a terminal absorbing state with $V(s_3) = 0$. Under a deterministic policy, the transition model gives: $(s_1, a_1) \to (s_2, r = +2)$ and $(s_2, a_2) \to (s_3, r = +4)$. Compute $V_\pi(s_2)$, $V_\pi(s_1)$, $Q_\pi(s_1, a_1)$, $Q_\pi(s_2, a_2)$, and the **Advantage Function** $A_\pi(s_1, a_1)$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B(a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 Mark) ŌĆö Define the Bellman operator $T_\pi$:**

$$(T_\pi V)(s) \;=\; \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} p(s' \mid s, a)\,\Big[\,r(s, a, s') + \gamma\, V(s')\,\Big]$$

The fixed-point equation $T_\pi V = V$ is precisely the Bellman expectation equation for $V_\pi$.

**Step 2 (2 Marks) ŌĆö Setup the contraction proof:** For any two bounded value functions $V$ and $U$, evaluate $\vert (T_\pi V)(s) - (T_\pi U)(s) \vert$ for an arbitrary $s$:

$$(T_\pi V)(s) - (T_\pi U)(s) \;=\; \sum_{a}\pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s'}p(s'\mid s,a)\,\gamma\,\big[\,V(s') - U(s')\,\big]$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks) ŌĆö Bound the absolute value:** Taking absolute values, using the triangle inequality, and recognizing that the sum $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a)$ equals $1$ (a probability measure):

$$\vert (T_\pi V)(s) - (T_\pi U)(s) \vert \;\leq\; \gamma \sum_{a}\pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s'}p(s'\mid s,a)\,\vert V(s') - U(s') \vert \;\leq\; \gamma\, \vert \vert V - U \vert \vert_\infty$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark) ŌĆö Sup-norm contraction conclusion:** The bound holds for every $s$, so:

$$\vert \vert T_\pi V - T_\pi U \vert \vert_\infty \;\leq\; \gamma\, \vert \vert V - U \vert \vert_\infty$$

Hence $T_\pi$ is a **$\gamma$-contraction** in the sup norm.

**Step 5 (1 Mark) ŌĆö Consequence via Banach:** By the **Banach Fixed-Point Theorem**, a $\gamma$-contraction on a complete metric space has a *unique* fixed point and iterative application $V_{k+1} = T_\pi V_k$ converges to it from any starting $V_0$ at linear rate $O(\gamma^k)$. Therefore, $V_\pi$ is unique and can be computed by **Iterative Policy Evaluation**.

---

### Model Solution to Question B(b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Given:** $\gamma = 0.5$, $V_\pi(s_3) = 0$, transitions $s_1 \xrightarrow{a_1} s_2$ with $r=+2$ and $s_2 \xrightarrow{a_2} s_3$ with $r=+4$.

**Step 1 (1 Mark) ŌĆö Compute $V_\pi(s_2)$:**

$$V_\pi(s_2) \;=\; r(s_2, a_2, s_3) + \gamma\, V_\pi(s_3) \;=\; 4 + 0.5 \cdot 0 \;=\; 4$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark) ŌĆö Compute $V_\pi(s_1)$:**

$$V_\pi(s_1) \;=\; r(s_1, a_1, s_2) + \gamma\, V_\pi(s_2) \;=\; 2 + 0.5 \cdot 4 \;=\; 2 + 2 \;=\; 4$$

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks) ŌĆö Compute $Q_\pi(s_1, a_1)$:**

$$Q_\pi(s_1, a_1) \;=\; r(s_1, a_1, s_2) + \gamma\, V_\pi(s_2) \;=\; 2 + 0.5 \cdot 4 \;=\; 4$$

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks) ŌĆö Compute $Q_\pi(s_2, a_2)$:**

$$Q_\pi(s_2, a_2) \;=\; r(s_2, a_2, s_3) + \gamma\, V_\pi(s_3) \;=\; 4 + 0.5 \cdot 0 \;=\; 4$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark) ŌĆö Compute Advantage:**

$$A_\pi(s_1, a_1) \;=\; Q_\pi(s_1, a_1) - V_\pi(s_1) \;=\; 4 - 4 \;=\; 0$$

**Interpretation (1 Mark):** Advantage $A_\pi(s_1, a_1) = 0$ implies that action $a_1$ is exactly average in state $s_1$ under the current policy ŌĆö the deterministic policy is already optimal for this MDP.

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls on Value Function Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting the Markov property mention** in derivations. Examiners *specifically look* for the line "by the Markov property, $G_{t+1}$ given $S_{t+1}=s'$ is independent of the past." Omitting this typically costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Confusing the two Bellman equations.** Writing a $\max$ inside the *expectation* equation (or a $\sum_a \pi(a\mid s)$ inside the *optimality* equation) is the most common 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping the conversion identities.** $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) Q_\pi(s, a)$ and $Q_\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a)[\dots]$ are *separate identities* from the Bellman equations; mixing them up costs marks in numerical sub-parts.
> 4. **Using $\gamma = 1$ in infinite-horizon problems.** Always explicitly state the assumption: for episodic MDPs, $\gamma = 1$ is allowed; for continuing MDPs, $\gamma < 1$ is *required* for finiteness of $V_\pi$.
> 5. **Sign errors in numerical advantage computation.** $A_\pi = Q_\pi - V_\pi$ (not $V_\pi - Q_\pi$). Reversing the sign is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Value functions are expectations of cumulative discounted reward.** $V_\pi(s)$ depends only on $s$ and $\pi$; $Q_\pi(s, a)$ depends on $s$, $a$, and $\pi$.
- **The discount factor $\gamma \in [0,1)$** weights future rewards and guarantees finite returns in continuing tasks.
- **The Bellman Expectation Equation** is the *recursive* form of $V_\pi$ (or $Q_\pi$) under a *fixed* policy $\pi$. It is a *linear* recursion in $V_\pi$ when $\pi$ and $P$ are fixed.
- **The Bellman Optimality Equation** is the corresponding recursion for $V^*$ (or $Q^*$); the *expectation over actions* is replaced by a $\max$ over actions.
- **Conversion identities** between $V_\pi$ and $Q_\pi$:
  - $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s)\, Q_\pi(s, a)$
  - $Q_\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a)[\,r + \gamma V_\pi(s')\,]$
- **The Bellman operator $T_\pi$ is a $\gamma$-contraction** in sup norm, guaranteeing a *unique* fixed point $V_\pi$ via the **Banach Fixed-Point Theorem**, and a constructive iterative algorithm via $V_{k+1} = T_\pi V_k$.
- **An optimal policy is extracted greedily from $Q^*$** by $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s, a)$.
- **The Advantage Function** $A_\pi(s, a) = Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s)$ is central to Actor-Critic methods and variance reduction.
- **Markov property** is *silently* invoked in every Bellman derivation ŌĆö always state it explicitly to earn full marks.
- **Always distinguish the operator over actions:** $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s)$ for expectation, $\max_a$ for optimality, $\arg\max_a$ for policy extraction.
- **Existence and uniqueness of $V_\pi$** requires: finite $\mathcal{S}$ and $\mathcal{A}$, bounded rewards, and $\gamma < 1$ (for continuing tasks).

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Mantra (Module 2)**
> "Sum over actions with $\pi$ for **evaluation**; **max** over actions for **optimality**; **argmax** over actions for **policy extraction**; **Markov property** must always be named; **$\gamma$-contraction** guarantees uniqueness of $V_\pi$."
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bellman Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT868 - INTRODUCTION TO REINFORCEMENT LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Bellman Equations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bellman Equation (Sutton & Barto Formalism):** A set of recursive equations fundamental to **Markov Decision Processes (MDPs)** that express the *value* of a state (or state-action pair) in terms of the *immediate reward* and the *discounted value of the next state* (or state-action pair), under a given policy $\pi$. The Bellman Equation is essentially the **mathematical heart of Reinforcement Learning**. It decomposes a long-horizon, sequential decision-making problem into a *one-step lookahead* recursive relationship, transforming an infinite-horizon sum of rewards into a tractable closed-form (or solvable) system of linear/non-linear equations. The four canonical forms of the Bellman Equation in the KTU 2024 syllabus are: 1. **State-Value Bellman Expectation Equation** ŌĆö $V^{\pi}(s)$ 2. **Action-Value Bellman Expectation Equation** ŌĆö $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ 3. **State-Value Bellman Optimality Equation** ŌĆö $V^{*}(s)$ 4. **Action-Value Bellman Optimality Equation** ŌĆö $Q^{*}(s,a)$ > [!NOTE] > **Where the name comes from:** Named after **Richard E. Bellman** (1957), the founder of *Dynamic Programming*. He proposed the *Principle of Optimality*: *"An optimal policy has the property that whatever the initial state and initial decision are, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: "The Long Road Trip GPS" Imagine you are planning a multi-day road trip from **Kochi to Delhi**. The total trip value (estimated time of arrival) is not just the next hour's drive ŌĆö it depends on the entire remaining journey. | Layer | RL Analogy | Road Trip Equivalent | |---|---|---| | **State** $s$ | Current city of the car | Kochi | | **Action** $a$ | Choosing a highway / turn | Take NH44 | | **Reward** $r$ | Time spent / fuel used | Drive time for that segment | | **Return** $G_t$ | Total remaining trip time | ETA in Delhi | | **Bellman Eq.** | $V = r + \gamma \times \text{rest}$ | "Hours from here to next major city **plus** 90% of the remaining hours from there" | > [!TIP] > **Intuitive takeaway:** Just as a GPS decomposes total journey time into *"time for the next segment + discounted remaining time,"* the Bellman Equation decomposes a long-term reward into *"immediate reward + discounted future value."* The discount factor $\gamma$ acts like the *'importance weight'* you place on tomorrow's cost vs. today's cost. ## 1.3 Standard Constants & KTU Metric Conventions - **Discount factor:** $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ (commonly **0.9** or **0.99** in KTU numericals). - **Policy:** $\pi(a \mid s) \in [0, 1]$ ŌĆö probability of choosing action $a$ in state $s$. - **Transition probability:** $p(s', r \mid s, a) \in [0, 1]$. - **Reward:** $r \in \mathbb{R}$ (real number, can be negative). - **Terminal states** (absorbing): $V(s_{terminal}) = 0$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Decomposing the Logic: The 4 Layers of the Bellman Equation The Bellman Equation is built on **4 fundamental building blocks**. Every KTU 2024 problem on this topic will test at least one of these. **Layer 1 ŌĆö The Return (what we are valuing).** The discounted return starting from time $t$ is: $$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$ **Layer 2 ŌĆö The Value Function (expected return).** - State-value under policy $\pi$: $$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$$ - Action-value under policy $\pi$: $$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$ **Layer 3 ŌĆö The Recursion (applying the Markov Property + Law of Iterated Expectations).** This is where Bellman's genius lies: by conditioning on the *next* state $s'$, the infinite sum collapses into a *one-step* equation. **Layer 4 ŌĆö The Optimisation (for $V^*$ and $Q^*$).** Replace the policy-weighted expectation with a $\max$ operator over actions. ## 2.2 The "WHY" Behind Each Step | Step | Why we do it | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---| | Take expectation over $p(s', r \mid s, a)$ | The environment is **stochastic**; we average over possible next states and rewards. | Handles noisy sensors, probabilistic faults. | | Take expectation over $\pi(a \mid s)$ | The policy may be **stochastic** (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy). | Supports exploration in unknown environments. | | Multiply by $\gamma$ | Future rewards are **less valuable** than immediate ones (financial discounting, decay). | Solves the infinite-horizon convergence problem. | | $\max_a$ for $V^*$ | The *optimal* agent picks the **best** action, not the average one. | Finds globally optimal control policies in robotics. | > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in engineering:** The Bellman Equation is the backbone of **optimal control** (e.g., the Apollo guidance computer, autonomous vehicles, drone trajectory planning, stock-trading bots, and even HVAC temperature control in smart buildings). Any time you solve *"what is the best long-term decision given uncertainty about the future,"* you are using a Bellman Equation. ## 2.3 KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet (MUST MEMORISE) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Table 2.1 ŌĆö Master Formula Sheet for KTU Module 2 (Bellman Equations)** | # | Name | Formula | Used For | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Defining what we maximise | | 2 | State Value | $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$ | How good is this state under $\pi$? | | 3 | Action Value | $Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$ | How good is this action here? | | 4 | **Bellman Expectation (V)** | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a \mid s)\sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\left[ r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \right]$ | Evaluating a fixed policy | | 5 | **Bellman Expectation (Q)** | $Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a)\left[ r + \gamma \sum_{a'}\pi(a' \mid s') Q^{\pi}(s', a') \right]$ | Evaluating action values under $\pi$ | | 6 | **Bellman Optimality (V\*)** | $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\left[ r + \gamma V^{*}(s') \right]$ | Computing optimal state value | | 7 | **Bellman Optimality (Q\*)** | $Q^{*}(s, a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a') \right]$ | Computing optimal action value | | 8 | V-Q relationship | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a \mid s)\,Q^{\pi}(s, a)$ | Bridge between $V$ and $Q$ | | 9 | V*-Q* relationship | $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} Q^{*}(s, a)$ | Bridge at the optimum | | 10 | Terminal state | $V(s_{terminal}) = 0,\ Q(s_{terminal}, \cdot) = 0$ | Boundary condition | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Pitfall:** In KTU answer sheets, the inner absolute-value / pipe notation is best written as $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid ambiguity with conditional probability. For example, $\pi(a \mid s)$ is the **correct** notation, **not** $\pi(a \vert s)$ in formal notation ŌĆö though $\vert$ is accepted. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Bellman Expectation Equation for $V^{\pi}(s)$ We start from the *definition* of the state-value function and apply the **Law of Iterated Expectations**, conditioning on the *first* action and the *resulting* next state. **Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the definition:** $$V^{\pi}(s) \;\triangleq\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Split the return into the immediate reward and the discounted remainder:** $$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$ Therefore: $$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Use linearity of expectation:** $$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right] + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the **Markov Property** (future depends only on $S_{t+1}$, not the history).** This means we can drop everything except the conditioning on the next state: $$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right] + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \right] \quad \text{(conditioned on } s' \text{)}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Now apply the **Law of Iterated Expectations**: first condition on the action $a$ chosen by $\pi$, then on the environment's next state and reward $(s', r)$:** $$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a \mid s)\,\sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\left[\, r + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \right] \,\right]$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Recognise the inner expectation as $V^{\pi}(s')$** by definition: $$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a \mid s)\,\sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\left[\, r + \gamma\, V^{\pi}(s') \,\right] \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Bellman Expectation Equation for $Q^{\pi}(s, a)$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the definition:** $$Q^{\pi}(s, a) \;\triangleq\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Split the return:** $$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Use linearity of expectation and the Markov Property:** $$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right] + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \right]$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Condition on the next state and reward via the dynamics $p(s', r \mid s, a)$:** $$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\left[\, r + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \right] \,\right]$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Now the inner expectation is taken over the *next* action $a' \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s')$, giving us $Q^{\pi}(s', a')$:** $$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\left[\, r + \gamma\, \sum_{a'}\pi(a' \mid s')\, Q^{\pi}(s', a') \,\right] \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^{*}(s)$ The derivation mirrors Derivation 1, but instead of averaging over $\pi$, the *optimal* agent picks the action that **maximises** future returns: **Step 1 ŌĆö Start from the definition of the optimal value:** $$V^{*}(s) \;\triangleq\; \max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s)$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö At the optimum, the policy deterministically selects the best action. The expectation over $\pi$ becomes a $\max$ over $a$:** $$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \mathbb{E}\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \,\right]$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Expand over the environment dynamics $p(s', r \mid s, a)$ and apply the Markov Property to recognise $V^{*}(s')$:** $$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\left[\, r + \gamma\, V^{*}(s') \,\right] \quad \blacksquare$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key structural difference (KTU-favourite question!):** > - **Expectation equation** ŌĆö has $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s)$ (linear in $V$, $\Rightarrow$ **linear system**). > - **Optimality equation** ŌĆö has $\max_a$ (non-linear in $V$, $\Rightarrow$ **non-linear system**, harder to solve). > This is why *Value Iteration* (which solves the optimality equation) uses a fixed-point iteration while *Policy Evaluation* (which solves the expectation equation) can use linear algebra. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Style Problem) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** Consider a 3-state MDP with states $s_1, s_2, s_3$ (where $s_3$ is a *terminal absorbing state* with $V(s_3) = 0$). The agent has a **deterministic policy** that always takes action $a$ in $s_1$ (going to $s_2$ with reward $+1$) and action $b$ in $s_2$ (going to $s_3$ with reward $+2$). Let $\gamma = 0.5$. Using the **Bellman Expectation Equation**, compute $V^{\pi}(s_1)$. **Given data:** | From State | Action | To State | Reward | |---|---|---|---| | $s_1$ | $a$ | $s_2$ | $+1$ | | $s_2$ | $b$ | $s_3$ | $+2$ | **Step 1 ŌĆö Write the boundary condition.** Since $s_3$ is terminal: $$V^{\pi}(s_3) = 0$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Bellman Equation at $s_2$.** The policy is deterministic, so $\pi(b \mid s_2) = 1$: $$V^{\pi}(s_2) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s_2, b)\left[\, r + \gamma\, V^{\pi}(s') \,\right]$$ Substituting the data: the only possible outcome is $s' = s_3, r = +2$: $$V^{\pi}(s_2) = 1 \cdot \left[\, 2 + 0.5 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_3) \,\right]$$ $$V^{\pi}(s_2) = 2 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 2$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the Bellman Equation at $s_1$:** $$V^{\pi}(s_1) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s_1, a)\left[\, r + \gamma\, V^{\pi}(s') \,\right]$$ Substituting: the only outcome is $s' = s_2, r = +1$: $$V^{\pi}(s_1) = 1 \cdot \left[\, 1 + 0.5 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_2) \,\right]$$ $$V^{\pi}(s_1) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 2$$ $$V^{\pi}(s_1) = 1 + 1 = 2$$ **Final Answer:** $V^{\pi}(s_1) = \mathbf{2},\ V^{\pi}(s_2) = \mathbf{2},\ V^{\pi}(s_3) = \mathbf{0}$. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the 4 Bellman Variants > [!IMPORTANT] > **Table 3.1 ŌĆö Side-by-Side Comparison (KTU Exam Favourite)** | Property | Expectation (V) | Expectation (Q) | Optimality (V\*) | Optimality (Q\*) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Operator over actions | $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s)$ | None (action is fixed) | $\max_a$ | $\max_{a'}$ inside | | Inner variable | $V^{\pi}(s')$ | $Q^{\pi}(s', a')$ | $V^{*}(s')$ | $Q^{*}(s', a')$ | | Linearity in value | **Linear** in $V$ | **Linear** in $Q$ | **Non-linear** (max) | **Non-linear** (max) | | What it solves | *Policy Evaluation* | *Q for given $\pi$* | *Value Iteration* | *Q-Value Iteration* | | Use case | Evaluate a known policy | Evaluate action quality | Find the **best** policy | Find the **best** action | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 ŌĆö Backup Diagram for $V^{\pi}(s)$ (Bellman Expectation) ```mermaid flowchart TD s1["STATE s_t
Value V pi of s_t"] -->|"Action a_1 (prob pi a_1 given s_t)"| env1["ENVIRONMENT DYNAMICS p s prime r given s_t a_1"] s1 -->|"Action a_2 (prob pi a_2 given s_t)"| env2["ENVIRONMENT DYNAMICS p s prime r given s_t a_2"] s1 -->|"Action a_n (prob pi a_n given s_t)"| env3["ENVIRONMENT DYNAMICS p s prime r given s_t a_n"] env1 --> r1["REWARD r sub 1 + gamma times V pi of s prime 1"] env2 --> r2["REWARD r sub 2 + gamma times V pi of s prime 2"] env3 --> r3["REWARD r sub 3 + gamma times V pi of s prime n"] r1 --> sum["EXPECTED VALUE SUM OVER ACTIONS AND STATES"] r2 --> sum r3 --> sum sum --> out["OUTPUT V pi of s_t"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram (for KTU answer sheets):** The *root* node is the state $s$ whose value we want. We *branch out* over all possible actions (weighted by the policy), and for each action we branch over all possible next states and rewards. The *leaves* of the tree are the immediate rewards plus the discounted next-state values. The Bellman Equation says: $V^{\pi}(s)$ = weighted average of all leaves. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 ŌĆö Backup Diagram for $Q^{\pi}(s, a)$ (Bellman Expectation on Q) ```mermaid flowchart LR qnode["Q pi of s_t and a_t"] -->|"ENVIRONMENT p s prime r given s a"| nextS1["NEXT STATE s prime 1 with reward r_1"] qnode -->|"ENVIRONMENT p s prime r given s a"| nextS2["NEXT STATE s prime 2 with reward r_2"] nextS1 -->|"Policy pi a prime 1 given s prime 1"| qBranch1["Q pi of s prime 1 and a prime 1"] nextS1 -->|"Policy pi a prime 2 given s prime 1"| qBranch2["Q pi of s prime 1 and a prime 2"] nextS2 -->|"Policy pi a prime 1 given s prime 2"| qBranch3["Q pi of s prime 2 and a prime 1"] nextS2 -->|"Policy pi a prime 2 given s prime 2"| qBranch4["Q pi of s prime 2 and a prime 2"] qBranch1 --> sumQ["SUM r + gamma times weighted Q values"] qBranch2 --> sumQ qBranch3 --> sumQ qBranch4 --> sumQ sumQ --> outQ["OUTPUT Q pi of s_t a_t"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 ŌĆö Bellman Optimality Backup for $V^{*}(s)$ (One-Step Lookahead Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD vStar["V STAR OF s_t OPTIMAL STATE VALUE"] -->|"MAX OVER ACTIONS a_1 a_2 ... a_n"| a1["ACTION a_1"] vStar --> a2["ACTION a_2"] vStar --> aN["ACTION a_n"] a1 --> e1["ENV p s prime r given s a_1"] a2 --> e2["ENV p s prime r given s a_2"] aN --> eN["ENV p s prime r given s a_n"] e1 --> l1["LEAF r + gamma times V STAR OF s prime"] e2 --> l2["LEAF r + gamma times V STAR OF s prime"] eN --> lN["LEAF r + gamma times V STAR OF s prime"] l1 --> pick["SELECT THE BRANCH WITH HIGHEST EXPECTED VALUE"] l2 --> pick lN --> pick pick --> outV["OUTPUT V STAR OF s_t"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key visual difference:** In the *expectation* backup, the agent's policy is fixed ŌĆö we take a weighted average. In the *optimality* backup, we replace the weighted average with a **maximum** selection. This is the mathematical signature of the difference between *Policy Evaluation* and *Value Iteration* in RL. ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture of Solving Bellman Equations ```mermaid flowchart TD inputA["INPUT MDP TRANSITION p OF s prime r given s a"] --> pE["POLICY EVALUATION BLOCK V pi OF s EQUALS SUM r plus gamma V pi OF s prime"] inputB["INPUT REWARD r"] --> pE inputC["INPUT POLICY pi OF a given s"] --> pE pE --> pEout["LINEAR SYSTEM OF EQUATIONS SOLVE FOR V pi"] pEout --> choice{"TYPE OF BELLMAN EQUATION"} choice -->|"EXPECTATION"| peSolve["GAUSS JORDAN OR ITERATIVE METHOD"] choice -->|"OPTIMALITY VI"| vi["VALUE ITERATION BELLMAN UPDATE V k plus 1 EQUALS MAX"] peSolve --> finalA["OUTPUT V pi OF s FOR FIXED POLICY"] vi --> finalB["OUTPUT V STAR AND PI STAR OPTIMAL"] ``` > [!TIP] > **How to use this in your KTU exam answer:** When asked *"Explain how Bellman Equations are solved in practice,"* you can draw this block diagram and label each block. It demonstrates understanding beyond rote memorisation ŌĆö exactly what KTU 2024 Scheme's *Outcome-Based Education* rubric rewards. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State the Bellman Optimality Equation for the state-value function $V^{*}(s)$ and explain the meaning of every term in the equation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > The Bellman Optimality Equation for the optimal state-value function is: > > $$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\left[\, r + \gamma\, V^{*}(s') \,\right]$$ > > **Term-by-term meaning:** > > - $V^{*}(s)$: The maximum expected discounted return achievable starting from state $s$ and following the *optimal* policy thereafter. > - $\max_{a}$: The optimal agent selects the action that yields the highest expected return. > - $\sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)$: Weighted sum over all possible next states $s'$ and rewards $r$ under the environment dynamics. > - $r + \gamma V^{*}(s')$: The immediate reward plus the discounted value of the optimal future from the next state. **Mark Split:** 1.5 marks for the equation, 1.5 marks for the term-by-term explanation. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between the Bellman Expectation Equation and the Bellman Optimality Equation. Why is the latter non-linear?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Bellman Expectation | Bellman Optimality | |---|---|---| | Action operator | $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s)$ (weighted sum) | $\max_a$ (best action) | | Linearity | **Linear** in $V^{\pi}$ | **Non-linear** in $V^{*}$ | | Use | Evaluate a *given* policy | Find the *optimal* policy | > **Why non-linear?** The $\max$ operator is a *piecewise-linear* non-smooth function. When we have $V^{*}$ appearing inside the $\max$ and also outside via the recursion, the resulting system of equations is non-linear and cannot be solved by simple linear algebra ŌĆö it requires *Value Iteration* or *Policy Iteration* methods. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **a)** Derive the Bellman Expectation Equation for the state-value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ starting from its definition. State clearly the two mathematical properties (tools) you used. **(7 marks)** **b)** Consider the deterministic 3-state MDP given in Section 3.4 of these notes. Now suppose the **policy is changed** to be stochastic: in $s_1$, $\pi(a \mid s_1) = 0.5$ and the alternate action $a'$ goes back to $s_1$ with reward $0$; in $s_2$, the policy remains the same. With $\gamma = 0.5$, recompute $V^{\pi}(s_1)$ and $V^{\pi}(s_2)$. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q-A(a) (7 marks)** **Step 1 ŌĆö State the definition (1 mark):** $$V^{\pi}(s) \;\triangleq\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Split the return into two parts (1 mark):** $$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute and apply linearity of expectation (1 mark):** $$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right] + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the **Markov Property** to make the second expectation depend only on $S_{t+1}$ (1.5 marks):** The Markov Property states that the future depends only on the present, not the history. So: $$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right] = \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s)\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \right]$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the **Law of Iterated Expectations**, first conditioning on the action $a$, then on $(s', r)$ (1.5 marks):** $$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a \mid s)\,\sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\left[\, r + \gamma\, V^{\pi}(s') \,\right] \quad \blacksquare$$ **Tools used (1 mark):** 1. **Markov Property** 2. **Law of Iterated Expectations** --- #### **Model Solution to Q-A(b) (7 marks)** **New MDP data (deterministic outcomes for each action):** | From State | Action | Prob $\pi$ | To State | Reward | |---|---|---|---|---| | $s_1$ | $a$ | 0.5 | $s_2$ | $+1$ | | $s_1$ | $a'$ | 0.5 | $s_1$ | $0$ | | $s_2$ | $b$ | 1.0 | $s_3$ | $+2$ | Boundary: $V(s_3) = 0$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Write the Bellman equation at $s_2$ (1 mark):** $$V^{\pi}(s_2) = 1 \cdot \left[\, 2 + 0.5 \cdot 0 \,\right] = 2$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Write the Bellman equation at $s_1$ (2 marks):** $$V^{\pi}(s_1) = \pi(a \mid s_1)\left[\, 1 + 0.5 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_2) \,\right] + \pi(a' \mid s_1)\left[\, 0 + 0.5 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_1) \,\right]$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the policy probabilities (1 mark):** $$V^{\pi}(s_1) = 0.5 \left[\, 1 + 0.5 \cdot 2 \,\right] + 0.5 \left[\, 0.5 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_1) \,\right]$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Simplify (1 mark):** $$V^{\pi}(s_1) = 0.5 \cdot 2 + 0.25 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_1)$$ $$V^{\pi}(s_1) = 1 + 0.25 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_1)$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Solve the linear equation (2 marks):** $$V^{\pi}(s_1) - 0.25 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_1) = 1$$ $$0.75 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_1) = 1$$ $$V^{\pi}(s_1) = \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.333$$ **Final Answer:** $V^{\pi}(s_1) = \mathbf{4/3 \approx 1.333},\ V^{\pi}(s_2) = \mathbf{2}$. > [!NOTE] > **Observation for examiners:** Compared to the deterministic case ($V^{\pi}(s_1) = 2$), the stochastic policy *reduces* the value of $s_1$ because there is a 50% chance of receiving 0 reward and looping. This is a classic KTU exam trick: *"stochastic policies can be worse than deterministic ones."* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **a)** Derive the Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^{*}(s, a)$ starting from its definition. Clearly state the role of the $\max$ operator. **(7 marks)** **b)** Consider a 2-armed bandit MDP with states $\{A, B\}$ (both non-terminal). The agent can take action $L$ (left) or $R$ (right). Transitions: from $A$, action $L$ returns to $A$ with reward $0$ (prob 0.5) or goes to $B$ with reward $+5$ (prob 0.5); from $A$, action $R$ deterministically goes to $B$ with reward $+1$. From $B$, both actions deterministically go to $A$ with reward $0$. Take $\gamma = 0.9$. Use the **Bellman Optimality Equation** to compute $V^{*}(A)$. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q-B(a) (7 marks)** **Step 1 ŌĆö Start from the definition of the optimal action-value (1 mark):** $$Q^{*}(s, a) \;\triangleq\; \max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Split the return (1 mark):** $$Q^{*}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Condition on the next state and reward (1.5 marks):** $$Q^{*}(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\left[\, r + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}\left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \right] \,\right]$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö At the optimum, the future return is $V^{*}(s') = \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')$ (1.5 marks):** $$Q^{*}(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\left[\, r + \gamma\, \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a') \,\right] \quad \blacksquare$$ **Role of the $\max$ operator (2 marks):** The $\max_{a'}$ is taken over **future actions** at the next state. It captures the *Principle of Optimality* (Bellman, 1957): once the agent has taken action $a$ in state $s$ and reached $s'$, the remainder of the trajectory must also be optimal. Thus the value of the future is the *best* possible value, not the average. --- #### **Model Solution to Q-B(b) (7 marks)** **Step 1 ŌĆö Write the Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^{*}(A)$ (1 mark):** $$V^{*}(A) = \max_{a \in \{L, R\}} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid A, a)\left[\, r + \gamma\, V^{*}(s') \,\right]$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the right-hand side for action $L$ in state $A$ (2 marks):** For action $L$ in $A$: - Probability 0.5: $s' = A$, $r = 0$ - Probability 0.5: $s' = B$, $r = +5$ $$\text{value}_L = 0.5 \left[\, 0 + 0.9 \cdot V^{*}(A) \,\right] + 0.5 \left[\, 5 + 0.9 \cdot V^{*}(B) \,\right]$$ $$\text{value}_L = 0.45\, V^{*}(A) + 0.45\, V^{*}(B) + 2.5$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the right-hand side for action $R$ in state $A$ (1 mark):** For action $R$ in $A$: deterministic transition to $B$ with reward $+1$: $$\text{value}_R = 1 \cdot \left[\, 1 + 0.9 \cdot V^{*}(B) \,\right] = 1 + 0.9\, V^{*}(B)$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Express $V^{*}(B)$ via the Bellman Optimality Equation (1 mark):** From state $B$, both actions give the same outcome (go to $A$ with reward $0$): $$V^{*}(B) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot V^{*}(A) = 0.9\, V^{*}(A)$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Compare the two actions (which is best?) and set up the system (1 mark):** Substitute $V^{*}(B) = 0.9\, V^{*}(A)$ into both candidates: $$\text{value}_L = 0.45\, V^{*}(A) + 0.45 \cdot 0.9\, V^{*}(A) + 2.5 = 0.45\, V^{*}(A) + 0.405\, V^{*}(A) + 2.5 = 0.855\, V^{*}(A) + 2.5$$ $$\text{value}_R = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 0.9\, V^{*}(A) = 1 + 0.81\, V^{*}(A)$$ Comparing: at $V^{*}(A) = 0$, value_L = 2.5 > value_R = 1. The two will be equal at: $$0.855\, V^{*}(A) + 2.5 = 1 + 0.81\, V^{*}(A) \;\Rightarrow\; 0.045\, V^{*}(A) = -1.5 \;\Rightarrow\; V^{*}(A) = -33.3$$ Since the *real* $V^{*}(A)$ is positive, **action $L$ dominates** at the optimum: $$V^{*}(A) = 0.855\, V^{*}(A) + 2.5$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Solve for $V^{*}(A)$ (1 mark):** $$V^{*}(A) - 0.855\, V^{*}(A) = 2.5$$ $$0.145\, V^{*}(A) = 2.5$$ $$V^{*}(A) = \frac{2.5}{0.145} = \frac{2500}{145} = \frac{500}{29} \approx 17.24$$ **Final Answer:** $V^{*}(A) \approx \mathbf{17.24}$. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 ways KTU students LOSE marks on Bellman Equation questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the boundary condition:** $V(s_{terminal}) = 0$ ŌĆö if you don't set this, every subsequent value is wrong. **ŌłÆ1 mark minimum.** > 2. **Confusing $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s)$ with $\max_a$:** The expectation equation averages over the policy; the optimality equation takes the maximum. Swapping them = **ŌłÆ2 marks** (conceptual error). > 3. **Forgetting to apply the Markov Property in derivations:** Examiners look for this in the derivation steps. **ŌłÆ1 mark.** > 4. **Not justifying the $\max$ operator in optimality:** Always state *"the optimal agent picks the best action."* **ŌłÆ1 mark.** > 5. **Arithmetic slips in numericals:** The discount factor $\gamma$ must multiply the *next-state* value, **not** the current-state value. **ŌłÆ1 to 2 marks** depending on cascade. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (last-minute exam prep):** > > - The Bellman Equation expresses value *recursively* in terms of immediate reward + discounted next value. > - **Two flavours:** *Expectation* (linear, evaluates a given $\pi$) and *Optimality* (non-linear, finds the best $\pi$). > - **Two flavours of value:** *State value* $V^{\pi}(s)$ and *action value* $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$. They are linked by $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) Q^{\pi}(s, a)$. > - **Key derivation tools used:** *Markov Property* + *Law of Iterated Expectations*. > - **The Principle of Optimality** (Bellman, 1957) is the philosophical backbone: an optimal policy's tail is also optimal. > - **Boundary condition:** Terminal / absorbing states have $V = 0$. > - **Discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1]$** ensures the infinite sum of rewards converges. > - **Expectation ŌåÆ linear system ŌåÆ solve by matrix inversion or iterative policy evaluation.** > - **Optimality ŌåÆ non-linear ŌåÆ solve by Value Iteration (Bellman update: $V_{k+1}(s) = \max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) [r + \gamma V_k(s')]$).** > - In KTU exams, the **stochastic-policy extension** of the Bellman Equation is a common 7-mark sub-question. > - **Memorise all 10 formulas in Table 2.1** ŌĆö they cover 90% of the marks. > - For numerical problems, always **state the boundary condition first**, then solve backwards from terminal states. > - The four canonical Bellman equations (V, Q, V*, Q*) together form the **mathematical foundation of all of Reinforcement Learning** ŌĆö every algorithm (Value Iteration, Policy Iteration, Q-Learning, SARSA) is a *method to solve* one of these four equations.

topics.title: Policy Evaluation and Improvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT868 - INTRODUCTION TO REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Policy Evaluation and Improvement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Policy Evaluation (The Prediction Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Policy Evaluation** is the process of computing the state-value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ for a **given** (fixed) policy $\pi$. It answers the question: *"How good is this policy?"* ŌĆö i.e., what is the expected long-term return if the agent starts in state $s$ and follows policy $\pi$ thereafter?

Mathematically, the value of a state $s$ under policy $\pi$ is defined as:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \doteq \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\,G_t \mid S_t = s\,\right] = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\,\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, S_t = s\,\right]$$

where $G_t$ is the return (cumulative discounted reward), and **$\gamma \in [0,1]$** is the discount factor.

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"Prediction"** is used because we are predicting the future expected return ŌĆö we are not yet changing the policy. This is sometimes called the **Prediction Problem** in classical DP literature (Sutton & Barto).

### Conceptual Analogy: Policy Evaluation as a "Performance Review"

Imagine a chess coach analyzing a student's playing style (the **policy**). The coach looks at every possible board position (the **state**) and asks:
- *"If you are in this position, and you keep playing in your typical way, what's the average outcome ŌĆö win, draw, or loss?"*

The coach writes a **score** (the value $V^{\pi}(s)$) for every board position. This score is exactly the **Policy Evaluation**. The policy itself is not changed during this process ŌĆö the coach is just *measuring* how good the existing strategy is.

## 1.2 Policy Improvement (The Control Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Policy Improvement** is the process of using the value function $V^{\pi}$ to construct a new, strictly better (or equally good) policy $\pi'$, denoted as $\pi' \geq \pi$. It answers the question: *"Can we tweak the policy to do better?"*

The core idea is **greedy action selection**: for each state, pick the action $a$ that maximizes the expected return when you take $a$ in state $s$ and *then* follow $\pi$ thereafter. This is captured by the action-value function:

$$Q^{\pi}(s,a) \doteq \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\,R_{t+1} + \gamma V^{\pi}(S_{t+1}) \,\big|\, S_t = s, A_t = a\,\right]$$

The improved (greedy) policy is then:

$$\pi'(s) \doteq \arg\max_{a} Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \arg\max_{a}\, \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,\big[\,r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\,\big]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Improvement" and not just "Change"?** The celebrated **Policy Improvement Theorem** guarantees that the new greedy policy $\pi'$ is at least as good as $\pi$ for *every* state. This monotonic improvement is the engine of DP-based control.

## 1.3 Policy Iteration ŌĆö Putting It All Together

**Policy Iteration** is the closed loop of alternating *evaluation* and *improvement* until the policy stabilizes (no more changes). Each iteration produces a strictly better policy, and convergence to the optimal policy $\pi^*$ is guaranteed in a finite MDP.

> [!TIP]
> The two-step loop **Evaluate ŌåÆ Improve ŌåÆ Evaluate ŌåÆ Improve ŌåÆ ...** is the conceptual heart of classical Dynamic Programming (DP) methods in RL. Whenever you see "iterative" RL algorithms, this alternating structure is almost always lurking underneath.

---

### Real-World Engineering Significance

- **Robotics**: Evaluating a locomotion gait (policy) by computing expected energy efficiency.
- **Autonomous Driving**: Scoring the safety of a lane-change policy before deployment.
- **Industrial Control**: Estimating the long-term cost of a manufacturing schedule.
- **Game AI (Chess/Go)**: Computing position evaluations to refine move-selection rules.
- **Finance / Resource Allocation**: Estimating expected portfolio returns under a fixed trading rule.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bellman Equation for a Fixed Policy

The defining property of $V^{\pi}$ is that it satisfies the **Bellman Equation for $V^{\pi}$**:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\Big[\,r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\,\Big]$$

This is a system of $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$ linear equations (one per state). If the dynamics $p(s',r \mid s,a)$ are known, this can be solved directly ŌĆö but at $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert^{3})$ cost. For large state spaces we use **iterative methods**.

## 2.2 Iterative Policy Evaluation Algorithm

Starting from an arbitrary $V_0$ (terminal states $\equiv 0$), repeatedly apply the **Bellman update** for all non-terminal states:

$$V_{k+1}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\Big[\,r + \gamma V_{k}(s')\,\Big]$$

As $k \to \infty$, $V_k \to V^{\pi}$ under the conditions:
- $\gamma \in [0,1)$ (or episodic tasks with terminal absorbing states)
- All states are visited infinitely often

> [!NOTE]
> This is essentially solving a linear system by **iterative approximation** ŌĆö like Jacobi iteration for solving $Ax = b$. Each "sweep" updates every state using the values from the previous sweep.

### Two Update Variants (KTU often asks this distinction!)

| Update Mode | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **In-Place** | Overwrite $V(s)$ immediately within the same sweep | Faster convergence in practice |
| **Two-Array** | Store $V_{k+1}$ in a new array, keeping $V_k$ unchanged | Cleaner analysis, theoretical clarity |

## 2.3 Policy Improvement Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Policy Improvement).** Let $\pi$ and $\pi'$ be two deterministic policies such that for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$:
> $$Q^{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$$
> Then $\pi'$ must be at least as good as $\pi$: $\;V^{\pi'}(s) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$ for all $s$.

**Proof sketch (logical sequence):**

1. From hypothesis: $V^{\pi}(s) \leq Q^{\pi}(s, \pi'(s))$
2. Expand: $Q^{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\!\big[R_{t+1} + \gamma V^{\pi}(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s\big]$
3. Apply the inequality recursively: $V^{\pi}(s) \leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\!\big[R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} V^{\pi}(S_{t+2}) \mid S_t = s\big]$
4. Continuing until horizon: $V^{\pi}(s) \leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\!\big[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \mid S_t = s\big] = V^{\pi'}(s)$ $\;\;\blacksquare$

If the inequality is strict in *any* state, the improvement is strict there too.

## 2.4 Policy Iteration Algorithm

The complete loop:

1. **Initialize**: arbitrary $V(s) \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\pi(s) \in \mathcal{A}(s)$ for all $s$.
2. **Policy Evaluation**: loop until convergence:
   $$V(s) \leftarrow \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\big[r + \gamma V(s')\big]$$
3. **Policy Improvement**: for each $s$:
   $$\pi(s) \leftarrow \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\big[r + \gamma V(s')\big]$$
4. If $\pi$ changed in step 3, go to step 2; else **STOP** ŌĆö $\pi$ is optimal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence guarantee:** For a finite MDP ($\vert \mathcal{S} \vert, \vert \mathcal{A} \vert < \infty$), policy iteration converges in a **finite** number of iterations (at most $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert}$ in the worst case, but typically far fewer).

## 2.5 Value Iteration ŌĆö A Truncated Variant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Value Iteration** is policy iteration where policy evaluation is truncated to a **single sweep** per iteration. The update rule combines evaluation and improvement:
> $$V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\big[r + \gamma V_{k}(s')\big]$$
> This is the **Bellman Optimality Equation** turned into an iterative update.

Value iteration converges to $V^*$ in $\mathcal{O}(\gamma)$ ŌĆö eventually, but the policy is implicit (extract via greedy selection at deployment time).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the boxed relationships. The "= vs max" distinction is the single most-tested concept.

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Key Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State-Value Function | $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\big[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \mid S_t = s\big]$ | $V^{\pi}(s)$ |
| 2 | Action-Value Function | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\big[R_{t+1} + \gamma V^{\pi}(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t=s, A_t=a\big]$ | $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ |
| 3 | Bellman Eq. for $V^{\pi}$ | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\big[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\big]$ | Backup with $\pi$ |
| 4 | Iterative PE Update | $V_{k+1}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\big[r + \gamma V_{k}(s')\big]$ | $\to V^{\pi}$ |
| 5 | Greedy Policy | $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\big[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\big]$ | Improvement |
| 6 | Bellman Optimality Eq. | $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\big[r + \gamma V^{*}(s')\big]$ | $V^{*}$ |
| 7 | Value Iteration | $V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\big[r + \gamma V_{k}(s')\big]$ | $\to V^{*}$ |
| 8 | Discount Factor Bound | $\gamma \in [0,1)$ | Guarantees convergence |
| 9 | Backup Operator $T^{\pi}$ | $(T^{\pi} V)(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\big[r + \gamma V(s')\big]$ | Contraction |
| 10 | Contraction Property | $\Vert T^{\pi} V - T^{\pi} U \Vert_{\infty} \leq \gamma \Vert V - U \Vert_{\infty}$ | $\gamma$-contraction |

### Real-World Utility of This Framework

- **Operations Research**: Optimal control of supply chains and inventory.
- **Telecommunications**: Routing protocols in packet-switched networks.
- **Smart Grid**: Optimizing energy storage dispatch policies.
- **Healthcare**: Adaptive treatment policies for chronic disease management.
- **Manufacturing**: Scheduling under uncertainty.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bellman Expectation Equation to Iterative Policy Evaluation

We start from the recursive definition of the return:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \cdots = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$

Taking the expectation under policy $\pi$ conditioned on $S_t = s$:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[G_t \mid S_t = s\right] = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\right]$$

Using linearity of expectation and the Markov property (future depends only on $S_{t+1}$):

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\right] + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\right]$$

The second term, by the Markov property, is $\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[V^{\pi}(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s\right]$. Expanding both expectations over actions and next-state/reward pairs:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\big[\,r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\,\big] \quad \blacksquare$$

This is the **Bellman expectation backup**. It says: *the value of a state equals the expected immediate reward plus the discounted value of the next state, averaging over what the policy does and what the environment returns.*

### Why Iteration Works ŌĆö The Contraction Argument

Define the **Bellman expectation operator** $T^{\pi}$ acting on any function $V : \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}$:

$$(T^{\pi} V)(s) \doteq \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\big[r + \gamma V(s')\big]$$

Then $V^{\pi}$ is the **unique fixed point** of $T^{\pi}$, i.e., $V^{\pi} = T^{\pi} V^{\pi}$. To see why iteration converges, we show $T^{\pi}$ is a $\gamma$-contraction in the max norm:

$$\big\Vert T^{\pi} U - T^{\pi} V \big\Vert_{\infty} = \gamma\, \big\Vert U - V \big\Vert_{\infty}$$

This is a direct consequence of the discount factor bounding the propagation of value differences. By the **Banach Fixed-Point Theorem**, repeated application of a contraction has a unique fixed point, and the iterates converge geometrically:

$$\big\Vert V_{k+1} - V^{\pi} \big\Vert_{\infty} \leq \gamma\, \big\Vert V_{k} - V^{\pi} \big\Vert_{\infty} \;\leq\; \gamma^{k}\, \big\Vert V_{0} - V^{\pi} \big\Vert_{\infty}$$

Thus the error shrinks by factor $\gamma$ each sweep.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Iterative Policy Evaluation on a 3-State Chain

**Problem Setup (Linear Chain MDP).**

- States: $\mathcal{S} = \{ s_1, s_2, s_3 \}$, where $s_3$ is the **terminal** (absorbing) state.
- Actions: Single deterministic action, $A = \{ a \}$.
- Transitions: $s_1 \xrightarrow{a} s_2 \xrightarrow{a} s_3$.
- Rewards: $R(s_1, a) = 0$, $\;R(s_2, a) = +1$, $\;R(s_3, \cdot) = 0$.
- Discount: $\gamma = 0.9$.
- Policy: $\pi(a \mid s) = 1$ for all $s$ (deterministic).

**Exact (closed-form) values.** The terminal state has $V^{\pi}(s_3) = 0$. Working backwards:

$$V^{\pi}(s_2) = R(s_2, a) + \gamma V^{\pi}(s_3) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 1$$

$$V^{\pi}(s_1) = R(s_1, a) + \gamma V^{\pi}(s_2) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 1 = 0.9$$

So $V^{\pi} = (V^{\pi}(s_1), V^{\pi}(s_2), V^{\pi}(s_3)) = (0.9, 1.0, 0)$.

**Iterative computation.** Start with $V_0 = (0, 0, 0)$. Apply the Bellman backup:

$$V_{k+1}(s) = R(s, a) + \gamma V_{k}(s') \quad\text{where } s' \text{ is the next state.}$$

**Sweep $k = 0 \to 1$:**

$$V_1(s_1) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot V_0(s_2) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 0$$

$$V_1(s_2) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot V_0(s_3) = 1 + 0 = 1$$

$$V_1(s_3) = 0 \;\;\text{(terminal)}$$

So $V_1 = (0, 1, 0)$.

**Sweep $k = 1 \to 2$:**

$$V_2(s_1) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot V_1(s_2) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 1 = 0.9$$

$$V_2(s_2) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot V_1(s_3) = 1 + 0 = 1$$

$$V_2(s_3) = 0 \;\;\text{(terminal)}$$

So $V_2 = (0.9, 1, 0) = V^{\pi}$. **Converged in 2 sweeps!**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why so fast?** This example has a *very* short effective horizon. In longer-horizon problems (e.g., grid worlds), you may need dozens to thousands of sweeps, but the geometric rate ($\gamma^{k}$) still holds.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Policy Improvement on the Same MDP

Suppose we now add a second action $a'$, where $s_1 \xrightarrow{a'} s_3$ directly, and $s_2 \xrightarrow{a'} s_2$ (stay in place). The reward is unchanged. Suppose we start with the deterministic policy $\pi$ that always picks $a$ (from Section 3.2), so $V^{\pi} = (0.9, 1.0, 0)$.

**Question:** Can we improve at $s_1$ by switching to $a'$?

Using the Bellman optimality backup at $s_1$:

$$Q^{\pi}(s_1, a)  = R(s_1, a) + \gamma V^{\pi}(s_2)  = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 1.0 = 0.9$$

$$Q^{\pi}(s_1, a') = R(s_1, a') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s_3) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 0$$

Greedy choice at $s_1$: $\arg\max\{0.9, 0\} = a$. So no improvement is possible at $s_1$ ŌĆö the original action is already optimal for the given value function.

**Now suppose the reward for $a'$ from $s_1$ is $R = +1.5$ instead of $0$:**

$$Q^{\pi}(s_1, a') = 1.5 + 0.9 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_3) = 1.5 + 0 = 1.5$$

Greedy choice at $s_1$: $\arg\max\{0.9, 1.5\} = a'$. **Policy improvement triggers!** Set $\pi'(s_1) = a'$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing DP Methods

| Aspect | Iterative PE | Policy Iteration | Value Iteration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Find $V^{\pi}$ for fixed $\pi$ | Find $\pi^{*}$ and $V^{*}$ | Find $V^{*}$ (extract $\pi^{*}$) |
| **Update rule** | Backup with $\pi$ (sum over $a$) | $\pi$ in PE step; $\max$ in PI step | Single backup with $\max$ |
| **Per-iteration cost** | $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert^{2} \vert \mathcal{A} \vert)$ | Same as PE, plus $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert \vert \mathcal{A} \vert)$ for improvement | $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert^{2} \vert \mathcal{A} \vert)$ |
| **Convergence** | Linear with factor $\gamma$ | Finite iterations to $\pi^{*}$ | Linear with factor $\gamma$ |
| **Storage** | $V$ array | $V$ and $\pi$ arrays | $V$ array (no explicit $\pi$) |
| **Practical use** | Analysis, baseline | Small-to-medium MDPs | Large MDPs, online deployment |

## 3.5 Derivation: Why Greedy Improvement Works (Sketch)

Given $V^{\pi}$, suppose we define a candidate policy $\pi'$ that greedily maximizes the **one-step look-ahead**:

$$\pi'(s) = \arg\max_{a}\, \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\big[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\big] = \arg\max_{a} Q^{\pi}(s, a)$$

We want to show $V^{\pi'}(s) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$ for all $s$. Define:

$$\delta(s) = V^{\pi}(s) - V^{\pi'}(s)$$

From the definition of $\pi'$ as greedy:

$$V^{\pi'}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi'(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\big[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\big]$$

(Wait ŌĆö this is *not* $V^{\pi'}$ using $V^{\pi'}$, it's using $V^{\pi}$.) We get:

$$V^{\pi'}(s) \geq \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\big[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\big] = V^{\pi}(s)$$

The first inequality uses the fact that $\pi'$ is the greedy maximizer of the right-hand side (using $V^{\pi}$ as the look-ahead). The last equality is just the Bellman equation for $V^{\pi}$. Hence $V^{\pi'}(s) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Policy Iteration ŌĆö Functional Flow

The complete policy-iteration loop, showing the interaction between the value function and the policy:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize arbitrary policy pi] --> B[Set V s = 0 for all states]
    B --> C[Policy Evaluation Sweep]
    C --> D{Convergence check on V}
    D -- Not converged --> C
    D -- Converged --> E[Compute Q pi s a for all s, a]
    E --> F[Greedy Policy Update]
    F --> G{Policy stable?}
    G -- No change in pi --> H[Return optimal pi star and V star]
    G -- Policy changed --> C
```

**Reading guide:**
- The inner loop (C Ōåö D) is the *prediction* step ŌĆö refining $V$ for the current $\pi$.
- The arrow from D ŌåÆ E ŌåÆ F is the *control* step ŌĆö using the converged $V$ to update $\pi$.
- The outer cycle (G ŌåÆ C) repeats until $\pi$ stops changing.

## 4.2 Backup Diagram ŌĆö Tree Structure of the Bellman Update

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[State s]
    a1[Action a1]
    a2[Action a2]
    a3[Action a3]
    s1[Next state s1 prime, reward r1]
    s2[Next state s2 prime, reward r2]
    s3[Next state s3 prime, reward r3]
    v1[V s1 prime]
    v2[V s2 prime]
    v3[V s3 prime]
    root --> a1
    root --> a2
    root --> a3
    a1 --> s1
    a1 --> s2
    a1 --> s3
    s1 --> v1
    s2 --> v2
    s3 --> v3
```

**Reading guide:**
- The root node is the current state $s$.
- The first level shows all possible actions (sum/branch weighted by $\pi(a \mid s)$).
- The second level shows all possible $(s', r)$ outcomes (branch weighted by $p(s', r \mid s, a)$).
- The leaves hold the values $V(s')$, which are then summed with the reward $r$ and discounted by $\gamma$.
- For **iterative policy evaluation** we **sum** over actions; for **value iteration** we **maximize** over actions.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö DP-Based RL Agent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EnvironmentBlock[Environment MDP]
        EnvDyn[p s prime, r mid s, a]
        EnvR[Reward Signal R]
        EnvSprime[Next State S prime]
    end
    subgraph AgentBlock[RL Agent]
        Pol[Policy pi]
        ValEst[Value Estimator V pi]
        Imp[Improver Module]
    end
    Pol -->|action a| EnvDyn
    EnvDyn --> EnvR
    EnvDyn --> EnvSprime
    EnvR --> ValEst
    EnvSprime --> ValEst
    ValEst -->|V pi values| Imp
    Imp -->|updated pi| Pol
```

**Reading guide:**
- The **Environment** provides transition dynamics and rewards.
- The **Policy** is the agent's current strategy.
- The **Value Estimator** uses environment data to compute $V^{\pi}$.
- The **Improver Module** takes $V^{\pi}$ and produces a new (greedy) policy.
- The cycle continues until the policy stabilizes.

## 4.4 Convergence Landscape ŌĆö Value Iteration Contraction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V0[V sub 0: random init] -->|max-backup| V1[V sub 1]
    V1 -->|max-backup| V2[V sub 2]
    V2 -->|max-backup| Vdots[...]
    Vdots -->|max-backup| Vstar[V sub star: optimal value]
    Vstar -->|greedy extraction| Pistar[pi sub star: optimal policy]
```

**Reading guide:**
- Each arrow is one Bellman optimality backup ($T$ operator).
- The error decays geometrically: $\Vert V_{k+1} - V^{*} \Vert_{\infty} \leq \gamma \Vert V_{k} - V^{*} \Vert_{\infty}$.
- For $\gamma = 0.9$, after $k = 22$ sweeps, the worst-case error shrinks by a factor of $0.9^{22} \approx 0.098$ (about 10├Ś reduction).
