<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout.** Three classic mistakes that drain marks:
> 1. **Confusing the backup operators.** Iterative PE uses **sum** over actions (weighted by $\pi$); value iteration uses **max** over actions. Mixing them up is an instant 3-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the terminal-state boundary condition.** In iterative PE, $V(\text{terminal}) = 0$ *always* ŌĆö it is not updated. Many students propagate values into terminal states and lose 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 3. **Stating the policy-improvement theorem without the strictness condition.** If $Q^{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) > V^{\pi}(s)$ for *any* state, the improvement is *strict* for that state. Examiners test this subtlety.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define policy evaluation. Mention the Bellman equation used for it.
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Policy Evaluation** is the process of computing the state-value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ for a given stationary policy $\pi$ in a Markov Decision Process (MDP).

It is defined as the expected return when starting in state $s$ and following $\pi$ thereafter:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, S_t = s\right]$$

The corresponding **Bellman expectation equation** (1 mark each for equation and explanation):

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\big[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\big]$$

This is solved iteratively (Bellman backup) until convergence to $V^{\pi}$. (1 mark)

---

### Q2. State the policy improvement theorem.
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Policy Improvement Theorem (Statement, 2 marks):**
Let $\pi$ and $\pi'$ be any two deterministic policies such that for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$:

$$Q^{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$$

Then the policy $\pi'$ is at least as good as $\pi$, i.e.,

$$V^{\pi'}(s) \geq V^{\pi}(s) \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$$

Furthermore, if the inequality is strict for even a single state, then $\pi'$ is strictly better than $\pi$ at that state. (1 mark for the strict-improvement corollary)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Part B offers a Module-wise internal choice. Both alternatives below are independent 14-mark questions with the standard $(a) = 7$ marks, $(b) = 7$ marks split.

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the iterative policy evaluation algorithm. [7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Setup (1 mark).** Consider a finite MDP with state set $\mathcal{S}$, action set $\mathcal{A}$, transition dynamics $p(s',r \mid s,a)$, reward function, and discount $\gamma \in [0,1)$. We are given a fixed policy $\pi(a \mid s)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Initialize** (1 mark). Set $V_0(s) = 0$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ (terminal states are always $0$).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Iterative update** (2 marks). For $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ and for every non-terminal $s$:

$$V_{k+1}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\big[r + \gamma V_{k}(s')\big]$$

This is called a **Bellman backup** or **sweep**. We use $V_{k+1}$ (new) or update $V_k$ in place.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Termination** (1 mark). Stop when $\max_s \vert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \vert < \theta$ for some small threshold $\theta$ (e.g., $10^{-4}$).

**Step 5 ŌĆö Convergence** (1 mark). As $k \to \infty$, $V_k \to V^{\pi}$ geometrically: $\Vert V_{k+1} - V^{\pi} \Vert_{\infty} \leq \gamma^{k} \Vert V_0 - V^{\pi} \Vert_{\infty}$ (Banach contraction).

**Step 6 ŌĆö Pseudocode / example** (1 mark). On a worked example (e.g., 3-state chain from Section 3.2), show two sweeps converging to $(0.9, 1, 0)$.

#### (b) Explain the policy iteration algorithm. How does it guarantee convergence to the optimal policy? [7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm (3 marks):**
1. **Initialize** $\pi_0$ arbitrarily and $V(s) = 0$.
2. **Loop**:
   - **Policy Evaluation**: Compute $V^{\pi_k}$ (via iterative PE).
   - **Policy Improvement**: For each $s$, set
     $$\pi_{k+1}(s) = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\big[r + \gamma V^{\pi_k}(s')\big]$$
3. **Stop** when $\pi_{k+1} = \pi_k$. Then $\pi_k$ is optimal.

**Convergence argument (3 marks):**
- **Monotonicity**: Policy Improvement Theorem guarantees $V^{\pi_{k+1}}(s) \geq V^{\pi_k}(s)$ for all $s$, with strict inequality if any state is not already greedy.
- **Finite MDP**: There are only finitely many deterministic policies ($\vert \mathcal{A} \vert^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert}$). Monotonic improvement + finiteness $\Rightarrow$ termination in a finite number of iterations.
- **Optimality**: At termination, no improving move exists, so the greedy step yields the same policy. This means $V^{\pi}(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$, which is precisely the **Bellman optimality equation**. Therefore $\pi = \pi^{*}$.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Draw the Evaluate ŌåÆ Improve cycle (see Section 4.1).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

#### (a) Differentiate between policy iteration and value iteration. [7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion (1 mark each) | Policy Iteration | Value Iteration |
|---|---|---|
| **PE depth** | Runs PE to full convergence before each improvement | Performs only one sweep of PE per iteration |
| **Update rule** | $V_{k+1}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi_{k}(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma V_k(s')]$, then $\pi_{k+1} = \text{greedy}(V_{k+1})$ | $V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma V_k(s')]$ |
| **Convergence** | Finite iterations (small MDPs) | Infinite (asymptotic), but geometric with rate $\gamma$ |
| **Output** | Explicit $\pi^{*}$ and $V^{*}$ | $V^{*}$; $\pi^{*}$ extracted at the end |
| **Per-iteration cost** | High (full PE sweep) | Low (single sweep) |
| **Practical use** | Small-to-medium MDPs, theoretical clarity | Large MDPs, when only the optimal value is needed |

**Common ground (1 mark):** Both use the Bellman operator iteratively and converge to the same optimal $V^{*}$ in the limit; they differ only in *how often* policy improvement is invoked.

#### (b) Prove the policy improvement theorem. [7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Statement (1 mark):** Let $\pi, \pi'$ be deterministic policies with $Q^{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$ for all $s$. Then $V^{\pi'}(s) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$ for all $s$.

**Proof (5 marks):**

By hypothesis, for every $s$:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \leq Q^{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma V^{\pi}(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s, A_t = \pi'(s)\right]$$

Since $\pi'$ is deterministic and used as the action selector, the right-hand side is precisely $\mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\!\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma V^{\pi}(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s\right]$. Therefore:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\!\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma V^{\pi}(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s\right] \quad (\star)$$

Apply $(\star)$ recursively to the term $V^{\pi}(S_{t+1})$:

$$V^{\pi}(S_{t+1}) \leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\!\left[R_{t+2} + \gamma V^{\pi}(S_{t+2}) \mid S_{t+1} = s'\right]$$

Substitute back:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\!\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} V^{\pi}(S_{t+2}) \mid S_t = s\right]$$

Continuing the recursion $n$ times:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n} V^{\pi}(S_{t+n}) \mid S_t = s\right]$$

Take $n \to \infty$. For a finite MDP with $\gamma < 1$, the term $\gamma^{n} V^{\pi}(S_{t+n}) \to 0$ (the discount dominates). Hence:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \mid S_t = s\right] = V^{\pi'}(s) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Strict improvement corollary (1 mark):** If $Q^{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) > V^{\pi}(s)$ for at least one state, then the inequality is strict for that state after the first application, and the same recursion preserves strictness.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist ŌĆö go through before entering the exam hall.**

- **Policy Evaluation** computes $V^{\pi}$ for a *fixed* policy; uses **sum** over actions weighted by $\pi$.
- **Policy Improvement** constructs a *greedy* policy using $\arg\max_{a} Q^{\pi}(s, a)$; uses **max** over actions.
- The **Bellman expectation equation** characterizes $V^{\pi}$; the **Bellman optimality equation** characterizes $V^{*}$.
- The **Bellman operator $T^{\pi}$** is a $\gamma$-contraction, guaranteeing geometric convergence of iterative PE.
- The **Policy Improvement Theorem** guarantees that greedy improvement is never worse than the original policy.
- **Policy Iteration** alternates full evaluation + improvement; converges in *finite* iterations to $\pi^{*}$.
- **Value Iteration** truncates evaluation to one sweep per iteration; converges geometrically to $V^{*}$.
- The improved policy at $s$ is $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$.
- **Discount factor $\gamma$** bounds the contraction rate and the effective horizon.
- **Terminal states always have value 0** and are *never* updated.
- **Deterministic vs stochastic policies**: the improvement theorem statement is cleanest for deterministic $\pi'$; stochastic policies require a $\sum_{a}$ formulation.
- **Convergence order of policy iteration**: in the worst case $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert}$, but typically $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert \cdot \vert \mathcal{A} \vert)$ in practice.
- **Markov property** is essential ŌĆö without it, the Bellman backup does not reduce to a state-wise update.
- **Off-policy vs on-policy**: both PE and PI as described are **on-policy** (they evaluate the policy being used to generate data).
- **KTU-favorite trick question**: "What happens if $\gamma = 1$ in an infinite-horizon MDP?" Answer: The contraction argument fails because the operator is no longer strictly contractive; convergence is not guaranteed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Policy Iteration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT868 - INTRODUCTION TO REINFORCEMENT LEARNING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Policy Iteration # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Policy Iteration (PI)** is a foundational **Dynamic Programming (DP)** algorithm in Reinforcement Learning used to compute the **optimal policy** $\pi_*$ for a finite **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**. An MDP is formally defined by the tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle$, where $\mathcal{S}$ is the set of states, $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of actions, $\mathcal{P}$ is the state-transition probability function, $\mathcal{R}$ is the reward function, and $\gamma$ is the **discount factor** ($0 \leq \gamma \leq 1$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Policy Iteration (KTU Syllabus Definition):** > Policy Iteration is an iterative algorithm that alternates between **Policy Evaluation** (computing the state-value function $V_\pi$ for the current policy $\pi$) and **Policy Improvement** (updating $\pi$ greedily with respect to $V_\pi$) until the policy converges to the optimal policy $\pi_*$. Mathematically, the algorithm returns a sequence of monotonically improving policies $\pi_0 \rightarrow \pi_1 \rightarrow \pi_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow \pi_*$ such that: $$V_{\pi_0}(s) \leq V_{\pi_1}(s) \leq V_{\pi_2}(s) \leq \dots \leq V_{\pi_*}(s) = V_*(s) \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Chess Coach and the Player" Imagine a chess coach and a student player learning a new opening: 1. The **student** adopts a particular opening strategy (this is the **policy** $\pi$). 2. The **coach** plays thousands of simulated games using that strategy and estimates, for every board position (state), how good the position is on average (this is **Policy Evaluation** ŌĆö computing $V_\pi$). 3. After analysis, the coach says: *"Whenever you see this position, you should play this move instead because it gives a better long-term outcome"* (this is **Policy Improvement** ŌĆö greedily choosing the action that maximizes the Q-value). 4. The student updates their strategy and the process repeats. > [!NOTE] > Just as the chess player keeps refining their strategy based on the coach's analysis, **Policy Iteration** keeps refining $\pi$ based on the latest $V_\pi$ ŌĆö until no further improvement is possible, at which point the policy is provably **optimal**. ## 1.3 Key Entities Used Throughout - **State-value function:** $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$, the expected return from state $s$ under policy $\pi$. - **Action-value function:** $Q_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$. - **Discount factor** $\gamma$: controls the relative importance of future vs. immediate rewards. - **Bellman Expectation Equation:** the linear system that defines $V_\pi$. ## 1.4 Why Policy Iteration Matters in Engineering | Application Domain | How PI Is Used | |---|---| | **Robotics** | Path planning and locomotion under uncertainty. | | **Autonomous Driving** | Lane-changing and merging policies in stochastic traffic. | | **Industrial Automation** | Optimal control of assembly-line robots. | | **Resource Management** | Energy-grid dispatch, network packet routing, inventory control. | | **Game AI** | Game-theoretic equilibrium computation (chess, Go sub-problems). | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two-Pillar Architecture of Policy Iteration Policy Iteration is built on **two alternating steps** that are executed in a loop until convergence: ### 2.1.1 Pillar 1: Iterative Policy Evaluation Given a fixed policy $\pi$, compute its **state-value function** $V_\pi$. Since $V_\pi$ satisfies the **Bellman Expectation Equation**, we can solve it via repeated application of the Bellman backup operator $\mathcal{T}^\pi$: $$V_{k+1}(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_k(s') \right]$$ - $V_k \to V_\pi$ as $k \to \infty$ when $\gamma < 1$ (or termination is handled). - The operator $\mathcal{T}^\pi$ is a **$\gamma$-contraction** in max-norm, guaranteeing a unique fixed point $V_\pi$. - **Stopping rule (in practice):** halt after $k$ sweeps when $\max_s \vert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \vert < \theta$ for a small threshold $\theta$. ### 2.1.2 Pillar 2: Policy Improvement Using the freshly computed $V_\pi$, generate a strictly better (or equivalent) policy $\pi'$ via **greedy action selection**: $$\pi'(s) = \underset{a \in \mathcal{A}}{\arg\max} \; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right] = \underset{a \in \mathcal{A}}{\arg\max} \; Q_\pi(s, a)$$ - If for some $s$ the greedy action is *not unique*, any of the maximizing actions may be selected. - This step requires **no learning rate** and no randomness; it is a deterministic update. ## 2.2 The Policy Improvement Theorem (Foundation of Correctness) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Policy Improvement Theorem:** Let $\pi$ and $\pi'$ be two deterministic policies such that for every state $s \in \mathcal{S}$: > $$Q_\pi(s, \pi'(s)) \geq V_\pi(s)$$ > Then the policy $\pi'$ is at least as good as $\pi$ everywhere: > $$V_{\pi'}(s) \geq V_\pi(s) \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$$ This theorem guarantees **monotonic improvement** at every step, which is the cornerstone of why Policy Iteration converges. ## 2.3 Convergence Guarantee (Why PI Terminates in Finite MDPs) In a finite MDP with $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$ states and $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ actions, there are exactly $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert}$ deterministic policies ŌĆö a *finite* number. Because each Policy Improvement step yields a strictly better policy (or no change), and a policy cannot be revisited (otherwise the algorithm would loop), PI must terminate in **at most $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert} - 1$ iterations**. Upon termination, the resulting policy satisfies the **Bellman Optimality Equation**, hence is optimal. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Purpose / Notes | |---|---|---| | **Bellman Expectation Backup** | $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) [r + \gamma V_\pi(s')]$ | Solved iteratively during Policy Evaluation. | | **Iterative Policy Evaluation Update** | $V_{k+1}(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) [r + \gamma V_k(s')]$ | One synchronous sweep; $V_k \to V_\pi$ as $k \to \infty$. | | **Greedy Policy Improvement** | $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) [r + \gamma V_\pi(s')]$ | Deterministic update rule. | | **Action-Value under $\pi$** | $Q_\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) [r + \gamma V_\pi(s')]$ | Used inside the $\arg\max$ of improvement. | | **Convergence Stopping Condition** | $\pi_{k+1}(s) = \pi_k(s) \;\; \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$ | Policy no longer changes $\Rightarrow$ optimal. | | **Max # of Iterations (finite MDP)** | $\leq \vert \mathcal{A} \vert^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert} - 1$ | Theoretical upper bound. | | **Discount Factor Range** | $0 \leq \gamma < 1$ | Guarantees contraction; $\gamma = 1$ allowed only with terminal states. | | **Bellman Optimality Equation** | $V_*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) [r + \gamma V_*(s')]$ | The fixed point that $\pi_*$ satisfies. | ## 2.5 Engineering Utility of the Formula Set These equations form the **numerical backbone of approximate DP** methods used in: - **Inventory control** (Arrow-Karlin dynamic inventory models), - **Manufacturing** (optimal maintenance scheduling of stochastic machines), - **Smart-grid** energy management (stochastic cost minimization), - **Autonomous-vehicle** decision modules (lane-changing under uncertainty). > [!NOTE] > Every modern deep-RL method (e.g., DQN, A3C, PPO) can be viewed as a **stochastic, function-approximated generalization** of either Policy Iteration or Value Iteration. ## 2.6 Policy Iteration vs. Value Iteration (Quick Comparison) | Aspect | Policy Iteration | Value Iteration | |---|---|---| | Inner Loop | Full policy evaluation to convergence | Single Bellman backup (truncated) | | Computation per iteration | Expensive (solves a linear system approximately) | Cheap (one sweep) | | Number of iterations to converge | Small (finite in tabular case) | Large (asymptotic) | | Total wall-clock cost | Often lower overall | Often higher overall | | Storage | Stores $V$ **and** $\pi$ | Stores only $V$ | | Convergence | Exact after finitely many steps | Approximate; needs $\varepsilon$-threshold | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö From the Return Definition to the Bellman Expectation Equation **Goal:** Show that $V_\pi$ satisfies a recursive linear equation. The return $G_t$ is defined as the discounted sum of future rewards: $$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$ **Step 1.** By definition, the state-value function is the expected return from state $s$ under policy $\pi$: $$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$$ **Step 2.** Substitute the definition of $G_t$ and split the sum after the first term: $$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$ **Step 3.** Apply the law of iterated expectations, conditioning first on the action $A_t \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)$ and then on the next state $S_{t+1}$ and reward $R_{t+1}$: $$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \right] \right]$$ **Step 4.** Recognize that the inner expectation is exactly $V_\pi(s')$ by definition: $$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$$ This is the **Bellman Expectation Equation**, a system of $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$ linear equations in $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$ unknowns. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Proof of the Policy Improvement Theorem **Given:** Two deterministic policies $\pi$ and $\pi'$ such that $Q_\pi(s, \pi'(s)) \geq V_\pi(s)$ for all $s$. **Step 1.** Start with the definition of $V_\pi$ and apply the assumption that $\pi'$ is at least as good as $\pi$ for the *current* time-step: $$ \begin{aligned} V_\pi(s) & \leq Q_\pi(s, \pi'(s)) \\ & = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V_\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s, A_t = \pi'(s) \right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2.** Now consider a one-step look-ahead where $\pi'$ is followed for the **first** step only and $\pi$ thereafter: $$V_\pi(s) \leq \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, \pi'(s)) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$$ **Step 3.** Iterate this argument: now $\pi'$ is followed for two steps, then $\pi$ for the remainder. Repeating the substitution $k$ times yields: $$V_\pi(s) \leq \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \dots + \gamma^{k-1} R_{t+k} + \gamma^k V_\pi(S_{t+k}) \mid S_t = s, \text{follow } \pi' \text{ for } k \text{ steps} \right]$$ **Step 4.** Take the limit $k \to \infty$. Because $\gamma < 1$ (or states eventually become terminal), the residual term $\gamma^k V_\pi(S_{t+k}) \to 0$: $$\lim_{k \to \infty} \gamma^k V_\pi(S_{t+k}) = 0$$ **Step 5.** The remaining expectation is precisely $V_{\pi'}(s)$ ŌĆö the return when $\pi'$ is followed from $s$: $$V_\pi(s) \leq V_{\pi'}(s) \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S} \quad \blacksquare$$ This proves that the new greedy policy $\pi'$ is **never worse** than $\pi$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Bellman Optimality Equation from Policy Improvement When Policy Iteration terminates, the greedy step yields the **same** policy, i.e., $\pi' = \pi$. By definition of greedy improvement: $$\pi(s) = \underset{a}{\arg\max} \; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$$ If the policy is greedy with respect to its **own** value function, then $V_\pi = V_*$ and the max can be moved outside: $$V_\pi(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$$ This is the **Bellman Optimality Equation** ŌĆö a *nonlinear* system (because of the $\max$) with the unique solution $V_*$. Hence the fixed point of Policy Iteration is provably the optimal value function. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Policy Iteration on a 3-State MDP ### 3.4.1 The MDP Specification | Element | Value | |---|---| | States | $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$ | | Actions | $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2\}$ | | Terminal state | $s_3$ (self-loops with reward 0) | | Discount factor | $\gamma = 0.9$ | | Initial value estimate | $V_0(s) = 0$ for all $s$ | **Transition-Reward Function $p(s', r \mid s, a)$:** | $(s, a)$ | $(s', r)$ | Probability | |---|---|---| | $(s_1, a_1)$ | $(s_2, -1)$ | $1.0$ | | $(s_1, a_2)$ | $(s_1, 0)$ | $1.0$ | | $(s_2, a_1)$ | $(s_3, +2)$ | $1.0$ | | $(s_2, a_2)$ | $(s_1, -1)$ | $1.0$ | | $(s_3, a_1)$ | $(s_3, 0)$ | $1.0$ | | $(s_3, a_2)$ | $(s_3, 0)$ | $1.0$ | **Initial policy $\pi_0$:** $\pi_0(a_1 \mid s_1) = 1$, $\pi_0(a_2 \mid s_2) = 1$, and any action at $s_3$. ### 3.4.2 Iteration 1 #### Policy Evaluation Under $\pi_0$, the Bellman backups are: $$ \begin{aligned} V(s_3) & = 0 \\ V(s_1) & = -1 + 0.9 \, V(s_2) \\ V(s_2) & = -1 + 0.9 \, V(s_1) \end{aligned} $$ Substitute the second into the first: $$V(s_1) = -1 + 0.9 \left[-1 + 0.9 V(s_1)\right] = -1.9 + 0.81 \, V(s_1)$$ Solve: $$0.19 \, V(s_1) = -1.9 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; V(s_1) = -10$$ Then: $$V(s_2) = -1 + 0.9 \cdot (-10) = -10$$ So under $\pi_0$: $V_{\pi_0} = [-10, -10, 0]$. #### Policy Improvement Compute the Q-values: $$ \begin{aligned} Q(s_1, a_1) & = -1 + 0.9 \cdot V(s_2) = -1 + 0.9 \cdot (-10) = -10 \\ Q(s_1, a_2) & = 0 + 0.9 \cdot V(s_1) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot (-10) = -9 \\ Q(s_2, a_1) & = +2 + 0.9 \cdot V(s_3) = +2 + 0 = 2 \\ Q(s_2, a_2) & = -1 + 0.9 \cdot V(s_1) = -1 - 9 = -10 \end{aligned} $$ Greedy choices: $\pi_1(s_1) = a_2$ (since $-9 > -10$); $\pi_1(s_2) = a_1$ (since $2 > -10$). **Policy changed.** ### 3.4.3 Iteration 2 #### Policy Evaluation Under $\pi_1$, $a_2$ at $s_1$ causes a self-loop with reward 0: $$ \begin{aligned} V(s_1) & = 0 + 0.9 \, V(s_1) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; V(s_1) = 0 \\ V(s_2) & = 2 + 0.9 \cdot V(s_3) = 2 \end{aligned} $$ So under $\pi_1$: $V_{\pi_1} = [0, 2, 0]$. #### Policy Improvement $$ \begin{aligned} Q(s_1, a_1) & = -1 + 0.9 \cdot 2 = 0.8 \\ Q(s_1, a_2) & = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 0 \\ Q(s_2, a_1) & = 2 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 2 \\ Q(s_2, a_2) & = -1 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = -1 \end{aligned} $$ Greedy choices: $\pi_2(s_1) = a_1$ (since $0.8 > 0$); $\pi_2(s_2) = a_1$ (since $2 > -1$). **Policy changed at $s_1$.** ### 3.4.4 Iteration 3 #### Policy Evaluation Under $\pi_2$: $$ \begin{aligned} V(s_2) & = 2 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 2 \\ V(s_1) & = -1 + 0.9 \cdot 2 = 0.8 \end{aligned} $$ So $V_{\pi_2} = [0.8, 2, 0]$. #### Policy Improvement $$ \begin{aligned} Q(s_1, a_1) & = -1 + 0.9 \cdot 2 = 0.8 \\ Q(s_1, a_2) & = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 0.8 = 0.72 \\ Q(s_2, a_1) & = 2 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 2 \\ Q(s_2, a_2) & = -1 + 0.9 \cdot 0.8 = -0.28 \end{aligned} $$ Greedy choices: $\pi_3(s_1) = a_1$, $\pi_3(s_2) = a_1$. **No change from $\pi_2$.** ### 3.4.5 Result Summary | Quantity | Final Value | |---|---| | Optimal value function $V_*$ | $V_*(s_1) = 0.8,\; V_*(s_2) = 2,\; V_*(s_3) = 0$ | | Optimal policy $\pi_*$ | $\pi_*(a_1 \mid s_1) = 1,\; \pi_*(a_1 \mid s_2) = 1$ | | Total PI iterations | 3 (convergence in finite steps) | > [!IMPORTANT] > The 3-state example illustrates the **two key facts** of Policy Iteration: > (i) the value function $V_\pi$ monotonically improves with each iteration, and > (ii) the algorithm converges in a **finite** number of steps in any finite MDP. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Policy Iteration Control-Flow Diagram The following Mermaid flowchart visualizes the closed-loop execution of Policy Iteration. Each block represents a deterministic computational stage; the two "pillars" (Policy Evaluation and Policy Improvement) are emphasized as subgraphs for clarity. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([START]) initA["Initialize policy pi_0
and value vector V_0 = 0"] evalStep["POLICY EVALUATION
Run iterative Bellman backups
V_{k+1}(s) = sum_a pi(a-mid-s) sum_{s,r} p(s,r-mid-s,a)[r + gamma V_k(s)]"] convCheckEval{"Max delta V
less than theta?"} improveStep["POLICY IMPROVEMENT
pi_new(s) = argmax_a sum_{s,r} p(s,r-mid-s,a)[r + gamma V_pi(s)]"] convCheckPolicy{"pi_new equals pi_old
for all states s?"} optimalOut[/"OPTIMAL POLICY pi_*
and V_* obtained"/] updateA["Set pi := pi_new
and V := V_pi"] stopA([STOP]) startA --> initA initA --> evalStep evalStep --> convCheckEval convCheckEval -- No --> evalStep convCheckEval -- Yes --> improveStep improveStep --> convCheckPolicy convCheckPolicy -- No --> updateA updateA --> evalStep convCheckPolicy -- Yes --> optimalOut optimalOut --> stopA ``` ## 4.2 Two-Pillar Block Architecture A higher-level architectural view emphasizing the **separation of concerns** between evaluation and improvement: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph pillarEval["PILLAR 1: POLICY EVALUATION"] direction TB inPi[/"Input: Current policy pi"/] bellmanOp["Apply Bellman Expectation Operator T^pi
V_{k+1} = T^pi V_k"] outV[/"Output: Value function V_pi"/] inPi --> bellmanOp --> outV end subgraph pillarImpr["PILLAR 2: POLICY IMPROVEMENT"] direction TB inV[/"Input: Value function V_pi"/] greedyOp["Greedy Action Selection
pi'(s) = argmax_a Q_pi(s,a)"] outPi[/"Output: Improved policy pi'"/] inV --> greedyOp --> outPi end pillarEval -- "V_pi feeds improvement" --> pillarImpr pillarImpr -- "pi' feeds next evaluation" --> pillarEval ``` ## 4.3 Policy Iteration vs. Value Iteration ŌĆö Block Comparison The diagram below contrasts the **two loops** in Policy Iteration (inner: full evaluation) with the **single loop** in Value Iteration (no inner loop). ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph piBlock["POLICY ITERATION"] direction TB piA["Initialize pi"] --> piB["Inner Loop: Policy Evaluation
Iterate V_{k+1} = T^pi V_k until convergence"] piB --> piC["Outer Loop: Policy Improvement
pi' = greedy(V_pi)"] piC --> piD{"pi' == pi?"} piD -- No --> piB piD -- Yes --> piE[/"Return pi_*"/] end subgraph viBlock["VALUE ITERATION"] direction TB viA["Initialize V"] --> viB["Single Loop
V_{k+1}(s) = max_a sum_{s,r} p(s,r-mid-s,a)[r + gamma V_k(s)]"] viB --> viC{"Max delta V
less than theta?"} viC -- No --> viB viC -- Yes --> viD["Extract pi(s) = argmax_a sum_{s,r} p(s,r-mid-s,a)[r + gamma V(s)]"] viD --> viE[/"Return pi_*/"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > In Policy Iteration the **value function** is always **consistent** with the current policy; in Value Iteration the value function is only **optimal in the limit** and may be inconsistent with any single policy during intermediate sweeps. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper, Module 2, 2024 Scheme] **Define Policy Iteration in the context of a Markov Decision Process. List its two main steps.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Policy Iteration is a dynamic programming algorithm that iteratively computes the optimal policy $\pi_*$ of a finite MDP by alternating between two operations: Policy Evaluation and Policy Improvement. - **Step 1 ŌĆö Policy Evaluation (1 mark):** Given a policy $\pi$, compute its state-value function $V_\pi$ by solving (or iteratively approximating) the Bellman Expectation Equation: $$V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$$ - **Step 2 ŌĆö Policy Improvement (1 mark):** Update the policy greedily with respect to the computed $V_\pi$: $$\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$$ The loop terminates when $\pi' = \pi$, yielding the optimal policy. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper, Module 2, 2024 Scheme] **State the Policy Improvement Theorem. Why is it important for the convergence of Policy Iteration?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Statement (2 marks):** Let $\pi$ and $\pi'$ be two deterministic policies such that for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$: $$Q_\pi(s, \pi'(s)) \geq V_\pi(s)$$ Then $\pi'$ is at least as good as $\pi$ everywhere: $V_{\pi'}(s) \geq V_\pi(s)$ for all $s$. - **Importance for convergence (1 mark):** The theorem guarantees that the greedy policy update **monotonically improves** (or leaves unchanged) the value at every state. Because there are only finitely many deterministic policies ($\vert \mathcal{A} \vert^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert}$), monotonic improvement cannot cycle, ensuring finite-step convergence to the optimal policy $\pi_*$. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:** > (1) Many students **forget the outer-loop convergence check** $\pi_{k+1} = \pi_k$, and report the policy as "optimal" after one improvement step. Always verify the policy has stabilized. > (2) In Policy Evaluation, students often **drop the $\pi(a \mid s)$ term** when the policy is deterministic ŌĆö but for stochastic policies it MUST be retained. > (3) Confusion between **$Q_\pi$** (action-value) and **$V_\pi$** (state-value) leads to incorrect greedy updates. Always compute $Q_\pi(s, a)$ *first*, then take $\arg\max_a$. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) #### (a) Derive the Bellman Expectation Equation for the state-value function $V_\pi(s)$ from first principles. Explain how it forms the basis of Iterative Policy Evaluation. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Define the return (1 mark):** The discounted return from time $t$ is $$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}, \quad 0 \leq \gamma < 1$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Define the state-value function (1 mark):** $$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Split the return at the first time-step (1 mark):** $$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Condition on the action via the policy (1 mark):** $$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \, \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Condition on the next state and reward via $p$ (1 mark):** $$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \right] \right]$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Recognize the inner term as $V_\pi(s')$ (1 mark):** $$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$$ **Step 7 ŌĆö Connect to Iterative Policy Evaluation (1 mark):** This is a linear system of $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$ equations. Rather than solve it directly, the iterative form $$V_{k+1}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_k(s') \right]$$ is applied as a **$\gamma$-contraction**; $V_k \to V_\pi$ geometrically as $k \to \infty$, forming the computational engine of Policy Evaluation. #### (b) Consider the 3-state MDP from Section 3.4 with discount $\gamma = 0.9$ and initial policy $\pi_0(a_1 \mid s_1) = 1$, $\pi_0(a_2 \mid s_2) = 1$. Perform one full iteration of Policy Iteration and show all intermediate computations. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Policy Evaluation for $\pi_0$ (3 marks):** Under $\pi_0$, the Bellman system is: $$V(s_1) = -1 + 0.9 V(s_2), \quad V(s_2) = -1 + 0.9 V(s_1), \quad V(s_3) = 0$$ Substituting: $$V(s_1) = -1 + 0.9 \left[-1 + 0.9 V(s_1)\right] = -1.9 + 0.81 V(s_1) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.19 V(s_1) = -1.9 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V(s_1) = -10$$ $$V(s_2) = -1 + 0.9(-10) = -10, \quad V(s_3) = 0$$ Thus $V_{\pi_0} = [-10, -10, 0]$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute action-values (2 marks):** $$Q(s_1, a_1) = -1 + 0.9 \cdot (-10) = -10$$ $$Q(s_1, a_2) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot (-10) = -9$$ $$Q(s_2, a_1) = 2 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = +2$$ $$Q(s_2, a_2) = -1 + 0.9 \cdot (-10) = -10$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Greedy policy improvement (2 marks):** $$\pi_1(s_1) = \arg\max \{Q(s_1, a_1), Q(s_1, a_2)\} = \arg\max \{-10, -9\} = a_2$$ $$\pi_1(s_2) = \arg\max \{Q(s_2, a_1), Q(s_2, a_2)\} = \arg\max \{2, -10\} = a_1$$ **Conclusion:** After one full iteration, the policy has **changed** (from $\pi_0$ to $\pi_1$), so Policy Iteration must continue. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) #### (a) State and prove the Policy Improvement Theorem. Clearly indicate the role of the discount factor $\gamma$ in the proof. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **Statement (1 mark):** Let $\pi$ and $\pi'$ be two deterministic policies such that $Q_\pi(s, \pi'(s)) \geq V_\pi(s)$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$. Then $V_{\pi'}(s) \geq V_\pi(s)$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$. **Proof (6 marks):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Base step (1 mark):** Start with the assumption: $$V_\pi(s) \leq Q_\pi(s, \pi'(s))$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Unroll the expectation (1 mark):** By definition of $Q_\pi$: $$V_\pi(s) \leq \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, \pi'(s)) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Iterate the inequality (1 mark):** Apply the same argument repeatedly. After $k$ iterations, $\pi'$ is followed for $k$ steps and $\pi$ thereafter: $$V_\pi(s) \leq \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \gamma^j R_{t+j+1} + \gamma^k V_\pi(S_{t+k}) \mid S_t = s,\; \text{follow } \pi' \text{ for } k \text{ steps} \right]$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Take the limit (2 marks):** As $k \to \infty$, since $0 \leq \gamma < 1$ and $\vert V_\pi \vert$ is bounded, $\gamma^k V_\pi(S_{t+k}) \to 0$. The remaining infinite sum is exactly $V_{\pi'}(s)$: $$V_\pi(s) \leq V_{\pi'}(s) \quad \blacksquare$$ **Role of $\gamma$ (1 mark):** The discount factor $\gamma < 1$ ensures the contraction of the residual term $\gamma^k V_\pi(S_{t+k}) \to 0$, allowing the limit $k \to \infty$ to be taken safely. Without $\gamma < 1$ the term would not vanish in non-episodic MDPs. #### (b) Compare and contrast Policy Iteration with Value Iteration. Discuss computational trade-offs in tabular settings. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** | Aspect | Policy Iteration | Value Iteration | |---|---|---| | **Algorithmic structure (2 marks)** | Two nested loops: outer policy improvement, inner policy evaluation to convergence. | Single loop combining evaluation and improvement in one Bellman-optimality backup. | | **Update rule (1 mark)** | First fully evaluate $V_\pi$, then update $\pi$ greedily. | Direct update: $V_{k+1}(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma V_k(s')]$. | | **Convergence (1 mark)** | Converges in **finite** steps (at most $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert} - 1$) in a finite MDP. | Converges **asymptotically** as $k \to \infty$ at a geometric rate $\gamma$. | | **Per-iteration cost (1 mark)** | Expensive ŌĆö each outer iteration requires many inner Bellman sweeps. | Cheap ŌĆö one sweep per iteration. | | **Total wall-clock cost (1 mark)** | Usually lower overall because the number of outer iterations is small. | Often higher because many iterations are needed. | | **Storage and practicality (1 mark)** | Stores $V$ and $\pi$ explicitly. | Stores only $V$; policy is extracted at the end. | **Conclusion:** In tabular settings, Policy Iteration is typically the preferred method when the state space is moderate. For very large state spaces, **modified Policy Iteration** (truncated evaluation) and Value Iteration variants are computationally more attractive. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition (high-priority):** Policy Iteration = Iterative **Policy Evaluation** + Greedy **Policy Improvement**, repeated until the policy stabilizes. - **Two pillars (high-priority):** *Policy Evaluation* solves the **Bellman Expectation Equation**; *Policy Improvement* uses a **greedy $\arg\max$** over Q-values. - **Iterative form (KTU favorite):** $V_{k+1}(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) [r + \gamma V_k(s')]$. - **Convergence theorem:** Finite in tabular MDPs (at most $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert} - 1$ outer iterations). - **Policy Improvement Theorem:** $Q_\pi(s, \pi'(s)) \geq V_\pi(s) \;\;\forall s \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; V_{\pi'}(s) \geq V_\pi(s) \;\;\forall s$. - **Stopping criterion:** $\pi_{k+1}(s) = \pi_k(s)$ for all $s$ (not just $\max_s \vert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \vert$). - **Discount factor requirement:** $0 \leq \gamma < 1$ (or episodic MDPs with terminal states) to guarantee contraction and termination of the inner loop. - **Bellman Optimality Equation:** The fixed point that the algorithm's converged solution satisfies: $$V_*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) [r + \gamma V_*(s')]$$ - **Common errors to avoid (exam traps):** (i) Confusing $V_\pi$ and $Q_\pi$; (ii) Forgetting to re-evaluate the policy after each improvement; (iii) Using $\gamma = 1$ in non-episodic MDPs; (iv) Reporting a policy as optimal after a single improvement step. - **Engineering significance:** Policy Iteration is the conceptual ancestor of all modern actor-critic and policy-gradient methods used in deep RL (e.g., PPO, A3C). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Revision Tip:** Always pair each **policy update** with a **value update** when solving numerical problems; the algorithm's correctness hinges on the *consistency* between $V_\pi$ and $\pi$ at every outer iteration.

topics.title: Value Iteration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT868 - INTRODUCTION TO REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Value Iteration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Value Iteration (VI)** is a model-based, dynamic programming algorithm in Reinforcement Learning (RL) used to compute the **optimal value function** $V^*(s)$ ŌĆö and consequently the **optimal policy** $\pi^*(a \mid s)$ ŌĆö of a finite **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**. It does this by iteratively applying the **Bellman Optimality Backup Operator** $T^*$ to an arbitrary initial value function $V_0(s)$ until convergence.

Formally, the update rule for each state $s \in \mathcal{S}$ at iteration $k+1$ is:

$$
V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V_k(s') \right]
$$

where $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the **discount factor**, $P(s' \mid s, a)$ is the **transition probability**, and $R(s, a, s')$ is the **expected reward**. The algorithm terminates when the maximum change in value across all states, $\max_s \lvert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \rvert$, falls below a small threshold $\theta > 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** Value Iteration is a cornerstone of Module 2 (Dynamic Programming methods in RL). It is a guaranteed question in ESE and is often clubbed with Policy Iteration as a comparison question worth **14 marks**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "GPS Rerouting" Intuition

Imagine you are driving in an unfamiliar city with a GPS navigator that only knows **immediate** road lengths ŌĆö not the distance to your destination. At first, your estimated time-to-destination is zero everywhere. After each "update cycle," the GPS asks every intersection: *"Given what my neighbors currently believe about the destination, what is my best one-step lookahead time?"* It then replaces its old estimate with the best such one-step value.

Over many cycles, these local beliefs propagate backwards through the road network. Eventually, the GPS settles on a consistent estimate: the true shortest travel time. The route you then take is the **optimal policy**.

This is exactly what **Value Iteration** does:
- **GPS intersections** Ōåö **States** $s \in \mathcal{S}$
- **Possible turns** Ōåö **Actions** $a \in \mathcal{A}$
- **Road lengths + time to traverse** Ōåö **Negative reward** (cost)
- **Repeated rerouting cycles** Ōåö **Bellman backups** $V_{k+1} = T^* V_k$
- **Final route** Ōåö **Optimal policy** $\pi^*$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Iterate the *Value*, not the *Policy*"?**
> Unlike Policy Iteration, VI never explicitly stores or improves a policy in between updates. It "bakes" the $\max$ operator directly into the value update, which is why it converges in a *value-space* manner. Policy extraction happens only **after** convergence, using $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^*(s')]$.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $V_k(s)$ | Value estimate of state $s$ at iteration $k$ |
| $T^*$ | Bellman Optimality Operator |
| $\gamma$ | Discount factor; standard is $\mathbf{0.9}$ to $\mathbf{0.99}$ |
| $\theta$ | Convergence tolerance; typical is $\mathbf{10^{-4}}$ |
| $\lVert V_{k+1} - V_k \rVert_\infty$ | Infinity-norm used for stopping criterion |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bellman Optimality Operator $T^*$

Define the **Bellman Optimality Operator** $T^*: \mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{S}|} \to \mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{S}|}$ acting on a value function $V$ as:

$$
(T^*V)(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V(s') \right]
$$

Value Iteration is simply the **fixed-point iteration** of this operator:

$$
V_{k+1} = T^* V_k
$$

The unique fixed point $V^*$ satisfies $V^* = T^* V^*$, which is exactly the **Bellman Optimality Equation** ŌĆö the defining equation of an optimal value function.

## 2.2 Why Does It Converge? ŌĆö The Contraction Property

The operator $T^*$ is a **$\gamma$-contraction** under the infinity norm. For any two value functions $V, U$:

$$
\lVert T^*V - T^*U \rVert_\infty \leq \gamma \, \lVert V - U \rVert_\infty
$$

Because $\gamma < 1$, repeated application of $T^*$ is a contraction mapping. By the **Banach Fixed-Point Theorem**, this guarantees:
1. **Existence** of a unique fixed point $V^*$.
2. **Global convergence** from any initial $V_0$.
3. **Geometric convergence rate** of $\gamma^k$ ŌĆö meaning after $k$ iterations, the error satisfies $\lVert V_k - V^* \rVert_\infty \leq \gamma^k \lVert V_0 - V^* \rVert_\infty$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common 7-mark question asks *"Why does Value Iteration converge?"* The answer must invoke the **contraction mapping property** of $T^*$ under the sup-norm, cite **Banach's Fixed-Point Theorem**, and state the bound $\lVert V_k - V^* \rVert_\infty \leq \gamma^k \lVert V_0 - V^* \rVert_\infty$.

## 2.3 Algorithmic Steps (KTU Exam Pseudocode Standard)

1. **Initialize:** $V_0(s) = 0$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ (or arbitrary finite values).
2. **Repeat** until $\Delta = \max_s \lvert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \rvert < \theta$:
   - $V_{k+1}(s) \leftarrow \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R(s, a, s') + \gamma V_k(s')]$ for every $s$.
   - Compute $\Delta \leftarrow \max(\Delta, \lvert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \rvert)$.
3. **Extract Policy:** For all $s$, $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^*(s')]$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|
| $V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R(s, a, s') + \gamma V_k(s')]$ | **Core Value Iteration update** ŌĆö must be memorized verbatim |
| $(T^*V)(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R(s, a, s') + \gamma V(s')]$ | Bellman Optimality Operator definition |
| $\lVert T^*V - T^*U \rVert_\infty \leq \gamma \lVert V - U \rVert_\infty$ | $\gamma$-contraction property ŌĆö convergence proof |
| $\lVert V_k - V^* \rVert_\infty \leq \dfrac{\gamma^k}{1-\gamma} \cdot \max_s \lvert R(s, a, s') \rvert$ | Finite-time error bound for stopping at iteration $k$ |
| $\Delta = \max_s \lvert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \rvert$ | Infinity-norm stopping criterion; compare with threshold $\theta$ |
| $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^*(s')]$ | Optimal policy extraction post-convergence |
| $K \geq \log_{\gamma} \left( \dfrac{\theta (1-\gamma)}{2 R_{\max}} \right)$ | Number of iterations required to achieve accuracy $\theta$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics Path Planning:** Computing optimal navigation policies in discretized workspaces where the MDP model is known.
- **Autonomous Resource Management:** Power-grid load balancing, where the transition model is stochastic but learnable.
- **Operations Research:** Solving shortest-path and inventory-control problems reducible to an MDP.
- **Game AI (Chess, Go):** Foundations for algorithms like AlphaZero's planning layer.
- **Network Routing:** Adaptive packet routing in stochastic telecommunication networks.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bellman Equation to Value Iteration Update

We start from the **Bellman Optimality Equation** for the optimal value function:

$$
V^*(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \mathbb{E}\left[ R_t + \gamma V^*(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]
$$

Expanding the expectation using the law of total probability over successor states $s' \in \mathcal{S}$:

$$
V^*(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a)\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^*(s') \right]
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recognize as a fixed-point equation:**
Define the operator $T^*$ such that $V^* = T^* V^*$. This is a non-linear system of $|\mathcal{S}|$ equations in $|\mathcal{S}|$ unknowns, generally **not solvable in closed form** for non-trivial MDPs.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply successive approximation (Jacobi-style relaxation):**
Treat $V_k$ as the current "guess" and replace $V^*$ on the right-hand side with $V_k$:

$$
V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a)\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V_k(s') \right]
$$

This is the **Value Iteration update**. At each step we use the *entire previous iterate* $V_k$ (a Gauss-Seidel variant would use updated values within the same sweep, which is also valid).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Verify it is a contraction:**
Take any two value functions $V$ and $U$. For a fixed action $a$, the inner sum is linear in the value of the next state. Because the $\max$ of linear functions is **1-Lipschitz** with respect to the infinity norm, we have:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lvert (T^*V)(s) - (T^*U)(s) \rvert &\leq \max_{a} \left\vert \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot \gamma \left[ V(s') - U(s') \right] \right\vert \\
&\leq \gamma \cdot \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot \lvert V(s') - U(s') \rvert \\
&\leq \gamma \cdot \max_{s'} \lvert V(s') - U(s') \rvert \\
&= \gamma \, \lVert V - U \rVert_\infty
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the max over $s$ on the left yields $\lVert T^*V - T^*U \rVert_\infty \leq \gamma \lVert V - U \rVert_\infty$. Since $0 \leq \gamma < 1$, $T^*$ is a strict contraction, so by Banach's theorem the sequence $V_{k+1} = T^* V_k$ converges geometrically to $V^*$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Two-State MDP

**MDP Definition:**
- States: $s_1$ (non-terminal), $s_2$ (terminal, absorbing with $V(s_2) = 0$).
- Actions from $s_1$: $a_1, a_2$.
- Transition / Reward table:

| Action | $P(s_1 \mid s_1, a)$ | $R(s_1, a, s_1)$ | $P(s_2 \mid s_1, a)$ | $R(s_1, a, s_2)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $a_1$ | $0.8$ | $+1$ | $0.2$ | $+10$ |
| $a_2$ | $0.5$ | $+2$ | $0.5$ | $+4$ |

- **Hyperparameters:** $\gamma = 0.9$, $\theta = 0.01$, $V_0(s_1) = V_0(s_2) = 0$.

**Iteration 1 ($k=0 \to 1$):**

For action $a_1$:

$$
Q_1(s_1, a_1) = 0.8(1 + 0.9 \cdot 0) + 0.2(10 + 0.9 \cdot 0) = 0.8 + 2.0 = 2.8
$$

For action $a_2$:

$$
Q_1(s_1, a_2) = 0.5(2 + 0.9 \cdot 0) + 0.5(4 + 0.9 \cdot 0) = 1.0 + 2.0 = 3.0
$$

Update: $V_1(s_1) = \max(2.8,\, 3.0) = 3.0$, $V_1(s_2) = 0$.
$\Delta_1 = \lvert 3.0 - 0 \rvert = 3.0 > \theta$.

**Iteration 2 ($k=1 \to 2$):**

For $a_1$:

$$
Q_2(s_1, a_1) = 0.8(1 + 0.9 \cdot 3.0) + 0.2(10 + 0.9 \cdot 0) = 0.8(3.7) + 2.0 = 2.96 + 2.0 = 4.96
$$

For $a_2$:

$$
Q_2(s_1, a_2) = 0.5(2 + 0.9 \cdot 3.0) + 0.5(4 + 0.9 \cdot 0) = 0.5(4.7) + 2.0 = 2.35 + 2.0 = 4.35
$$

Update: $V_2(s_1) = \max(4.96,\, 4.35) = 4.96$, $V_2(s_2) = 0$.
$\Delta_2 = \lvert 4.96 - 3.0 \rvert = 1.96 > \theta$.

**Iteration 3 ($k=2 \to 3$):**

For $a_1$:

$$
Q_3(s_1, a_1) = 0.8(1 + 0.9 \cdot 4.96) + 0.2(10 + 0.9 \cdot 0) = 0.8(5.464) + 2.0 = 4.3712 + 2.0 = 6.3712
$$

For $a_2$:

$$
Q_3(s_1, a_2) = 0.5(2 + 0.9 \cdot 4.96) + 0.5(4 + 0.9 \cdot 0) = 0.5(6.464) + 2.0 = 3.232 + 2.0 = 5.232
$$

Update: $V_3(s_1) = 6.3712$, $V_3(s_2) = 0$.
$\Delta_3 = \lvert 6.3712 - 4.96 \rvert = 1.4112 > \theta$.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Iteration $k$ | $Q(s_1, a_1)$ | $Q(s_1, a_2)$ | $V_k(s_1)$ | $\Delta_k$ | Best Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | 0.0000 | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| 1 | 2.8000 | 3.0000 | 3.0000 | 3.0000 | $a_2$ |
| 2 | 4.9600 | 4.3500 | 4.9600 | 1.9600 | $a_1$ |
| 3 | 6.3712 | 5.2320 | 6.3712 | 1.4112 | $a_1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the optimal action **flipped** from $a_2$ to $a_1$ between iterations 1 and 2. This is a frequently asked phenomenon: early in VI, terminal-reward-heavy actions look best, but as discounted future value propagates back, transition-stable actions dominate. This is a classic KTU conceptual trap.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Value Iteration Algorithm Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start Algorithm]) --> initNode["Initialize V_0 s = 0 for all s in S"]
    initNode --> loopStartNode{"Delta >= theta ?"}
    loopStartNode -- Yes --> deltaResetNode["Set Delta = 0"]
    deltaResetNode --> stateLoopNode["For each state s in S"]
    stateLoopNode --> actionLoopNode["For each action a in A s"]
    actionLoopNode --> qCalcNode["Compute Q s, a = sum over s prime of P s prime, s, a times R s, a, s prime + gamma V_k s prime"]
    qCalcNode --> qTrackNode["Track max Q value for this s"]
    qTrackNode --> actDoneNode{"All actions processed ?"}
    actDoneNode -- No --> actionLoopNode
    actDoneNode -- Yes --> vUpdateNode["Set V_{k+1} s = max Q s, a"]
    vUpdateNode --> deltaUpdateNode["Delta = max Delta, absolute V_{k+1} s minus V_k s"]
    deltaUpdateNode --> stateDoneNode{"All states processed ?"}
    stateDoneNode -- No --> stateLoopNode
    stateDoneNode -- Yes --> loopStartNode
    loopStartNode -- No --> policyNode["Extract optimal policy pi star s = argmax a Q s, a"]
    policyNode --> endNode([Return V star and pi star])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Value Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mdpInputNode["MDP Model: P, R, gamma, S, A"] --> bellmanOpNode["Bellman Optimality Operator T star"]
    valInitNode["Value Function Init V_0 s = 0"] --> bellmanOpNode
    bellmanOpNode --> backupNode["Optimality Backup: V_{k+1} s = max_a sum Q s, a"]
    backupNode --> stopCheckNode{"Stop Test: Delta less than theta ?"}
    stopCheckNode -- Not Converged --> bellmanOpNode
    stopCheckNode -- Converged --> optValNode["Optimal Value V star"]
    optValNode --> policyExtNode["Policy Extraction: pi star s = argmax_a Q s, a"]
    policyExtNode --> finalOutputNode["Final Output: V star, pi star"]
```

## 4.3 Convergence Behavior Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    k0Node["k = 0: V_0 = 0"] --> k1Node["k = 1: V_1"]
    k1Node --> k2Node["k = 2: V_2"]
    k2Node --> k3Node["k = 3: V_3"]
    k3Node --> k4Node["..."]
    k4Node --> kNNode["k = N: V_N approx V star"]
    kNNode --> convNode["Geometric Convergence: norm V_k minus V star sub infinity decreases by factor gamma each step"]
```
