# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Importance Sampling Concept `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define importance sampling in the context of off-policy reinforcement learning. What is the importance sampling ratio?

**Model Answer:**
Importance sampling is a statistical technique used in off-policy RL to estimate the value function of a target policy $\pi$ using trajectories generated by a different behavior policy $b$. The **importance sampling ratio** is defined for a trajectory segment from time $t$ to $T-1$ as:

$$\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \dfrac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}$$

This ratio reweights each observed return so that the empirical average converges to the expected return under $\pi$, even though samples were drawn from $b$. The environment dynamics $p$ cancel out in the ratio.

---

### Question 2: On-Policy vs Off-Policy `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]`

**Q:** Distinguish between on-policy and off-policy learning methods. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | On-Policy | Off-Policy |
|---|---|---|
| Data source | Same policy being evaluated | Different (behavior) policy |
| Examples | SARSA, REINFORCE | Q-Learning, DQN, DDPG |
| Exploration | Must be built into target policy | Decoupled; $b$ handles exploration |
| Stability | Generally more stable | Risk of divergence with function approximation |

Example: **SARSA** is on-policy; **Q-Learning** is off-policy because the target uses $\max_a Q(S', a)$ irrespective of how $A'$ was actually selected.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 carries internal choice. Students must answer **either Question A or Question B**. Both choices are provided below for thorough preparation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the importance sampling ratio for off-policy Monte Carlo prediction. Explain ordinary and weighted importance sampling estimators with their bias-variance properties. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

We start with the expected return under target policy $\pi$:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s] = \sum_{\tau} \Pr(\tau \mid \pi) \, G_t(\tau)$$

The probability of a trajectory under $\pi$:

$$\Pr(\tau \mid \pi) = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \mid S_k) \, p(S_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)$$

Under behavior policy $b$:

$$\Pr(\tau \mid b) = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} b(A_k \mid S_k) \, p(S_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)$$

Taking the ratio, the $p$ terms cancel:

$$\dfrac{\Pr(\tau \mid \pi)}{\Pr(\tau \mid b)} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \dfrac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)} = \rho_{t:T-1}$$

Hence:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s] = \mathbb{E}_{b}[\rho_{t:T-1} \, G_t \mid S_t = s]$$

**Ordinary IS (1.5 marks):**

$$\hat{V}_{\text{OIS}}(s) = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \rho_i \, G_t^{(i)}$$

Unbiased ($\mathbb{E}[\hat{V}_{\text{OIS}}] = v_{\pi}(s)$) but **high variance** because $\rho$ can be very large or small.

**Weighted IS (1.5 marks):**

$$\hat{V}_{\text{WIS}}(s) = \dfrac{\sum_i \rho_i \, G_t^{(i)}}{\sum_i \rho_i}$$

Slightly biased (bias $\to 0$ as $N \to \infty$) but **much lower variance**, bounded estimates. Preferred for control tasks.

---

**(b)** Explain the Q-Learning algorithm with its update rule. Discuss its convergence properties and why it is classified as off-policy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm (3 marks):** Q-Learning learns the optimal action-value function $Q^{*}(s, a)$ iteratively using samples from the environment. The update rule is:

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

The corresponding TD target is $Y_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a)$.

**Off-policy nature (2 marks):** The behavior policy $b$ (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy with respect to current $Q$) is used to *select* the next action $A_{t+1}$. However, the *target* of the update uses the $\max$ operator, which corresponds to the greedy (target) policy $\pi$. Since learning occurs about $\pi$ while behavior uses $b$, the algorithm is off-policy.

**Convergence (2 marks):** Under tabular representation, all state-action pairs visited infinitely often, and Robbins-Monro conditions on $\alpha_t$:

$$\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty, \qquad \sum_t \alpha_t^2 < \infty$$

Q-learning converges to $Q^{*}$ with probability 1. Convergence can fail with function approximation due to the **deadly triad** (off-policy + bootstrapping + function approximation).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the problem of **maximization bias** in Q-learning. Describe how Double Q-Learning addresses this issue, with the relevant update equations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Maximization Bias (3 marks):** In Q-learning, the TD target contains $\max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a)$. Since Q-values are *estimates* with stochastic noise, the $\max$ operation systematically selects overestimates, leading to an upward bias. Example: Suppose true $q(s, a_1) = q(s, a_2) = 0$, but estimates are noisy with variance $\sigma^2$. Then $\mathbb{E}[\max(\hat{q}_1, \hat{q}_2)] > 0$, even though the true maximum is $0$. This bias propagates through bootstrapping and corrupts the entire value function estimate.

**Double Q-Learning Solution (4 marks):** Maintain two Q-tables, $Q_1$ and $Q_2$, initialized independently. The update uses one table for action *selection* and the other for action *evaluation*:

$$Q_1(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q_1(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \, Q_2\!\left(S_{t+1}, \arg\max_a Q_1(S_{t+1}, a)\right) - Q_1(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

Symmetrically, with probability 0.5, swap roles:

$$Q_2(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q_2(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \, Q_1\!\left(S_{t+1}, \arg\max_a Q_2(S_{t+1}, a)\right) - Q_2(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

The estimator becomes **unbiased** under symmetric noise because the selection and evaluation noises are independent. Empirically, Double Q-Learning significantly reduces upward bias in benchmarks like the Maximization Bias example from the textbook.

---

**(b)** Explain the **Expected SARSA** algorithm. Compare its variance and bias with Q-Learning. Why is it considered a generalization bridging on-policy and off-policy? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm (3 marks):** Expected SARSA replaces the $\max$ in Q-learning with an expectation under the (possibly stochastic) target policy $\pi$:

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid S_{t+1}) Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

The TD target is $Y_t^{\text{ES}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{a' \sim \pi}[Q(S_{t+1}, a')]$.

**Variance and Bias Comparison (2.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Q-Learning | Expected SARSA |
|---|---|---|
| Bias | Maximization bias | No $\max$ noise |
| Variance | Higher (max is a non-smooth operator) | Lower (smooth expectation) |
| Computation | Cheaper (just $\max$) | Costly (sum over action space) |

**Generalization (1.5 marks):** When $\pi$ is the greedy policy, Expected SARSA reduces to Q-Learning (the expectation collapses to the $\max$ under a deterministic $\pi$). When $\pi = b$, it becomes standard SARSA (on-policy). Thus Expected SARSA interpolates between SARSA and Q-Learning depending on the choice of $\pi$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the coverage condition.** Many students write the IS ratio but fail to mention that we require $b(a \vert s) > 0$ for all $a$ with $\pi(a \vert s) > 0$. Without coverage, the importance sampling estimate is undefined (division by zero in the ratio).
>
> 2. **Confusing ordinary vs weighted importance sampling bias properties.** Writing that both are unbiased (or both are biased) loses 1ŌĆō2 marks. Remember: OIS is unbiased, WIS is biased.
>
> 3. **Writing Q-learning as on-policy.** The most common error. Q-learning is **off-policy** because the target uses $\max$ (greedy) while behavior uses $\epsilon$-greedy.
>
> 4. **Omitting the cancellation of dynamics $p$** in the IS ratio derivation. This is a critical step examiners look for. Without it, the answer is incomplete.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning the deadly triad** when discussing convergence with function approximation. For full marks on convergence questions, students must explicitly state the limitation of Q-learning with neural networks (DQN addresses this partially with experience replay and a target network).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Off-policy learning** decouples exploration from learning: target $\pi$ is learned from data collected under behavior $b$.
- **Importance sampling ratio**: $\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \mid S_k)/b(A_k \mid S_k)$. Dynamics $p$ cancel out.
- **Ordinary IS**: Unbiased but high variance; **Weighted IS**: Biased but low variance (preferred for control).
- **Coverage condition**: $b(a \vert s) > 0$ wherever $\pi(a \vert s) > 0$ ŌĆö mandatory for correctness.
- **Q-learning update**: $Q(S,A) \leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha[R + \gamma \max_a Q(S',a) - Q(S,A)]$.
- Q-learning is **off-policy** because the $\max$ corresponds to a target policy different from $b$.
- **Expected SARSA**: Replaces $\max$ with $\mathbb{E}_{\pi}$, reducing variance and eliminating maximization bias.
- **Maximization bias** arises from noisy estimates under the $\max$ operator.
- **Double Q-Learning** uses two Q-tables for decoupled selection-evaluation; eliminates upward bias.
- **Convergence conditions**: Robbins-Monro ($\sum \alpha_t = \infty$, $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$) + infinite visits.
- **Deadly triad**: Off-policy + bootstrapping + function approximation can cause divergence. Mitigations: target networks, experience replay, Double Q-learning.
- **Modern usage**: DQN, DDPG, TD3, SAC all rely on off-policy TD control foundations discussed here.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TD Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT868 - INTRODUCTION TO REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TD Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Temporal Difference (TD) Prediction** is a model-free, online, bootstrapping-based method in Reinforcement Learning used to estimate the *value function* $V_\pi(s)$ ŌĆö the expected cumulative discounted future reward ŌĆö for a given target policy $\pi$, directly from raw experience trajectories sampled by an agent interacting with the environment, without requiring a model of the environment's dynamics (transition probabilities $P$ or reward function $R$).

Mathematically, the goal of TD prediction is to compute:

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right] = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$

where $G_t$ is the return, $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ is the discount factor, and the expectation is taken under policy $\pi$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** TD Prediction is the cornerstone of *Model-Free Policy Evaluation*. It bridges the gap between **Monte Carlo (MC)** methods (which require episode termination) and **Dynamic Programming (DP)** methods (which require a known model). TD learning is defined by the fact that it **updates estimates based on other learned estimates** ŌĆö a process formally called *bootstrapping*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a student trying to predict whether you will pass the KTU end-semester exam by the time you reach Module 3.

- **Monte Carlo Approach (Wait Until the End):** You do not change your prediction of passing throughout the semester. You wait until the actual exam result is announced at the very end, and only then do you update your internal "probability of passing" estimate based on the final outcome.
- **Dynamic Programming Approach (Need the Syllabus Model):** You build a complete mathematical model of every question, every marking scheme, and your probability of answering each one ŌĆö and then solve the entire semester in one shot.
- **TD Prediction (Learn as You Go):** After every internal assessment (Series 1, Series 2, Assignment), you peek at your current score and form a rough *guess* of the final outcome based on that intermediate signal. You then **shift your belief** in the direction of this guess. You do not wait for the final result (so you learn online), and you do not need a full model of the exam (so it is model-free). You just iteratively refine your estimate using noisy, partial signals ŌĆö this iterative refinement is the **TD Error**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The TD Error ($\delta_t$):** The single most important quantity in this module. It is the difference between what you *predict now* ($R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$) and what you *previously believed* ($V(S_t)$). TD methods literally *learn from the error in their own predictions*.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics Summary

| Property | Monte Carlo | Dynamic Programming | **TD Prediction** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Requires Model of Environment | No | **Yes** | **No** |
| Requires Episode Termination | **Yes** | No | **No** |
| Uses Bootstrapping | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Online / Incremental | No (usually) | Yes (if model known) | **Yes** |
| Bias | Zero (unbiased) | Biased (initially) | **Biased (initially)** |
| Variance | **High** | Low | **Lower than MC** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Convention:** Throughout this module, we use the **discount factor** $\gamma$ with range $[0, 1)$, and assume infinite-horizon discounted MDPs unless otherwise specified. The expected horizon is $1 / (1 - \gamma)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The TD(0) Algorithm ŌĆö Operational Logic

The simplest form of TD prediction is **TD(0)**, the one-step Temporal Difference method. Its update rule is:

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \right]$$

where:
- $V(S_t)$ is the current value estimate of state $S_t$
- $\alpha \in (0, 1]$ is the **step-size / learning rate**
- $R_{t+1}$ is the observed reward after taking action $A_t$
- $\gamma$ is the discount factor
- $V(S_{t+1})$ is the current estimate of the next state's value
- The term in brackets $[\cdot]$ is the **TD error**, denoted $\delta_t$

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Initialize:** Set $V(s)$ arbitrarily for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ (e.g., $V(s) = 0$), with $V(terminal) = 0$.
2. **Loop for each episode:**
    - Initialize state $S_0$ (non-terminal).
    - **For each step $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots$:**
        - Take action $A_t$ from policy $\pi$ given $S_t$, observe reward $R_{t+1}$ and next state $S_{t+1}$.
        - Compute the **TD target**: $\text{Target} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$.
        - Compute the **TD error**: $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$.
        - **Update:** $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \cdot \delta_t$.
        - Transition: $S_t \leftarrow S_{t+1}$.
3. **Termination:** Stop when $S_t$ is a terminal state. Repeat for many episodes until $V(s)$ converges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works ŌĆö The "Why" and "How":** The TD target $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ is itself an unbiased (but noisy, due to $V(S_{t+1})$ being an estimate) sample of the true value $V_\pi(S_t)$. The update rule is essentially a **stochastic gradient descent** step that minimizes the squared error between $V(S_t)$ and this target.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Name | Definition / Formula | Constraint / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V_\pi(s)$ | True Value Function | $\mathbb{E}_\pi[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \mid S_t = s]$ | Exact target |
| $V(s)$ | Estimated Value | Updated online | $\rightarrow V_\pi(s)$ as $t \to \infty$ |
| $G_t$ | Return | $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | Used by MC |
| $\delta_t$ | TD Error | $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ | Convergence $\to 0$ |
| $\alpha$ | Step-size (Learning Rate) | $V \leftarrow V + \alpha \cdot \delta$ | $\alpha \in (0, 1]$ |
| $\gamma$ | Discount Factor | Discounts future rewards | $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ |
| $n$ | n-step horizon | Length of rollout used in target | $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ |
| $V^{(n)}_\pi(s)$ | n-step Return Value | $\mathbb{E}_\pi[\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n}) \mid S_t = s]$ | Backs up $n$ steps |

## 2.3 n-Step TD Prediction ŌĆö A Generalization

TD(0) uses a 1-step lookahead. We can extend this to $n$ steps:

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ G_{t:t+n} - V(S_t) \right]$$

where the **n-step return** is defined as:

$$G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$$

The endpoint convention is: $G_{t:t+n} = G_t$ if $t+n \geq T$ (episode terminated), effectively recovering the **Monte Carlo return**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tradeoff Parameter $n$:** As $n$ increases, the bias of the estimate decreases (less bootstrapping), but variance increases (more stochastic rewards accumulated). $n=1$ is pure TD(0); $n=\infty$ is pure MC. The optimal $n$ is task-dependent.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Robotics:** Real-time policy evaluation where episodes are non-terminating.
- **Finance:** Online estimation of expected future cash flows in continuous markets.
- **Game AI (e.g., AlphaGo):** Bootstrapped value estimates enable planning without rolling out to terminal states.
- **Recommendation Systems:** Online evaluation of user-state values from streaming click data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the TD(0) Update Rule from the Bellman Equation

The **Bellman Equation for the value function** under policy $\pi$ is:

$$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$$

We want a *sample-based, model-free* update rule that converges to $V_\pi(s)$. Let us proceed step by step.

### Step 1: Sample the One-Step Transition

In a real interaction, the agent samples a single transition: $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$. From this, we form the **empirical one-step return** as our sample target for $V_\pi(S_t)$:

$$\hat{Y}_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$$

This is a *noisy, biased* sample of $V_\pi(S_t)$ because $V(S_{t+1})$ is an estimate, not the true $V_\pi(S_{t+1})$.

### Step 2: Define the Loss Function

We use a Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss between the current estimate and the sampled target:

$$\mathcal{L}_t(\theta) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \hat{Y}_t - V(S_t) \right]^{2}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Gradient

The gradient of the loss with respect to $V(S_t)$ is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial V(S_t)} = -\left[ \hat{Y}_t - V(S_t) \right] = -\delta_t$$

### Step 4: Apply Stochastic Gradient Descent

The gradient descent update is:

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial V(S_t)}$$

Substituting the gradient:

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) - \alpha \cdot \left( -\delta_t \right)$$

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \cdot \delta_t$$

Expanding $\delta_t$:

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \right]$$

This is precisely the **TD(0) update rule**. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This derivation shows that TD(0) is a stochastic approximation algorithm for solving the Bellman equation. Under the standard stochastic approximation conditions (Robbins-Monro: $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_t^{2} < \infty$), TD(0) converges to $V_\pi$ with probability 1.

## 3.2 Derivation of the n-Step Return

Starting from the standard return $G_t$:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \dots$$

We truncate the explicit sum at $n-1$ rewards and bootstrap the remainder using $V(S_{t+n})$:

$$G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$$

In compact summation form:

$$G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edge case:** If the episode terminates at time $T < t + n$, then $G_{t:t+n} = G_t$ (the full Monte Carlo return from $t$).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Bias-Variance Tradeoff Across $n$

Let us formalize the bias-variance relationship. Define the **error signal** $e_t = G_{t:t+n} - V_\pi(S_t)$.

| Method | $n$ | Bias (in target) | Variance | Bootstrapping Depth |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| TD(0) | 1 | High (uses $V(S_{t+1})$) | **Lowest** | 1 step |
| TD(2) | 2 | Moderate | Moderate | 2 steps |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| MC | $\infty$ | **Zero** (no bootstrapping) | **Highest** | 0 (fully sampled) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MC has zero bias but high variance:** The MC target $G_t = \sum \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ is an *unbiased* sample of $V_\pi(S_t)$ because it is the definition of $V_\pi$. However, the sum of many random rewards accumulates variance linearly with episode length, hence high variance.

## 3.4 Convergence Analysis of TD(0)

For a fixed policy $\pi$, TD(0) converges in expectation to $V_\pi$ under the **off-policy** projection framework (Tsitsiklis & Van Roy, 1997). The expected update is:

$$\mathbb{E}[\Delta V(S_t)] = \alpha \left[ \mathbb{E}_\pi[R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})] - V(S_t) \right] = \alpha \left[ \mathcal{T}^\pi V(S_t) - V(S_t) \right]$$

where $\mathcal{T}^\pi$ is the **Bellman operator**. Since $\mathcal{T}^\pi$ is a $\gamma$-contraction in the sup-norm ($\| \mathcal{T}^\pi V - \mathcal{T}^\pi U \|_\infty \leq \gamma \| V - U \|_\infty$), repeated application drives the estimate to the unique fixed point $V_\pi$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TD(0) Backup Diagram

The following Mermaid block renders the classical Sutton & Barto *backup diagram* for TD(0). It shows how a single state $S_t$ is updated using a one-step sample transition.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["S_t (Sampled)"] -->|Action A_t| node2["S_{t+1} (Sampled)"]
    node2 -.->|Reward R_{t+1}| node1
    node2 -->|Bootstrapped V| node3["V(S_{t+1}) Estimated"]
    node1 --> node4["V(S_t) Estimated"]
    node3 -->|Target = R + gamma V| node5["TD Target"]
    node4 -->|Update| node6["New V(S_t)"]
    node5 --> node6
    style node1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style node2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style node5 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style node6 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *solid filled nodes* are the sampled (observed) state and the new updated estimate. The *bootstrapped value* $V(S_{t+1})$ provides the lookahead signal without requiring a full rollout.

## 4.2 Monte Carlo Backup Diagram (for Comparison)

```mermaid
graph TD
    mc1["S_t (Sampled)"] -->|A_t| mc2["S_{t+1}"]
    mc2 -->|A_{t+1}| mc3["S_{t+2}"]
    mc3 -->|A_{t+2}| mc4["S_{t+3}"]
    mc4 -->|...| mc5["S_T Terminal"]
    mc5 -.->|Sum of Rewards| mc1
    style mc1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style mc5 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contrast:** MC requires traversing the entire trajectory until the terminal state $S_T$ before producing any update. TD produces an update *at every step*.

## 4.3 n-Step TD Backup Diagram (for general $n$)

```mermaid
graph LR
    p0["S_t"] -->|R_{t+1}| p1["S_{t+1}"]
    p1 -->|R_{t+2}| p2["S_{t+2}"]
    p2 -->|R_{t+3}| p3["S_{t+3}"]
    p3 -->|Dots| pN["S_{t+n} (Bootstrapped)"]
    pN -.->|gamma^n V| p0
    style p0 fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style pN fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
    p0 --> updateN["Update V(S_t) with G_{t:t+n}"]
    style updateN fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 4.4 Algorithm Flow: TD(0) Policy Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    init["Initialize V(s) for all s, V(terminal)=0"] --> loopStart{"For each episode"}
    loopStart -->|Episode| sampleS["Initialize S (non-terminal)"]
    sampleS --> stepLoop{"For each step t"}
    stepLoop -->|Until terminal| actA["Choose A from policy pi given S"]
    actA --> observeR["Observe R, S'"]
    observeR --> compDelta["Compute TD Error delta = R + gamma V(S') - V(S)"]
    compDelta --> updateV["V(S) <- V(S) + alpha times delta"]
    updateV --> assignS["S <- S'"]
    assignS --> stepLoop
    stepLoop -->|Terminal| loopStart
    style init fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style compDelta fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825
    style updateV fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of TD Learning System

```mermaid

    agentBlock --> policyBlock["Policy pi(a|s)"]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Past Year Style Practice Questions

---

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, PECAT868]**
*Define Temporal Difference (TD) Prediction. What is the TD error, and why is it central to the TD learning paradigm?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Temporal Difference (TD) Prediction is a model-free, online method in Reinforcement Learning used to estimate the value function $V_\pi(s)$ of a given policy $\pi$ directly from experience samples. **(1 Mark)**

The **TD error** at time $t$ is defined as:

$$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$$

**(1 Mark)**

It is central to TD learning because it captures the discrepancy between the predicted value and the observed reality (one-step reward plus discounted next value). The TD update rule $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \delta_t$ is essentially a stochastic gradient descent step on the squared TD error, and the entire TD framework can be understood as *learning from the error in one's own predictions* ŌĆö i.e., bootstrapping. **(1 Mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*Differentiate between Monte Carlo (MC) prediction and TD(0) prediction. Mention any two distinguishing factors.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Factor | Monte Carlo | TD(0) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Episode Termination | **Required** (must wait for $S_T$) | **Not required** (learns online) |
| Bootstrapping | Does not bootstrap | Bootstraps on $V(S_{t+1})$ |
| Bias | **Unbiased** estimate of $V_\pi$ | **Biased** initially (uses estimates) |
| Variance | **High** | **Lower** |

**(1 Mark each for two correctly identified differences, 1 Mark for justification/conclusion).**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write "MC is biased" ŌĆö this is **incorrect**. MC estimates are *unbiased*; they have *high variance*. TD(0) estimates are *biased* (because of bootstrapping) but have *lower variance*. Confusing bias and variance costs 1 mark.

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Part B - Question A (14 Marks)**
*`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, PECAT868 Module 3]`*

**(a)** Explain the TD(0) algorithm for policy evaluation with the complete update equation. Describe the role of the step-size parameter $\alpha$ and the discount factor $\gamma$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following deterministic 4-state Markov Reward Process with discount factor $\gamma = 0.9$ and policy that always moves right:

- States: $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ (with $S_4$ terminal).
- Transitions and rewards:
  - $S_1 \xrightarrow{R=0} S_2$
  - $S_2 \xrightarrow{R=0} S_3$
  - $S_3 \xrightarrow{R=1} S_4$ (terminal, $V(S_4) = 0$)
- Initial estimate: $V(S_1) = V(S_2) = V(S_3) = 0.5$, $V(S_4) = 0$.
- Use step-size $\alpha = 0.1$.

Perform **one full pass of TD(0) updates** (one sweep through the episode $S_1 \rightarrow S_2 \rightarrow S_3 \rightarrow S_4$). Show all intermediate computations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) TD(0) Algorithm Explanation (7 Marks):**

The TD(0) algorithm iteratively estimates $V_\pi(s)$ for a fixed policy $\pi$ using the update rule:

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \right] \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Role of $\alpha$ (Step-Size):** It controls the magnitude of the update. A small $\alpha$ leads to slow but stable convergence; a large $\alpha$ can cause oscillations or divergence. To guarantee convergence, $\alpha$ should decrease over time satisfying the Robbins-Monro conditions: $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^{2} < \infty$. **(2 Marks)**

**Role of $\gamma$ (Discount Factor):** It determines the present value of future rewards. $\gamma = 0$ makes the agent myopic (only immediate reward matters); $\gamma \to 1$ makes the agent far-sighted. The effective horizon is $1/(1-\gamma)$. **(2 Marks)**

**Operational Steps:** Initialize $V$ arbitrarily, set $V(\text{terminal}) = 0$. For each step: take action under $\pi$, observe $R_{t+1}$ and $S_{t+1}$, compute TD error $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$, apply update (1). **(3 Marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Sweep (7 Marks):**

We process the episode backward (from $t=2$ down to $t=0$):

**Step $t=2$: At $S_3 \to S_4$ (terminal).**

- $R_{4} = 1$, $V(S_3) = 0.5$, $V(S_4) = 0$.
- $\delta_2 = R_4 + \gamma V(S_4) - V(S_3) = 1 + 0.9 \times 0 - 0.5 = 0.5$. **(1 Mark)**
- $V(S_3) \leftarrow 0.5 + 0.1 \times 0.5 = 0.5 + 0.05 = 0.55$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step $t=1$: At $S_2 \to S_3$.**

- $R_3 = 0$, $V(S_2) = 0.5$, $V(S_3) = 0.55$ (just updated).
- $\delta_1 = R_3 + \gamma V(S_3) - V(S_2) = 0 + 0.9 \times 0.55 - 0.5 = 0.495 - 0.5 = -0.005$. **(1 Mark)**
- $V(S_2) \leftarrow 0.5 + 0.1 \times (-0.005) = 0.5 - 0.0005 = 0.4995$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step $t=0$: At $S_1 \to S_2$.**

- $R_2 = 0$, $V(S_1) = 0.5$, $V(S_2) = 0.4995$ (just updated).
- $\delta_0 = R_2 + \gamma V(S_2) - V(S_1) = 0 + 0.9 \times 0.4995 - 0.5 = 0.44955 - 0.5 = -0.05045$. **(1 Mark)**
- $V(S_1) \leftarrow 0.5 + 0.1 \times (-0.05045) = 0.5 - 0.005045 = 0.494955$. **(1 Mark)**

**Final updated values after one TD(0) sweep:**

$$V(S_1) = 0.494955, \quad V(S_2) = 0.4995, \quad V(S_3) = 0.55, \quad V(S_4) = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for student:** Observe that $V(S_3)$ got the largest update (closest to the reward). Values "ripple backward" through the chain over multiple episodes, eventually converging to the true $V_\pi = [0.729, 0.81, 0.9, 0]$ after many sweeps.

---

### Part B - Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *Internal Choice Alternative*
*`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, PECAT868 Module 3 (Alternate)]`*

**(a)** Derive the TD(0) update rule starting from the Bellman equation. Clearly state the loss function, the gradient, and the final update equation. Why is this method called "bootstrapping"? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **n-step TD prediction**. Write down the n-step return $G_{t:t+n}$ and the corresponding update rule. Discuss the bias-variance tradeoff as $n$ varies from 1 to $\infty$, and identify the two boundary cases. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Derivation of TD(0) (7 Marks):**

Start with the Bellman equation: $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[R_{t+1} + \gamma V_\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s]$. **(1 Mark)**

To obtain a sample-based update, replace $V_\pi(S_{t+1})$ with our current estimate $V(S_{t+1})$ to form the **empirical target**:

$$\hat{Y}_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$$

**Loss function** (MSE): $\mathcal{L}_t = \frac{1}{2}[\hat{Y}_t - V(S_t)]^{2}$ **(1 Mark)**

**Gradient:** $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial V(S_t)} = -[\hat{Y}_t - V(S_t)] = -\delta_t$ **(1 Mark)**

**Gradient Descent Update:** $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) - \alpha \cdot (-\delta_t) = V(S_t) + \alpha \delta_t$ **(1 Mark)**

**Expanded form:** $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha [R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)]$ **(1 Mark)**

**Why "Bootstrapping":** The update to $V(S_t)$ depends on the estimate $V(S_{t+1})$ ŌĆö i.e., the estimate is updated using another estimate, "pulling itself up by its own bootstraps." This distinguishes TD from MC (which uses the actual return $G_t$). **(2 Marks)**

---

**(b) n-Step TD Prediction (7 Marks):**

The **n-step return** generalizes the one-step TD target by using $n$ actual rewards and then bootstrapping:

$$G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$$

**(1 Mark)**

The corresponding **n-step TD update** is:

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha [G_{t:t+n} - V(S_t)]$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Bias-Variance Tradeoff Discussion (4 Marks):**

- As $n$ increases, the target uses more sampled rewards and less bootstrapped estimate ŌåÆ **bias decreases**.
- However, more sampled rewards accumulate stochastic noise ŌåÆ **variance increases**.
- The mean-squared error has a U-shape in $n$, with an optimal $n$ that depends on the task.

**Boundary Cases:**

| $n$ | Method | Bias | Variance |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $n=1$ | **TD(0)** | Highest | Lowest |
| $n=\infty$ | **Monte Carlo** | Zero | Highest |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** For continuing (non-episodic) tasks, $n$ must be finite, and $n$-step methods must handle the *off-trajectory* issue where the agent's actions after time $t+n$ are not aligned with the policy being evaluated. This is a stepping stone toward $\text{TD}(\lambda)$ and eligibility traces.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö TD Prediction (Module 3)**

- [ ] **Goal:** Estimate $V_\pi(s)$ for a fixed policy $\pi$ using sampled experience.
- [ ] **TD(0) Update:** $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha [R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)]$.
- [ ] **TD Error ($\delta_t$):** $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ ŌĆö *the* central quantity.
- [ ] **Bootstrapping:** Update uses an *estimate* ($V(S_{t+1})$), not a full sample return.
- [ ] **Model-Free:** No knowledge of $P(s', r \mid s, a)$ required.
- [ ] **Online:** Updates occur at every time step, no episode termination required.
- [ ] **Bias-Variance:** TD(0) ŌåÆ *biased but low variance*; MC ŌåÆ *unbiased but high variance*.
- [ ] **Convergence:** TD(0) converges to $V_\pi$ if all states are visited infinitely often and $\alpha$ satisfies Robbins-Monro.
- [ ] **n-Step Return:** $G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$.
- [ ] **n-Step Update:** $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha [G_{t:t+n} - V(S_t)]$.
- [ ] **Limiting Cases:** $n=1$ ŌåÆ TD(0); $n=\infty$ ŌåÆ Monte Carlo.
- [ ] **Optimal $n$:** Exists due to bias-variance tradeoff; task-dependent.
- [ ] **Bellman Operator $\mathcal{T}^\pi$:** A $\gamma$-contraction; guarantees unique fixed point $V_\pi$.
- [ ] **Stochastic Approximation View:** TD(0) = stochastic gradient descent on $\frac{1}{2}\delta_t^{2}$.
- [ ] **Real-time use:** Suited for non-terminating, online applications (robotics, finance, streaming).

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Valuation Warnings:**
> 1. **Never** confuse MC's "unbiased" property with "better." Variance matters!
> 2. **Always** state the role of $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ separately ŌĆö they are independent hyperparameters.
> 3. In numerical problems, **show all intermediate computations** of $\delta_t$ and updated $V$ values. Marks are awarded step-by-step.
> 4. When asked for n-step, **explicitly write the summation form** of $G_{t:t+n}$, not just the conceptual description.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Q-Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT868 - INTRODUCTION TO REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Q-Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Q-Learning** is a **model-free**, **off-policy** temporal-difference (TD) reinforcement learning algorithm introduced by **Christopher Watkins (1989)** that directly learns the optimal **action-value function** $Q^*(s, a)$ without requiring a model of the environment's transition dynamics or reward structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Q-Learning is foundational to all modern value-based RL methods (including Deep Q-Networks). The algorithm estimates the expected cumulative discounted reward of taking action $a$ in state $s$ and subsequently following the **optimal policy**.

Mathematically, the learned Q-function approximates:

$$Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi^*}\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \,\bigg|\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a \right]$$

where $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the **discount factor** and $\pi^*$ is the optimal policy.

## 1.2 Key Properties

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Algorithm Type | **Value-Based** |
| Policy | **Off-Policy** (learns optimal while following exploratory) |
| Model Requirement | **Model-Free** (no transition probabilities needed) |
| Bootstrapping | **Yes** (uses TD target) |
| Convergence Guarantee | **Yes** (with sufficient exploration & decaying $\alpha$) |
| Standard Reference | **0.1 Ōēż ╬▒ Ōēż 0.5**, **0.9 Ōēż ╬│ Ōēż 0.99** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Caf├® Explorer" ­¤ŹĄ

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine you are new in a city and want to find the **best caf├®**. You have a notebook (the **Q-Table**). Every time you visit a caf├® (state-action pair), you taste the coffee (immediate reward $r$) and update your notebook with a "quality score" that also factors in *how good the caf├®s near it* are (the **max Q of the next state**).
>
> - **Exploration (random visits):** You sometimes try a caf├® you've never been to.
> - **Exploitation (greedy choice):** Once your notebook is reliable, you always go to the highest-scored caf├®.
> - **Learning rate ($\alpha$):** How quickly you overwrite old opinions.
> - **Discount factor ($\gamma$):** How much you care about future caf├®s versus today's coffee.

Over hundreds of visits, your notebook converges to the **true quality score of every caf├®**, even though you didn't systematically try all of them ŌĆö this is the essence of **Q-Learning**.

## 1.4 Why Q-Learning Matters in Engineering

Q-Learning is the bedrock of:
- **Robotics** (path planning, manipulation)
- **Autonomous Systems** (lane keeping, traffic signal control)
- **Game AI** (Atari, Go, StarCraft)
- **Industrial Control** (HVAC optimization, energy grid balancing)
- **Recommendation Systems** (sequential user-interaction modeling)

The defining engineering virtue is its **simplicity**: no transition model, no policy gradient, no function approximator is strictly required ŌĆö just a table and an update rule.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ŌÜÖ’ĖÅ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Algorithmic Pipeline (Logic Steps)

Q-Learning operates as a closed-loop **AgentŌĆōEnvironment interaction** governed by the Markov Decision Process (MDP) tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$.

1. **Initialize** the Q-Table $Q(s, a)$ arbitrarily for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$, $a \in \mathcal{A}$.
2. **Observe** the current state $s_t$ from the environment.
3. **Select an action** $a_t$ using an exploration strategy (typically **╬Ą-greedy**) over $Q(s_t, \cdot)$.
4. **Execute** $a_t$; observe immediate reward $r_{t+1}$ and next state $s_{t+1}$.
5. **Update** the Q-value using the **Bellman Optimality Backup**:

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \;\leftarrow\; Q(s_t, a_t) \;+\; \alpha \Big[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t) \Big]$$

6. **Set** $s_t \leftarrow s_{t+1}$ and repeat until termination or convergence.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of the Update Equation

| Symbol | Name | Role | Typical Range |
|--------|------|------|---------------|
| $\alpha$ | Learning rate | Step size for the update; controls how aggressively new information overrides old | **0.0 ŌĆō 1.0** |
| $\gamma$ | Discount factor | Determines present value of future rewards; closer to 1 = far-sighted | **0.0 ŌĆō 0.99** |
| $\varepsilon$ | Exploration rate | Probability of choosing a random action (╬Ą-greedy) | **0.0 ŌĆō 1.0** (decayed) |

## 2.3 Why Each Term Exists ŌĆö The "Why" Behind the Math

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why Box:**
> - The term $\max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a')$ makes Q-Learning **off-policy** ŌĆö it assumes the agent will *act optimally* in the next state, even if it is currently exploring. This is the *optimism in the face of uncertainty* principle.
> - The difference $r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$ is the **TD-error** $\delta_t$, the innovation signal that drives learning.
> - The Bellman operator $T^*$ is a **contraction mapping** under the sup-norm, guaranteeing a unique fixed point ŌĆö the very reason Q-Learning converges.

## 2.4 Convergence Theorem (Watkins & Dayan, 1992)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite Theorem:** Q-Learning converges to $Q^*$ with probability 1 if:
> 1. All state-action pairs are visited **infinitely often** (infinite exploration),
> 2. The learning rate satisfies $\sum_t \alpha_t(s, a) = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^2(s, a) < \infty$ (Robbins-Monro conditions),
> 3. Rewards are bounded: $\vert r \vert \leq R_{\max}$.

## 2.5 Exploration vs. Exploitation Strategies

| Strategy | Formula | Behavior |
|----------|---------|----------|
| **╬Ą-greedy** | $a = \text{random}$ w.p. $\varepsilon$, else $\arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ | Simple, widely used |
| **Decaying ╬Ą-greedy** | $\varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_0 \cdot d^t$ | Encourages early exploration, late exploitation |
| **Boltzmann (Softmax)** | $P(a \vert s) = \frac{e^{Q(s,a)/\tau}}{\sum_{a'} e^{Q(s,a')/\tau}}$ | Probabilistic, temperature $\tau$ controls randomness |
| **UCB** | $a = \arg\max_a \left[ Q(s,a) + c\sqrt{\tfrac{\ln t}{N(s,a)}} \right]$ | Optimism under uncertainty |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | LaTeX | Engineering Use |
|---------|-------|------------------|
| Q-Update | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \big[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \big]$ | Core learning step |
| TD-Error | $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$ | Drives every update |
| Target | $y_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a')$ | Supervised-learning label |
| ╬Ą-greedy Action | $a_t = \begin{cases} \text{random} & \text{w.p. } \varepsilon \\ \arg\max_a Q(s_t,a) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Action selection |
| Bellman Optimality | $Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(S_{t+1},a') \big\vert S_t=s, A_t=a\big]$ | Defines optimum |
| Discounted Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Objective to maximize |
| ╬▒-decay (Robbins-Monro) | $\alpha_t = \tfrac{1}{1 + \text{visits}(s,a)/C}$ | Guarantees convergence |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In an HVAC controller, the Q-table maps `(room_temp, occupancy)` ŌåÆ `(fan_speed, valve_opening)`, and the Q-Learning update tunes the controller online without needing a thermodynamic model of the building.

# ­¤¦« SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 ŌĆö From Bellman Optimality to the Q-Learning Update

### Step 1: Start with the Bellman Optimality Equation

By definition, the optimal action-value function satisfies the **Bellman Optimality Equation**:

$$Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(S_{t+1}, a') \,\bigg|\, S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$

### Step 2: Drop the Expectation (Sample-based Approximation)

In a model-free setting we do not know the transition probabilities $P(s' \vert s, a)$. We therefore replace the expectation with a **single-sample realization**:

$$Q^*(s, a) \;\approx\; r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$$

This is justified because the **law of large numbers** guarantees that averaging over many samples recovers the expectation.

### Step 3: Recognize this as a Fixed-Point Equation

Treating the right-hand side as an operator $\mathcal{T}$ applied to $Q^*$, we seek a fixed point:

$$Q^* = \mathcal{T} Q^* \quad \text{where} \quad \mathcal{T}Q(s,a) = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$$

### Step 4: Stochastic Iterative Update (Robbins-Monro Style)

To find the fixed point, perform a **stochastic gradient step** on the squared TD-error:

$$\mathcal{L}(s, a) = \tfrac{1}{2} \big( r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \big)^2$$

### Step 5: Compute the Gradient

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Q(s, a)} = -\big( r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \big)$$

### Step 6: Apply Gradient Descent Update

Using $Q \leftarrow Q - \alpha \nabla \mathcal{L}$:

$$Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha \big( r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \big)$$

### Step 7: Final Canonical Q-Learning Update

$$\boxed{\,Q(s, a) \;\leftarrow\; Q(s, a) \;+\; \alpha \Big[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \Big]\,}$$

This is the **Watkins Q-Learning update rule**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 ŌĆö The TD-Error

### Step 1: Define the Bootstrapped Target

The **TD-target** is the one-step lookahead estimate of the return from state $s'$:

$$y_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a')$$

### Step 2: Define the TD-Error

The discrepancy between the current estimate and the target is:

$$\delta_t = y_t - Q(s_t, a_t)$$

### Step 3: Substitute

$$\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$$

### Step 4: Recognize the Update as Error Correction

The Q-Learning update can be compactly written as:

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \, \delta_t$$

Hence, Q-Learning is **literally** gradient descent on the squared TD-error $\delta_t^2$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 ŌĆö Convergence via Contraction Mapping

### Step 1: Define the Bellman Optimality Operator

$$(\mathcal{T}Q)(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{s'}\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') \right]$$

### Step 2: Show Contraction Under Sup-Norm

For any two Q-functions $Q_1, Q_2$:

$$|(\mathcal{T}Q_1)(s, a) - (\mathcal{T}Q_2)(s, a)| = \gamma \big\vert \max_{a'} Q_1(s', a') - \max_{a'} Q_2(s', a') \big\vert$$

Using the Lipschitz property of the max function:

$$\big\vert \max_x f(x) - \max_x g(x) \big\vert \leq \max_x \vert f(x) - g(x) \vert$$

Therefore:

$$\|\mathcal{T}Q_1 - \mathcal{T}Q_2\|_\infty \leq \gamma \, \|Q_1 - Q_2\|_\infty$$

### Step 3: Apply Banach Fixed-Point Theorem

Since $0 \leq \gamma < 1$, the operator $\mathcal{T}$ is a **╬│-contraction**. By the **Banach Fixed-Point Theorem**, repeated application of $\mathcal{T}$ converges to a **unique fixed point** $Q^*$.

### Step 4: Implication

Because Q-Learning performs a **stochastic approximation** of $\mathcal{T}$, and the Robbins-Monro conditions are satisfied, the iterate $Q_t \to Q^*$ with probability 1.

$$\boxed{\,\lim_{t \to \infty} Q_t(s, a) = Q^*(s, a) \quad \text{w.p. } 1\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö The 3-State Grid

Consider a 1-D corridor with three states $s_1, s_2, s_3$. Actions: `L` (left) or `R` (right). Reaching $s_3$ yields reward $+10$, $s_1$ yields $+1$, all other transitions give $0$. Discount $\gamma = 0.9$, learning rate $\alpha = 0.5$. All Q-values initialized to $0$.

**Trajectory 1:** $(s_1, R) \to s_2, r = 0$

Target: $y = 0 + 0.9 \cdot \max(Q(s_2, L), Q(s_2, R)) = 0$

Update: $Q(s_1, R) = 0 + 0.5 (0 - 0) = 0$

**Trajectory 2:** $(s_2, R) \to s_3, r = 10$

Target: $y = 10 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 10$

Update: $Q(s_2, R) = 0 + 0.5 (10 - 0) = 5$

**Trajectory 3:** $(s_1, R) \to s_2, r = 0$

Target: $y = 0 + 0.9 \cdot \max(Q(s_2,L), Q(s_2,R)) = 0.9 \cdot 5 = 4.5$

Update: $Q(s_1, R) = 0 + 0.5 (4.5 - 0) = 2.25$

After many iterations, $Q$ converges such that the optimal policy is **always go right**: $\pi^*(s_1) = \pi^*(s_2) = R$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Q-Learning vs. SARSA

| Aspect | **Q-Learning** | **SARSA** |
|--------|----------------|-----------|
| Policy Type | **Off-policy** | **On-policy** |
| Target | $r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ | $r + \gamma Q(s', a')$ where $a'$ is the *actually taken* action |
| Update Rule | Uses **max** | Uses **sampled** next action |
| Optimality | Learns $Q^*$ directly | Learns $Q^\pi$ for the current policy |
| Risk in Risky Environments | Tends to be **more aggressive** | Tends to be **more conservative** |
| Convergence | To optimal $Q^*$ | To near-optimal under continued exploration |

## 4.1 The Q-Learning Interaction Loop (AgentŌĆōEnvironment)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START Initialize Q-Table to Zero]) --> B[Observe current state s_t]
    B --> C{Choose Action via epsilon greedy}
    C -- random w.p. epsilon --> D[Action a_t random]
    C -- greedy w.p. 1 minus epsilon --> E[Action a_t = argmax Q s a]
    D --> F[Execute a_t in Environment]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Receive Reward r t plus 1]
    G --> H[Observe Next State s t plus 1]
    H --> I[Compute TD Target y = r + gamma max Q s' a']
    I --> J[Compute TD Error delta = y - Q s_t a_t]
    J --> K[Update Q s_t a_t = Q s_t a_t + alpha times delta]
    K --> L{Is episode done}
    L -- No --> M[Set s_t = s t plus 1]
    M --> B
    L -- Yes --> N[Decay epsilon and alpha]
    N --> O([END Episode])
```

# ­¤Ä» SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## ­¤ōØ Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define Q-Learning. State its update rule and explain the significance of the term $\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$.**

**Model Answer:**

Q-Learning is a model-free, off-policy temporal-difference reinforcement learning algorithm proposed by Watkins (1989) that learns the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s, a)$ directly from experience tuples $(s, a, r, s')$ without requiring a model of the environment.

The canonical update rule is:

$$Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha \big[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \big]$$

**Significance of $\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$:** This term makes the algorithm **off-policy** because it computes the value of the next state as if the agent were already following the **optimal policy**, regardless of the action actually taken. This enables the agent to learn the optimum even while exploring randomly, accelerating convergence to $Q^*$.

*(3 marks ŌĆö Expected: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the rule, 1 mark for explanation of max term.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between on-policy and off-policy learning. Classify Q-Learning.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | On-Policy | Off-Policy |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| Definition | Learns about the policy being executed | Learns about a policy different from the one being executed |
| Data Source | Samples from the same (current) policy | Samples may come from any exploratory policy |
| Target | $r + \gamma Q(s', a')$ (sampled next action) | $r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ (greedy next action) |
| Variance | Lower | Higher |
| Example | **SARSA** | **Q-Learning** |

**Classification:** Q-Learning is **off-policy** because it uses the **max** operator over next-state Q-values rather than the value of the action actually taken by the behavior policy.

*(3 marks ŌĆö Expected: 1 mark per row of distinction + 1 mark for Q-Learning classification with justification.)*

---

## ­¤ōÜ Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō July 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive the Q-Learning update rule starting from the Bellman optimality equation. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 4-state chain with $S = \{s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4\}$ where $s_4$ is a terminal goal state giving $r = +20$, all other transitions give $r = 0$. Use $\gamma = 0.9$, $\alpha = 0.5$, and the Q-Table initialized to zero. Perform **two complete updates** for the trajectory:

$$s_1 \xrightarrow{a_1} s_2 \xrightarrow{a_2} s_3 \xrightarrow{a_3} s_4$$

Show the evolution of the relevant Q-values. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ­¤ō¢ Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Bellman Optimality Equation** *(1 mark)*

$$Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(S_{t+1}, a') \;\big|\; S_t = s, A_t = a \big]$$

**Step 2: Sample-based Approximation** *(1 mark)*

Since we do not know the transition model $P$, we replace the expectation with a single sample $(r, s')$:

$$Q^*(s, a) \approx r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$$

**Step 3: Loss Function Definition** *(1 mark)*

Define the squared TD-error loss to be minimized:

$$\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{2} \big( r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \big)^2$$

**Step 4: Gradient Computation** *(1 mark)*

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Q(s, a)} = -\big( r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \big)$$

**Step 5: Update via Gradient Descent** *(1 mark)*

$$Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) - \alpha \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Q(s, a)} = Q(s, a) + \alpha \big[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \big]$$

**Step 6: State Assumptions** *(1 mark)*
- All state-action pairs are visited infinitely often.
- Robbins-Monro conditions on $\alpha_t$ hold.
- Rewards are bounded: $\vert r \vert \leq R_{\max}$.

**Step 7: Convergence Conclusion** *(1 mark)*

By the Banach Fixed-Point Theorem applied to the Bellman operator (which is a $\gamma$-contraction in sup-norm), repeated application converges to the unique $Q^*$.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Updates (7 marks):**

**Initialize:** All $Q(s, a) = 0$; $\gamma = 0.9$; $\alpha = 0.5$.

**Update 1** at $(s_1, a_1) \to s_2$ with $r = 0$:

Target: $y_1 = 0 + 0.9 \cdot \max(Q(s_2, L), Q(s_2, R)) = 0.9 \cdot 0 = 0$

Update: $Q(s_1, a_1) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot (0 - 0) = 0$

**Update 2** at $(s_2, a_2) \to s_3$ with $r = 0$:

Target: $y_2 = 0 + 0.9 \cdot \max(Q(s_3, L), Q(s_3, R)) = 0$

Update: $Q(s_2, a_2) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 0$

**Update 3** at $(s_3, a_3) \to s_4$ with $r = 20$:

Target: $y_3 = 20 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 20$

Update: $Q(s_3, a_3) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot (20 - 0) = 10$

**Back-propagation after Episode End** (optional follow-up):

- $Q(s_2, a_2) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 (0 + 0.9 \cdot 10 - 0) = 4.5$
- $Q(s_1, a_1) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 (0 + 0.9 \cdot 4.5 - 0) = 2.025$

**Final Q-values after two episodes of this chain:**

| State-Action | Q-Value |
|--------------|---------|
| $Q(s_1, a_1)$ | $\approx 2.025$ |
| $Q(s_2, a_2)$ | $\approx 4.5$ |
| $Q(s_3, a_3)$ | $= 10.0$ |

The Q-values correctly **propagate reward backward** from the goal state, demonstrating the **temporal credit assignment** property of Q-Learning. *(Full 7 marks: 1 mark per major computation step + 1 mark for tabulation + 1 mark for interpretation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the role of the **exploration parameter $\varepsilon$** in Q-Learning. Compare the ╬Ą-greedy and Boltzmann exploration strategies, giving the explicit mathematical form of each. Discuss why pure greedy selection can fail to converge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot must navigate a 2├Ś2 grid world. State space: $\{A, B, C, D\}$, Actions: $\{N, S, E, W\}$. Rewards: reaching $D$ gives $+10$, all other transitions give $-1$, and hitting walls keeps the robot in place. Use $\gamma = 0.95$, $\alpha = 0.2$, $\varepsilon = 0.1$. Starting at $A$, the robot executes the trajectory:

$$A \xrightarrow{E} B \xrightarrow{E} C \xrightarrow{S} C \;(\text{wall}) \xrightarrow{E} D$$

Perform the Q-Learning updates for **every transition** and state the optimal policy after one full episode. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ­¤ō¢ Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) ŌĆö Exploration Strategies (7 marks):**

**Role of $\varepsilon$** *(2 marks)*: The exploration parameter $\varepsilon$ controls the **explorationŌĆōexploitation tradeoff**. With probability $\varepsilon$ the agent picks a random action (exploration), and with probability $1 - \varepsilon$ it picks the current best action (exploitation). This is essential to gather information about under-visited state-action pairs.

**╬Ą-greedy** *(1 mark)*:

$$a_t = \begin{cases} \text{Uniformly random } a \in \mathcal{A} & \text{with probability } \varepsilon \\ \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} Q(s_t, a) & \text{with probability } 1 - \varepsilon \end{cases}$$

**Boltzmann (Softmax)** *(1 mark)*:

$$P(a \vert s) = \frac{\exp\big( Q(s, a) / \tau \big)}{\sum_{a' \in \mathcal{A}} \exp\big( Q(s, a') / \tau \big)}$$

where $\tau$ is the **temperature**. As $\tau \to 0$, behavior becomes greedy; as $\tau \to \infty$, behavior is uniform random.

**Comparison** *(1 mark)*: ╬Ą-greedy treats all non-greedy actions equally; Boltzmann weights them by their Q-values, giving more frequent selection to "promising" actions.

**Why pure greedy fails** *(2 marks)*: With pure greedy action selection ($\varepsilon = 0$), the agent never explores unseen actions. State-action pairs with low initial Q-values are never updated. The agent can thus converge to a **suboptimal policy** because the Bellman backup is never applied to the truly best action. This is the **"greedy trap."**

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Grid World Updates (7 marks):**

**Initial state:** All $Q(s, a) = 0$. Hyperparameters: $\gamma = 0.95$, $\alpha = 0.2$.

Assume optimal-action reference (taking $\varepsilon$-greedy resolves ties arbitrarily).

**Update 1:** $A \xrightarrow{E} B$, $r = -1$

$$Q(A, E) \leftarrow 0 + 0.2 \big[ -1 + 0.95 \cdot \max_a Q(B, a) - 0 \big] = 0 + 0.2(-1) = -0.2$$

**Update 2:** $B \xrightarrow{E} C$, $r = -1$

$$Q(B, E) \leftarrow 0 + 0.2 \big[ -1 + 0.95 \cdot \max_a Q(C, a) \big] = 0.2(-1) = -0.2$$

**Update 3:** $C \xrightarrow{S} C$ (wall, $r = -1$)

$$Q(C, S) \leftarrow 0 + 0.2 \big[ -1 + 0.95 \cdot \max_a Q(C, a) \big] = -0.2$$

**Update 4:** $C \xrightarrow{E} D$ (goal, $r = +10$)

$$Q(C, E) \leftarrow 0 + 0.2 \big[ 10 + 0.95 \cdot 0 \big] = 0.2 \times 10 = 2.0$$

**Back-propagation** (apply updates in reverse for temporal consistency):

$$Q(B, E) \leftarrow -0.2 + 0.2 \big[ -1 + 0.95 \cdot 2.0 \big] = -0.2 + 0.2(0.9) = -0.2 + 0.18 = -0.02$$

$$Q(A, E) \leftarrow -0.2 + 0.2 \big[ -1 + 0.95 \cdot (-0.02) \big] = -0.2 + 0.2(-1.019) = -0.4038$$

**Q-Table after one episode:**

| State | N | S | E | W |
|-------|-----|-----|------|-----|
| A | 0 | 0 | $-0.404$ | 0 |
| B | 0 | 0 | $-0.02$ | 0 |
| C | 0 | $-0.2$ | $\mathbf{2.0}$ | 0 |
| D | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Optimal policy after one episode:** $\pi^*(C) = E$ (highest $Q$), with the agent learning to move **East** through $A \to B \to C \to D$.

---

## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SARSA and Q-Learning targets.** Writing $r + \gamma Q(s', a')$ for Q-Learning instead of $r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ is an **instant 1-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting the "max" operator.** This is the *defining* characteristic of Q-Learning.
> 3. **Skipping the Bellman optimality step in derivations.** Examiners award a separate mark for **stating the Bellman equation explicitly** before deriving.
> 4. **Not mentioning Robbins-Monro conditions** when asked about convergence. This is a frequent **2-mark trap**.
> 5. **Off-policy / On-policy confusion** in MCQ-style 3-markers ŌĆö always justify with the *target* operator.
> 6. **Numerical questions:** Students forget to **initialize Q(s,a) = 0** and to show **all intermediate targets** $y_t$, losing full process marks.
