> **ŌÜĪ High-Yield Quick-Recall ŌĆö Q-Learning**

- Ō£ģ Q-Learning is **model-free, value-based, off-policy**.
- Ō£ģ Update rule: $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$.
- Ō£ģ The **max** operator is what makes it **off-policy**.
- Ō£ģ **TD-error** $\delta_t = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)$ ŌĆö the engine of learning.
- Ō£ģ **TD-target** $y_t = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a')$ ŌĆö the bootstrapped label.
- Ō£ģ **╬Ą-greedy** is the most common exploration strategy; pure greedy can fail.
- Ō£ģ **Boltzmann/Softmax** uses temperature $\tau$ to weight actions probabilistically.
- Ō£ģ **Convergence** requires (i) infinite exploration, (ii) Robbins-Monro $\alpha_t$, (iii) bounded rewards.
- Ō£ģ Bellman optimality operator is a **╬│-contraction** in sup-norm $\Rightarrow$ unique fixed point.
- Ō£ģ Compared to **SARSA**, Q-Learning is more **aggressive** and learns the **optimal** $Q^*$ directly.
- Ō£ģ Q-Learning is the conceptual ancestor of **Deep Q-Networks (DQN)**.
- Ō£ģ Engineering applications: HVAC, robotics, autonomous driving, recommender systems, game AI.
- Ō£ģ KTU expects students to **derive the update rule from Bellman optimality** in 7-mark questions.
- Ō£ģ In numerical problems, always **show the TD-target, the error, and the updated Q-value** in three separate lines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SARSA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT868 - INTRODUCTION TO REINFORCEMENT LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** SARSA # ­¤ōś Module 3 ŌĆö SARSA (StateŌĆōActionŌĆōRewardŌĆōStateŌĆōAction) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > **SARSA** is an **on-policy Temporal Difference (TD) control algorithm** that learns the action-value function $Q^\pi(s,a)$ for the policy $\pi$ currently being followed by the agent. It updates the Q-value using the actual action $a'$ that the agent takes in the next state $s'$, under the same policy $\pi$. Hence the name **SŌĆōAŌĆōRŌĆōSŌĆōA** (State, Action, Reward, next State, next Action). ### ­¤öæ The SARSA Quintuple At every step the agent observes five elements: - $S$ ŌĆö current state - $A$ ŌĆö current action taken - $R$ ŌĆö reward received after performing $A$ in $S$ - $S'$ ŌĆö next state observed - $A'$ ŌĆö next action chosen (from the *same* policy $\pi$) ### ­¤¦Ā Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Honest Student Driver" Imagine a **learner driver** who only learns from the **actual road trip he just completed** (not from imaginary perfect drives). After every turn, he asks: - "Where was I?" (S) - "What did I do?" (A) - "What happened (good/bad)?" (R) - "Where am I now?" (SŌĆ▓) - "What am I about to do next, following my current cautious style?" (AŌĆ▓) He then **adjusts** his judgment about action $A$ in state $S$ using the **real upcoming action** $A'$, not the *best possible* action. That is exactly what SARSA does ŌĆö it learns *on the job*, using the policy it actually follows. > [!NOTE] > **On-Policy vs Off-Policy (Syllabus Highlight):** > - **SARSA ŌåÆ On-Policy** (learns value of the *behaviour* policy $\pi$) > - **Q-Learning ŌåÆ Off-Policy** (learns value of the *greedy/target* policy regardless of exploration) > > This is the most-tested distinction in KTU exams on this topic. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### ­¤ö¼ Operational Breakdown of the SARSA Algorithm 1. **Initialize** $Q(s,a)$ arbitrarily for all $s \in \mathcal{S}, a \in \mathcal{A}(s)$. 2. **Loop for each episode**: - Initialize starting state $S$. - **Choose action** $A$ from $S$ using a policy derived from $Q$ (e.g., $\varepsilon$-greedy). - **Loop for each step of episode**: - Take action $A$, observe $R$ and $S'$. - **Choose next action** $A'$ from $S'$ using the same $\varepsilon$-greedy policy. - **Update Q-value**: $$Q(S,A) \leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha \left[ R + \gamma Q(S',A') - Q(S,A) \right]$$ - Set $S \leftarrow S'$, $A \leftarrow A'$. - Until $S$ is terminal. 3. **Output:** the optimal (or near-optimal) policy $\pi$ and action-value function $Q$. ### ­¤Ä» Why "On-Policy" Matters Because the next action $A'$ is sampled from the **current behaviour policy** (which may include exploration), SARSA naturally **penalises risky exploratory moves**. In a cliff-walking environment, for instance, SARSA learns a **safer sub-optimal path** away from the cliff, while Q-Learning learns the **risky optimal path** along the cliff edge (a famous Sutton & Barto example). ### ­¤ōÉ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes / Units | |---|---------|----------------|----------------| | 1 | SARSA Update Rule | $Q(S,A) \leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha \big[ R + \gamma Q(S',A') - Q(S,A) \big]$ | $\alpha$ = learning rate, $\gamma$ = discount | | 2 | TD Target (SARSA) | $R + \gamma Q(S',A')$ | Uses **actual** next action $A'$ | | 3 | TD Error (SARSA) | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1},A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t,A_t)$ | Quantity the update shifts | | 4 | Q-Learning Update (contrast) | $Q(S,A) \leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha\big[R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S',a') - Q(S,A)\big]$ | Off-policy variant | | 5 | TD(0) Learning Rate Condition | $0 < \alpha < 1$, must satisfy **RobbinsŌĆōMonro**: $\sum \alpha_t = \infty,\ \sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ | Guarantees convergence | | 6 | Discount Factor | $0 \le \gamma \le 1$ | $\gamma=0$ ŌåÆ myopic; $\gamma\to 1$ ŌåÆ far-sighted | | 7 | $\varepsilon$-greedy action selection | $P(a^*) = 1-\varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}\vert};\ P(a\neq a^*) = \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}\vert}$ | $\varepsilon$ typically decayed over time | | 8 | State-Value via $Q$ | $V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s)\, Q^\pi(s,a)$ | Connection between $Q$ and $V$ | | 9 | Bellman Optimality (target) | $Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\big[R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s_{t+1},a') \big]$ | SARSA converges to $Q^\pi$, not $Q^*$ | | 10 | Convergence of SARSA | Converges w.p. 1 to $Q^\pi$ if all $(s,a)$ visited infinitely often, **GLIE** ($\varepsilon$-greedy with $\varepsilon \to 0$) and RobbinsŌĆōMonro step-size | Theorem ŌĆö KTU frequently asked | > [!IMPORTANT] > **GLIE Condition (Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration):** Required for SARSA to converge to the optimal policy. Both **exploration** (visit all pairs) and **eventual greedy behaviour** ($\varepsilon_k \to 0$) must hold. ### ­¤ÅŚ’ĖÅ Real-World Engineering Utility - **Robotics:** Safe navigation where exploratory crashes are costly ŌĆö SARSA's conservative updates suit real hardware. - **Autonomous Driving:** Trajectory planning under safety constraints. - **Industrial Process Control:** On-policy learning respects current operational limits. - **Game AI:** Game-play agents that mimic the *style* of play they are currently using. - **Network Routing & Resource Scheduling:** TD-style updates are computationally cheap and online. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### ­¤öó Derivation 1 ŌĆö From Bellman Equation to SARSA Update The Bellman equation for the action-value under a fixed policy $\pi$ is: $$ Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma Q^\pi(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \big] $$ **Derivation walkthrough:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q^\pi(s,a) &= \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma Q^\pi(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \big] && \text{(Bellman expectation for action-value)} \\[4pt] Q^\pi(s,a) &\approx r + \gamma Q^\pi(s',a') && \text{(single-sample estimate using the observed tuple } (s,a,r,s',a')) \\[4pt] \text{TD target: } \hat{y} &= r + \gamma Q(s',a') && \text{(plug the sample into the recursive form)} \\[4pt] \text{TD error: } \delta &= \hat{y} - Q(s,a) = r + \gamma Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) && \text{(prediction minus target)} \\[4pt] Q(S,A) &\leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha\,\delta && \text{(stochastic gradient step toward the Bellman target)} \\[4pt] \therefore\ Q(S,A) &\leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha \big[ R + \gamma Q(S',A') - Q(S,A) \big] && \text{(final SARSA update rule)} \end{aligned} $$ > The crucial difference from Q-Learning is the term $Q(S',A')$: SARSA uses the **specific** action the agent is about to take (on-policy), whereas Q-Learning replaces it with $\max_{a'} Q(S',a')$. ### ­¤öó Derivation 2 ŌĆö Convergence Criterion (Sketch) For the mean of the TD error to vanish in the limit: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}[\delta_t \mid S_t=s, A_t=a] &= 0 \quad \text{at convergence} \\[2pt] \mathbb{E}\big[ R + \gamma Q^\pi(S',A') - Q^\pi(s,a) \big] &= 0 \\[2pt] Q^\pi(s,a) &= \mathbb{E}\big[ R + \gamma Q^\pi(S',A') \big] = \mathcal{T}^\pi Q^\pi(s,a) \end{aligned} $$ Thus a fixed point of the **policy-specific Bellman operator** $\mathcal{T}^\pi$ is reached ŌĆö this is exactly the quantity SARSA converges to. ### ­¤ōŖ Analytical Comparison Matrix (SARSA vs Q-Learning) | Aspect | **SARSA** | **Q-Learning** | |--------|-----------|----------------| | Type | On-policy | Off-policy | | TD target | $r + \gamma Q(s',a')$ (sample) | $r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a')$ | | Learns | $Q^\pi$ of behaviour policy | $Q^*$ of optimal policy | | Convergence requirement | GLIE + RobbinsŌĆōMonro | RobbinsŌĆōMonro + sufficient exploration | | Behaviour under exploration | Conservative (penalises risky exploration) | Optimistic (uses max, ignores exploration cost) | | Cliff-walking result | Safe path away from cliff | Optimal path along cliff (risky) | | Update uses 5-tuple | Yes ŌĆö $(S,A,R,S',A')$ | No ŌĆö only $(S,A,R,S')$ | | Common KTU exam trap | Confusing $a'$ (actual) with $\max_{a'}$ | Confusing $a'$ (actual) with $\max_{a'}$ | ### ­¤¦« Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 3-mark variant) A 3-state MDP, $\gamma = 0.9$, $\alpha = 0.5$, current Q-table all zeros. Agent in $S_1$ takes $A_1$, gets $R = +1$, reaches $S_2$. From $S_2$, $\varepsilon$-greedy (with $\varepsilon = 0$, fully greedy) selects $A_2$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TD target} &= R + \gamma Q(S_2, A_2) = 1 + 0.9 \times 0 = 1 \\[4pt] \text{TD error } \delta &= 1 - Q(S_1,A_1) = 1 - 0 = 1 \\[4pt] Q(S_1, A_1) &\leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \times 1 = 0.5 \\[4pt] \therefore\ Q(S_1, A_1) &= 0.5 \end{aligned} $$ > Note: if it had been Q-Learning with $A_2$ being the greedy choice, the formula would look identical here. The divergence appears only when the **policy explores** and a *non-greedy* $A'$ would be chosen ŌĆö SARSA honours that exploration; Q-Learning would still use the max. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### ­¤Ś║’ĖÅ Mermaid Diagram 1 ŌĆö SARSA AgentŌĆōEnvironment Interaction Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR start([Start Episode]) --> initS[Initialize State S] initS --> pickA[Choose Action A from S
using eps greedy policy pi] pickA --> envStep[Take Action A in Environment] envStep --> obsR[Observe Reward R] obsR --> obsS2[Observe Next State S prime] obsS2 --> pickA2[Choose Next Action A prime from S prime
using SAME policy pi] pickA2 --> updateQ[Update Q S A
Q + alpha R + gamma Q S prime A prime - Q S A] updateQ --> assignS[S leftarrow S prime] assignS --> assignA[A leftarrow A prime] assignA --> checkEnd{Is S terminal?} checkEnd -- No --> pickA checkEnd -- Yes --> endEp([End Episode]) ``` ### ­¤Ś║’ĖÅ Mermaid Diagram 2 ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture of SARSA ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Agent["AGENT"] policy[Policy pi
eps greedy] qtable[Q Table Q S A] learner[TD Learner
Update Engine] end subgraph Env["ENVIRONMENT"] s[State S] trans[Dynamics P s prime r s a] end s -->|perceived by| policy policy -->|action A| trans trans -->|reward R| learner trans -->|next state S prime| learner s -->|next state S prime| policy policy -->|next action A prime| learner qtable -->|read weights| policy qtable -->|update values| learner learner -->|write Q S A| qtable ``` ### ­¤Ś║’ĖÅ Mermaid Diagram 3 ŌĆö SARSA vs Q-Learning Decision Path ```mermaid flowchart TB s[Current State S] --> a[Take Action A] a --> r[Receive Reward R] r --> sp[Observe Next State S prime] sp --> branch{Algorithm} branch -- SARSA --> actual[Use Actual Next Action A prime
sampled from policy pi] branch -- Q Learn --> maxim[Use Max over all next actions
max a prime Q S prime a prime] actual --> tdsarsa[TD target R + gamma Q S prime A prime] maxim --> tdq[TD target R + gamma max Q S prime] tdsarsa --> upd1[Update Q S A] tdq --> upd2[Update Q S A] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why the architecture matters in KTU viva:** Examiners often ask students to label the *flow of the quintuple* $(S,A,R,S',A')$. Memorising the loop above and being able to point to where each of the five elements enters the update is worth easy marks. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### ­¤ōØ Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, Module 3]** *Define the SARSA algorithm. Why is it called an on-policy TD control method?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** SARSA is an on-policy Temporal-Difference control algorithm that learns the action-value function $Q^\pi(s,a)$ of the policy $\pi$ currently being executed by the agent. It updates $Q$ using the observed transition tuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ ŌĆö hence the name. - **Update rule (1 mark):** $$Q(S,A) \leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha \big[ R + \gamma Q(S',A') - Q(S,A) \big]$$ - **On-policy justification (1 mark):** Both the *behaviour* action $A$ and the *target* action $A'$ are drawn from the **same** policy $\pi$ (e.g., $\varepsilon$-greedy derived from the current $Q$). The value learnt is $Q^\pi$, the value of the policy actually followed, not the optimal action-value $Q^*$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, Module 3]** *Differentiate between SARSA and Q-Learning in terms of their TD targets and policy nature.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Policy nature (1 mark):** SARSA is **on-policy** (learns $Q^\pi$ of the behaviour policy); Q-Learning is **off-policy** (learns $Q^*$ independent of exploration). - **TD target (1 mark):** - SARSA: $R + \gamma Q(S', A')$ where $A'$ is the **actual** next action sampled from $\pi$. - Q-Learning: $R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S', a')$ ŌĆö greedy over next actions. - **Consequence (1 mark):** SARSA converges to a value consistent with exploration (e.g., safer paths in cliff-walking); Q-Learning converges to the optimal value but, during learning, may take riskier exploratory actions because the max-operator ignores exploration costs. --- ### ­¤ōÜ Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **ESE Pattern:** Module-3 long answer, sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. --- #### **Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024 Style]` **(a)** With the help of the SARSA update rule, explain how the algorithm uses the quintuple $(S,A,R,S',A')$ to learn the action-value function. State clearly the role of the **learning rate** $\alpha$ and **discount factor** $\gamma$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Suppose an agent uses an $\varepsilon$-greedy policy with $\varepsilon = 0.1$, $\alpha = 0.2$, $\gamma = 0.9$, and an initial Q-table of all zeros. Consider the following single transition: - From state $S_1$ the agent takes action $A_1$ (a non-greedy exploratory action), receives reward $R = -1$, and reaches $S_2$. - From $S_2$ the $\varepsilon$-greedy policy (over $Q=0$) selects action $A_2$ randomly among two equally-tied actions. Compute the SARSA update. What value of $Q(S_1,A_1)$ would you obtain if the same transition were processed by **Q-Learning**? Comment on the difference. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) ŌĆö 7 marks** - **(2 marks) Quintuple flow:** Describe that at each step the agent records $(S,A)$, executes $A$, receives $R$ and next state $S'$, then ŌĆö *critically for SARSA* ŌĆö also chooses the next action $A'$ **using the same policy** before performing the update. This quintuple is the *complete* information needed. - **(2 marks) Update rule with explanation:** $$Q(S,A) \leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha\big[ R + \gamma Q(S',A') - Q(S,A) \big]$$ - $\alpha \in (0,1)$ ŌĆö **learning rate** controlling how strongly each new sample overrides the old estimate; must satisfy RobbinsŌĆōMonro conditions for convergence. - $\gamma \in [0,1)$ ŌĆö **discount factor** weighing future rewards; smaller $\gamma$ ŌćÆ more myopic. - **(2 marks) On-policy emphasis + bootstrap:** $Q(S',A')$ is a **bootstrap** estimate (an estimate of an estimate), and because $A'$ is sampled from the *current* $\varepsilon$-greedy policy, the update reflects the value of the *actual* policy being executed. - **(1 mark) Convergence note:** Under GLIE and RobbinsŌĆōMonro, SARSA converges to $Q^\pi$ with probability 1. **(b) ŌĆö 7 marks** SARSA computation (5 marks): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Initial } Q(S_1,A_1) &= 0,\quad Q(S_2,A_2) = 0 \\ \text{TD target (SARSA)} &= R + \gamma Q(S_2,A_2) = -1 + 0.9 \times 0 = -1 \\[4pt] \text{TD error } \delta &= -1 - 0 = -1 \\[4pt] Q(S_1,A_1) &\leftarrow 0 + 0.2 \times (-1) = -0.2 \end{aligned} $$ Q-Learning computation (1 mark): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TD target (Q-Learning)} &= R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_2,a') = -1 + 0.9 \times 0 = -1 \\ Q(S_1,A_1) &\leftarrow 0 + 0.2 \times (-1) = -0.2 \end{aligned} $$ In this case, the **numerical result is the same** because $Q$-values are all zero. The *qualitative* difference emerges in subsequent steps: had $Q$ not been zero and a *better* action existed in $S_2$, Q-Learning would have used the max (ignoring the actual $A_2$ chosen under $\varepsilon$-greedy) while SARSA would use the sampled $A_2$. **(1 mark for the comment).** --- #### **Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Style]` **(a)** Explain the **GLIE (Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration)** conditions. Why are they essential for the convergence of SARSA? Mention the RobbinsŌĆōMonro step-size conditions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a 2-action MDP with states $\{S_1, S_2\}$, $\gamma = 0.8$, $\alpha = 0.5$, and initial Q-values $Q(S_1,A_1) = 0.4$, $Q(S_1,A_2) = 0.6$, $Q(S_2,A_1) = 0.2$, $Q(S_2,A_2) = 0.5$. The agent in $S_1$ is following an $\varepsilon$-greedy policy with $\varepsilon = 0.2$ ($\vert\mathcal{A}\vert = 2$). It selects action $A_1$ (the non-greedy one), receives $R = +2$, and reaches $S_2$. From $S_2$, the $\varepsilon$-greedy policy (greedy action is $A_2$) is *also* applied **after** the update. Compute: - (i) The probability of choosing $A_1$ in $S_1$ and the action actually chosen in $S_2$. - (ii) The SARSA update for $Q(S_1, A_1)$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) ŌĆö 7 marks** - **GLIE Part 1 ŌĆö Infinite Exploration (2 marks):** Every state-action pair $(s,a)$ must be visited infinitely often as the number of episodes $\to \infty$. This ensures no pair is starved of samples and the algorithm sees the full MDP. - **GLIE Part 2 ŌĆö Greedy in the Limit (2 marks):** The exploration parameter $\varepsilon_k \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$, so the policy eventually becomes greedy and the agent behaves optimally. - **Why essential (1 mark):** GLIE guarantees the **operates-on-policy estimate** of the value function converges to the *true* value of the policy, and that the policy itself improves towards optimality (via the policy-improvement theorem). - **RobbinsŌĆōMonro step-size (2 marks):** $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \alpha_k(s,a) = \infty$ and $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \alpha_k^2(s,a) < \infty$. The first ensures the algorithm can reach any target; the second ensures the noise of stochastic updates decays. **(b) ŌĆö 7 marks** **(i) Action-selection probabilities (3 marks):** For $S_1$: greedy action is $A_2$ (since $Q(S_1,A_2)=0.6 > 0.4$). $$ P(A_1 \mid S_1) = \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert} = \frac{0.2}{2} = 0.1 $$ So the *probability* of choosing $A_1$ is $\mathbf{0.1}$ (the problem statement asserts the agent did choose $A_1$ ŌĆö this is consistent with a single sampled trajectory). For $S_2$: greedy action is $A_2$ ($Q=0.5$). $$ P(A_2 \mid S_2) = 1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert} = 1 - 0.2 + 0.1 = 0.9 $$ So $A_2$ is chosen with probability $\mathbf{0.9}$ (we assume it is sampled). $P(A_1 \mid S_2) = 0.1$. **(ii) SARSA update (4 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} Q(S_1,A_1) &= 0.4,\quad Q(S_2,A_2) = 0.5,\quad R = 2,\ \alpha=0.5,\ \gamma=0.8 \\[4pt] \text{TD target} &= R + \gamma Q(S_2, A_2) = 2 + 0.8 \times 0.5 = 2.4 \\[4pt] \text{TD error } \delta &= 2.4 - 0.4 = 2.0 \\[4pt] Q(S_1,A_1) &\leftarrow 0.4 + 0.5 \times 2.0 = 0.4 + 1.0 = 1.4 \\[4pt] \therefore\ Q(S_1, A_1) &= \mathbf{1.4} \end{aligned} $$ > Note that even though $A_1$ was the *non-greedy* (suboptimal) action in $S_1$, the positive reward of $+2$ and the value of the next action $A_2$ in $S_2$ push $Q(S_1,A_1)$ **above** $Q(S_1,A_2) = 0.6$. On the next visit to $S_1$, the greedy action will *switch* to $A_1$ ŌĆö this is policy improvement in action. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Mixing up the TD target.** Writing $R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S',a')$ for SARSA ŌĆö that is the Q-Learning target. SARSA must use the **specific** $Q(S',A')$ where $A'$ is sampled from the policy. > 2. **Forgetting to choose $A'$ before the update.** Many students apply the update *before* selecting $A'$; in canonical SARSA the next action is **selected first**, then used in the update, then assigned to $A$ for the next iteration. > 3. **Skipping the GLIE / RobbinsŌĆōMonro conditions.** A 7-mark sub-part on convergence expects *both* conditions explicitly listed, not just the words "SARSA converges". > 4. **Conflating SARSA with n-step TD / Expected SARSA.** Board answers sometimes present the Expected-SARSA target $\sum_a \pi(a\mid s') Q(s',a)$ ŌĆö note this is a *variant* and not the same as vanilla SARSA. > 5. **No units / no mention of bounds on $\alpha$ and $\gamma$.** Always state $0<\alpha<1$ and $0\le\gamma<1$ for full credit. --- ### ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **SARSA = State, Action, Reward, next State, next Action** ŌĆö a five-tuple, on-policy TD control. - **Update rule:** $Q(S,A) \leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha \big[ R + \gamma Q(S',A') - Q(S,A) \big]$. - **On-policy:** Learns the value of the *policy being followed*, not the optimal policy. Use the actual next action $A'$, not $\max_{a'}$. - **Q-Learning contrast:** $Q(S,A) \leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha \big[ R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S',a') - Q(S,A) \big]$ ŌĆö off-policy. - **Convergence requires:** (i) GLIE conditions (infinite exploration, $\varepsilon_k \to 0$); (ii) RobbinsŌĆōMonro step-sizes ($\sum \alpha = \infty$, $\sum \alpha^2 < \infty$); (iii) all $(s,a)$ visited infinitely often. - **Behavioural trait:** SARSA is **conservative** under exploration ŌĆö it learns safe paths; Q-Learning is **optimistic** and may take risky exploratory paths. - **Algorithm loop mnemonic:** *S ŌåÆ A ŌåÆ (env) ŌåÆ R, SŌĆ▓ ŌåÆ AŌĆ▓ (same policy) ŌåÆ Update ŌåÆ shift SŌåÉSŌĆ▓, AŌåÉAŌĆ▓*. - **Variants to know for viva (KTU advanced questions):** - **Expected SARSA:** replace $Q(S',A')$ with $\sum_a \pi(a\mid S') Q(S',a)$ ŌĆö reduces variance. - **n-step SARSA:** uses $n$-step returns instead of one-step TD. - **SARSAA (modified SARSA with eligibility traces):** adds an accumulating or replacing trace. - **Common exam keywords to memorise:** *temporal difference, on-policy, quintuple, Bellman expectation operator, GLIE, RobbinsŌĆōMonro, $\varepsilon$-greedy, bootstrap, cliff-walking example, conservative vs optimistic, behavioural vs target policy.*

topics.title: Eligibility Traces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT868 - INTRODUCTION TO REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Eligibility Traces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eligibility Traces** are a fundamental mechanism in Reinforcement Learning that act as a short-term, decaying memory of recently visited states (or stateŌĆōaction pairs). They provide an elegant *temporal bridge* between one-step **Temporal Difference (TD) learning** and **Monte Carlo (MC) methods**, enabling every step of an episode to be credited for future rewards efficiently.

Mathematically, an **eligibility trace** $E_t(s) \in \mathbb{R}$ is a scalar value assigned to each state $s$ at time $t$. It indicates how *eligible* that state is to undergo a learning update when a TD error occurs.

The fundamental **accumulating trace** update rule is:

$$E_t(s) = \begin{cases} \gamma \lambda \, E_{t-1}(s) & \text{if } s \neq S_t \\ \gamma \lambda \, E_{t-1}(s) + 1 & \text{if } s = S_t \end{cases}$$

where:
- $\gamma$ is the **discount factor** ($0 \le \gamma \le 1$),
- $\lambda$ is the **trace-decay parameter** ($0 \le \lambda \le 1$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Coffee Stain" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Sticky Note in a Classroom**
> Imagine a teacher announces a surprise test tomorrow. As the students hear the news, each one receives a *sticky note* worth **1 unit**. Every hour that passes, the stickiness of the note fades by some factor (say 50%). When the test actually happens two days later, the teacher inspects *who in the room* still has a noticeable sticky residue. Those with the *highest residue* receive the **most credit** for predicting the test.

- The **sticky note** = the eligibility trace $E_t(s)$.
- The **fading factor** = $\gamma \lambda$.
- The **teacher's inspection** = the TD error $\delta_t$.
- The **credit given** = the weight update $\alpha \, \delta_t \, E_t(s)$.

States visited long ago and rarely revisited have **near-zero** eligibility ŌĆö they receive negligible updates. States visited frequently and recently are **highly eligible** ŌĆö they receive strong updates.

## 1.3 Why Eligibility Traces Matter (KTU High-Yield Point)

> [!TIP]
> Without eligibility traces, **TD(0)** updates only the *single* state just visited. With eligibility traces, **TD(╬╗)** updates *all* recently visited states *in proportion* to their recency and visit frequency. This is the central theme of Module 3.

**Key Roles in Reinforcement Learning:**
1. **Credit Assignment** ŌĆö propagates TD errors backward through time efficiently.
2. **Speed of Learning** ŌĆö accelerates convergence compared to pure TD(0).
3. **Theoretical Bridge** ŌĆö unifies **TD(0)** ($\lambda = 0$), **MC** ($\lambda = 1$), and every intermediate *n-step* learner into a single continuum.
4. **Biological Plausibility** ŌĆö mirrors *synaptic eligibility traces* observed in real neural circuits (temporally decaying synaptic tags).

## 1.4 Three Equivalent Mathematical Views of Eligibility Traces

KTU examiners frequently test these three perspectives ŌĆö **memorize all three:**

| # | Perspective | Core Idea |
|---|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Forward View (Theoretical)** | Defines an *n-step* return $G_t^{(n)}$ and a geometric average $G_t^\lambda$ that is estimated at the end of the episode. |
| 2 | **Backward View (Mechanistic)** | A decaying trace is left at each visited state; updates are applied online using $\delta_t$ and the trace vector. |
| 3 | **Implementation (Computational)** | A *vector* $\mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{S}|}$ is maintained and updated with one operation per time step. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sutton & Barto's Theorem:** The **forward** and **backward** views are *mathematically equivalent* (in the offline, every-visit Monte Carlo sense) ŌĆö they compute exactly the same total update.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The n-Step Return ŌĆö Building Block of Eligibility Traces

Before we define $\lambda$-returns, we need the **n-step return**, which is a reward estimate that looks $n$ steps into the future.

For a state $S_t$, the **1-step TD return** is:

$$G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$$

The **2-step return** is:

$$G_t^{(2)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 V(S_{t+2})$$

Generalizing to $n$ steps:

$$G_t^{(n)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$$

And the corresponding **n-step TD error** is:

$$\delta_t^{(n)} = G_t^{(n)} - V(S_t)$$

The **n-step TD update rule** is then:

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \, \delta_t^{(n)}$$

**Logic steps of the n-step return:**
- **(a)** Look ahead $n$ actual rewards (sampled from experience).
- **(b)** Bootstrap the remainder using the current value estimate $V(S_{t+n})$.
- **(c)** Discount future rewards by $\gamma^k$ for distance $k$.
- **(d)** Apply the update *only* to the state $S_t$ that initiated the look-ahead.

> [!NOTE]
> The **variance** of $G_t^{(n)}$ grows with $n$ (more random rewards), but the **bias** shrinks (less reliance on a possibly wrong bootstrap). Eligibility traces elegantly let us *average over all $n$* simultaneously.

## 2.2 The ╬╗-Return ŌĆö Averaging the n-Step Returns

The **╬╗-return** is a geometric-weighted average of all n-step returns from $n=1$ to $n=\infty$:

$$G_t^\lambda = (1 - \lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t$$

where $G_t$ is the **complete Monte Carlo return** (used as the terminal weight). The factor $(1-\lambda)$ normalizes the weights so they sum to **1** (this is a *convex combination*).

### Properties of $G_t^\lambda$:
- When $\lambda = 0$: $G_t^\lambda = G_t^{(1)}$ ŌĆö the **pure TD(0)** estimator.
- When $\lambda = 1$: $G_t^\lambda = G_t$ ŌĆö the **pure Monte Carlo** estimator.
- When $0 < \lambda < 1$: a **biased low-variance compromise** blending both ends.

## 2.3 The Backward View ŌĆö TD(╬╗) Algorithm

The **mechanistic** (backward) view maintains the eligibility trace vector $\mathbf{E}_t$ and updates value estimates *online*, step by step.

### The Three Recurrences Per Time Step

| Step | Equation | Meaning |
|------|----------|---------|
| **1. TD Error** | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ | Measures surprise at time $t$ |
| **2. Trace Update** | $E_t(s) = \gamma\lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | Decay all traces; boost the current state |
| **3. Value Update** | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t E_t(s) \quad \forall s$ | Spread the surprise via the traces |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic ŌĆö "Decay, Boost, Spread"** ŌĆö every step in TD(╬╗): *decay* old traces by $\gamma\lambda$, *boost* the current state's trace by $+1$, then *spread* the TD error $\delta_t$ through the eligibility vector.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Conditions |
|---------|---------|---------------------|
| **n-step return** | $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \gamma^{k-1} R_{t+k} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$ | $n \ge 1$ |
| **n-step TD error** | $\delta_t^{(n)} = G_t^{(n)} - V(S_t)$ | Bootstrap at depth $n$ |
| **╬╗-return** | $G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1}G_t$ | Forward view |
| **Eligibility trace (accumulating)** | $E_t(s) = \gamma\lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t=s)$ | Default Sutton-Barto rule |
| **Eligibility trace (replacing)** | $E_t(s) = (1-\mathbf{1}(S_t=s)) \gamma\lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t=s)$ | Bounded by 1; cap at 1 |
| **TD(╬╗) value update** | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t E_t(s)$ | Per-state, per-step |
| **TD error** | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ | One-step bootstrap |
| **Offline equivalence** | $\sum_t \alpha \delta_t E_t(s) = \alpha \sum_t [G_t^\lambda - V(S_t)] \mathbf{1}(S_t=s)$ | Only in offline every-visit MC |
| **Convergence (tabular)** | $\lambda \in [0,1)$ | Convergence to fixed point of $V_\pi$ |
| **Sarsa(╬╗) action-value** | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \delta_t E_t(s,a)$ | Same logic for actions |
| **True Online TD(╬╗) trace** | $E_t(s) = \gamma\lambda E_{t-1}(s) + 1 - \alpha\gamma\lambda E_{t-1}(S_t)$ | Corrects online bias |
| **Dutch trace** | $E_t = \gamma\lambda E_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)\mathbf{1} + \alpha \gamma\lambda E_{t-1}\mathbf{1}$ | Replacing + decay correction |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of Eligibility Traces

Eligibility traces are not merely a theoretical curiosity ŌĆö they power numerous real engineering applications:

1. **Atari Game-Playing Agents (Deep RL):** The original A3C and related actor-critic architectures use eligibility-like multi-step credit assignment to accelerate training of deep neural policies.
2. **Robotic Control:** Continuous-control tasks (walking, grasping) benefit from trace-based credit assignment because delays between action and reward are common.
3. **Recommendation Systems:** When a user clicks on a long-delayed item, eligibility traces help attribute the click to earlier browsing states.
4. **AlphaGo & Game AI:** Temporal credit assignment over long game sequences is a classic use case for $\lambda$-style methods.
5. **Neural-Science Models:** *Synaptic eligibility traces* in biological brains have a 100+ year history; RL traces are their computational analog.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **utility** of eligibility traces lies in the *one-update-per-step* efficiency of the backward view while maintaining the *unbiased-but-lower-variance* theoretical properties of the forward view's $\lambda$-return.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the ╬╗-Return Weights Sum to 1

The ╬╗-return is defined as:

$$G_t^\lambda = (1 - \lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t$$

**Step 1.** Define the *weights* $w_n$ for each n-step return.

$$w_n = (1-\lambda) \lambda^{n-1} \quad \text{for } 1 \le n \le T-t-1, \quad w_{T-t} = \lambda^{T-t-1}$$

**Step 2.** Compute the sum of weights for the first $T-t-1$ terms. This is a finite geometric series.

$$\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} w_n = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1}$$

**Step 3.** Apply the geometric sum formula $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} r^n = \frac{1-r^N}{1-r}$:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} w_n = (1-\lambda) \cdot \frac{1 - \lambda^{T-t-1}}{1 - \lambda}$$

**Step 4.** Simplify (the $(1-\lambda)$ cancels):

$$\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} w_n = 1 - \lambda^{T-t-1}$$

**Step 5.** Add the final Monte Carlo weight $w_{T-t} = \lambda^{T-t-1}$:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} w_n + w_{T-t} = 1 - \lambda^{T-t-1} + \lambda^{T-t-1} = 1$$

**Conclusion:** The weights form a valid probability distribution (convex combination). $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: TD(╬╗) Error as a Sum of n-Step Errors

The **TD(╬╗) error** $\delta_t^\lambda$ is defined via the ╬╗-return as:

$$\delta_t^\lambda = G_t^\lambda - V(S_t)$$

Using the linearity of expectation, we substitute the ╬╗-return:

$$\delta_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t - V(S_t)$$

**Step 1.** Each $G_t^{(n)} - V(S_t) = \delta_t^{(n)}$, the n-step TD error.

$$\delta_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} [V(S_t) + \delta_t^{(n)}] + \lambda^{T-t-1}[V(S_t) + G_t - V(S_t)] - V(S_t)$$

**Step 2.** Use $G_t = V(S_t) + \sum_{m=1}^{T-t} \gamma^{m-1} R_{t+m} + \gamma^{T-t} V(S_T) - V(S_t)$ ŌĆö for clarity, treat the terminal as a sum. Let us instead proceed with the canonical simplification:

$$\delta_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} \delta_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1}(G_t - V(S_t))$$

**Step 3.** Substitute $G_t - V(S_t) = \sum_{k=1}^{T-t} \gamma^{k-1} \delta_{t+k-1}^{(1)}$ ŌĆö which is a telescoping identity. The classical Sutton & Barto result is:

$$\delta_t^\lambda = \sum_{k=t}^{T-1} (\gamma\lambda)^{k-t} \delta_k^{(1)}$$

**Step 4.** The total value update summed over an episode equals:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \delta_t^\lambda = \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \sum_{k=t}^{T-1} (\gamma\lambda)^{k-t} \delta_k^{(1)}$$

**Step 5.** Re-index (swap the order of summation) to get the **backward view identity**:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \delta_t^\lambda \cdot \mathbf{1}(S_t = s) = \sum_{k=0}^{T-1} \delta_k^{(1)} \cdot \sum_{t=0}^{k} (\gamma\lambda)^{k-t} \mathbf{1}(S_t=s)$$

**Step 6.** Recognize the inner sum as the **eligibility trace** at the moment the reward is observed:

$$E_k(s) = \sum_{t=0}^{k} (\gamma\lambda)^{k-t} \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$$

**Step 7.** Therefore:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \alpha \delta_t^\lambda \mathbf{1}(S_t = s) = \alpha \sum_{k=0}^{T-1} \delta_k^{(1)} E_k(s)$$

This is the **fundamental backwardŌĆōforward equivalence theorem**: *the sum of forward-view $\lambda$-return updates equals the sum of backward-view TD(╬╗) updates*. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation: Recursive Form of the Eligibility Trace

The non-recursive definition is:

$$E_t(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{t} (\gamma\lambda)^{t-k} \mathbf{1}(S_k = s)$$

**Step 1.** Split off the most recent term ($k = t$):

$$E_t(s) = \mathbf{1}(S_t = s) + \sum_{k=0}^{t-1} (\gamma\lambda)^{t-k} \mathbf{1}(S_k = s)$$

**Step 2.** Factor $(\gamma\lambda)$ out of the sum:

$$E_t(s) = \mathbf{1}(S_t = s) + \gamma\lambda \sum_{k=0}^{t-1} (\gamma\lambda)^{t-1-k} \mathbf{1}(S_k = s)$$

**Step 3.** Recognize the inner sum as $E_{t-1}(s)$:

$$E_t(s) = \gamma\lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s) \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Pseudocode Logic (Tabular TD(╬╗))

Below is a high-yield KTU matrix summarizing the **online backward-view TD(╬╗)** algorithm in compact form. *(Per protocol, no code ŌĆö algorithmic logic only.)*

| Phase | Operation | Equation |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| **Init** | Set $V(s) = 0$ for all $s$; $E(s) = 0$ for all $s$ | ŌĆö |
| **For each episode** | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| 1 | Observe $S_t$ | ŌĆö |
| 2 | Choose $A_t \sim \pi(\cdot \vert S_t)$ | ŌĆö |
| 3 | Take action, observe $R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}$ | ŌĆö |
| 4 | **Compute TD error** | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ |
| 5 | **Increment trace** | $E(S_t) \leftarrow E(S_t) + 1$ |
| 6 | **Decay all traces** | $E(s) \leftarrow \gamma\lambda E(s) \quad \forall s$ |
| 7 | **Update all values** | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t E(s) \quad \forall s$ |
| 8 | $S_t \leftarrow S_{t+1}$; repeat from step 2 | ŌĆö |
| **End** | Until $S_t$ is terminal | ŌĆö |

> [!NOTE]
> Steps 6 and 7 are often *combined* as: $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t E(s)$ after performing $E(s) \leftarrow \gamma\lambda E(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t=s)$.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> [!TIP]
> This is the type of calculation a KTU examiner loves. Trace through it step by step.

**Setup:** An MDP with $\gamma = 0.9$, $\lambda = 0.8$, $\alpha = 0.1$, and an episode of length 3 with rewards and values:

| $t$ | $S_t$ | $R_{t+1}$ | $V(S_t)$ initially |
|-----|-------|-----------|-------------------|
| 0 | A | 1 | 0.5 |
| 1 | B | 1 | 0.4 |
| 2 | C | 2 | 0.3 (terminal) |

Initial $V(A) = 0.5$, $V(B) = 0.4$, $V(C) = 0.3$.

**Step 1 (t = 0):** Visit A, observe reward $R_1 = 1$, next state B.

$$\delta_0 = R_1 + \gamma V(B) - V(A) = 1 + 0.9 \times 0.4 - 0.5 = 1 + 0.36 - 0.5 = 0.86$$

**Trace update:** $E(A) = \gamma\lambda \cdot 0 + 1 = 1$; all others remain 0.

**Value update for A:** $V(A) = 0.5 + 0.1 \times 0.86 \times 1 = 0.5 + 0.086 = \mathbf{0.586}$

**Step 2 (t = 1):** Visit B, observe reward $R_2 = 1$, next state C.

$$\delta_1 = R_2 + \gamma V(C) - V(B) = 1 + 0.9 \times 0.3 - 0.4 = 1 + 0.27 - 0.4 = 0.87$$

**Trace update:** $E(A) = \gamma\lambda \times 1 = 0.9 \times 0.8 \times 1 = 0.72$ (decayed). $E(B) = 0.72 + 1 = 1.72$ (B was boosted + A's old trace decayed).

**Value updates:**

$$V(A) = 0.586 + 0.1 \times 0.87 \times 0.72 = 0.586 + 0.0626 = \mathbf{0.6486}$$

$$V(B) = 0.4 + 0.1 \times 0.87 \times 1.72 = 0.4 + 0.1496 = \mathbf{0.5496}$$

**Step 3 (t = 2):** Visit C, observe reward $R_3 = 2$, terminal (next state value = 0).

$$\delta_2 = R_3 + \gamma \cdot 0 - V(C) = 2 - 0.3 = 1.7$$

**Trace update:** $E(A) = 0.72 \times 0.72 = 0.5184$; $E(B) = 1.72 \times 0.72 = 1.2384$; $E(C) = 0 + 1 = 1$.

**Value updates:**

$$V(A) = 0.6486 + 0.1 \times 1.7 \times 0.5184 = 0.6486 + 0.0881 = \mathbf{0.7367}$$

$$V(B) = 0.5496 + 0.1 \times 1.7 \times 1.2384 = 0.5496 + 0.2105 = \mathbf{0.7601}$$

$$V(C) = 0.3 + 0.1 \times 1.7 \times 1 = 0.3 + 0.17 = \mathbf{0.47}$$

Notice how A's value was updated at **three different time steps** even though A was visited only once ŌĆö this is the power of the eligibility trace.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: TD(╬╗) System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Environment] --> nodeB[Agent State St]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Action Selection Pi]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Take Action At]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Observe Reward Rt+1]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Observe Next State St+1]
    nodeE --> nodeG[TD Error Delta t]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Eligibility Trace Vector E]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Trace Decay by Gamma times Lambda]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Boost Current State Trace]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Value Update for All States]
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Value Function V]
    nodeL --> nodeB
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: One Time Step of TD(╬╗)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Env as Environment
    participant Agent as Agent
    participant Trace as Eligibility Vector
    participant Value as Value Function
    Agent->>Env: Send Action At in State St
    Env-->>Agent: Return Reward Rt+1 and Next State St+1
    Agent->>Value: Query V St and V St+1
    Value-->>Agent: Return V St and V St+1
    Agent->>Agent: Compute Delta t = Rt+1 plus Gamma V St+1 minus V St
    Agent->>Trace: Decay all traces by Gamma times Lambda
    Agent->>Trace: Increment E of St by 1
    Agent->>Value: For all states s, V s = V s plus Alpha Delta t times E s
    Note over Agent,Value: Backward View Spreads Delta Back Through Time
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[TD 0 lambda equals 0] --> nodeB[One step TD Bias High Variance Low]
    nodeC[TD lambda 0 less than lambda less than 1] --> nodeD[Multi step n step blend Bias Medium Variance Medium]
    nodeE[Monte Carlo lambda equals 1] --> nodeF[Full episode return Bias Zero Variance High]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Eligibility Traces Unified Continuum]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG

```

    end

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, May 2024 Style, Module 3]**

### **Q1. Define the term "eligibility trace" in reinforcement learning and state its update rule. Mention the role of the parameters $\gamma$ and $\lambda$.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
An **eligibility trace** $E_t(s)$ is a scalar memory assigned to a state $s$ at time $t$ that decays over time and is incremented whenever $s$ is visited. It indicates how eligible $s$ is to receive a learning update when a TD error occurs.

The update rule is:

$$E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda \, E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$$

- $\gamma$ ŌĆö **discount factor** that determines how much future rewards matter relative to immediate ones.
- $\lambda$ ŌĆö **trace-decay rate** that controls the effective horizon of the trace ($\lambda = 0$ gives TD(0); $\lambda \to 1$ approaches Monte Carlo).

### **Q2. Differentiate between the forward view and backward view of TD(╬╗).** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Forward View | Backward View |
|--------|--------------|---------------|
| **What it is** | Theoretical construct ŌĆö defines the $\lambda$-return $G_t^\lambda$ | Mechanistic algorithm ŌĆö uses eligibility traces |
| **When updates happen** | After episode ends (offline) | Step by step (online) |
| **Storage** | Needs n-step returns at each $t$ | Needs only the trace vector |
| **Use** | Theory, proofs | Practical implementation |

Both views give **mathematically equivalent total updates** in the offline, every-visit case (Sutton & Barto theorem).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, End-Semester Estimation, Module 3]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define the **n-step return** $G_t^{(n)}$ and write the **╬╗-return** $G_t^\lambda$ as a weighted sum of n-step returns. Show that the weights sum to 1. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **TD(╬╗) algorithm** with the backward view. Derive the recursive form of the eligibility trace from its non-recursive definition. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Definition of n-step return:**

$$G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \gamma^{k-1} R_{t+k} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$$

**Definition of ╬╗-return:**

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t$$

**Proof that weights sum to 1:**

The weights are $w_n = (1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$ for $1 \le n \le T-t-1$ and $w_{T-t} = \lambda^{T-t-1}$.

$$\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} w_n = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} = (1-\lambda) \cdot \frac{1 - \lambda^{T-t-1}}{1-\lambda} = 1 - \lambda^{T-t-1}$$

Adding the terminal weight: $(1 - \lambda^{T-t-1}) + \lambda^{T-t-1} = 1$. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**TD(╬╗) backward view algorithm:**

The three operations per step are:

1. Compute TD error: $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$
2. Update traces: $E_t(s) = \gamma\lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ for all $s$
3. Update values: $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t E_t(s)$ for all $s$

**Derivation of recursive form:**

The non-recursive definition is:

$$E_t(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{t} (\gamma\lambda)^{t-k} \mathbf{1}(S_k = s)$$

Splitting off the $k=t$ term and factoring:

$$E_t(s) = \mathbf{1}(S_t = s) + \gamma\lambda \sum_{k=0}^{t-1} (\gamma\lambda)^{t-1-k} \mathbf{1}(S_k = s) = \gamma\lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$$

$\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the concept of **accumulating, replacing, and Dutch traces**. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider an MDP with $\gamma = 1$, $\lambda = 0.5$, $\alpha = 0.1$, and the following episode sequence of states and rewards: $S_0 = A, R_1 = 2, S_1 = B, R_2 = 0, S_2 = C, R_3 = 1, S_3 = \text{terminal}$. Assume $V(A) = 1.0$, $V(B) = 0.5$, $V(C) = 0.2$ initially and all eligibility traces are 0. Using TD(╬╗) with the backward view, compute the updated value estimates after the full episode. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**1. Accumulating Trace** (default Sutton-Barto):

$$E_t(s) = \gamma\lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$$

*Advantages:* Simple, derived directly from theory.
*Disadvantages:* Traces can grow very large for frequently visited states, leading to high-variance updates and slow learning.

**2. Replacing Trace** (Singh & Sutton, 1996):

$$E_t(s) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } s = S_t \\ \gamma\lambda E_{t-1}(s) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

*Advantages:* Trace values are bounded by 1, more stable updates, often empirically faster.
*Disadvantages:* Theoretical justification is weaker; loses some history.

**3. Dutch Trace** (van Seijen, 2014):

$$E_t(s) = \gamma\lambda E_{t-1}(s) + (1-\alpha) \mathbf{1}(S_t = s) + \alpha \gamma\lambda E_{t-1}(s) \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$$

*Advantages:* Combines the boundedness of replacing traces with the theoretical correctness of accumulating traces. The state-of-the-art trace type.
*Disadvantages:* More complex formula.

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

Given: $\gamma = 1$, $\lambda = 0.5$, $\alpha = 0.1$. Initial: $V(A) = 1.0$, $V(B) = 0.5$, $V(C) = 0.2$. All $E = 0$.

**Step 1: t = 0, state A, $R_1 = 2$, next state B.**

$$\delta_0 = R_1 + \gamma V(B) - V(A) = 2 + 1 \times 0.5 - 1.0 = 1.5$$

Trace update: $E(A) = 1 \times 0.5 \times 0 + 1 = 1$; $E(B) = 0.5 \times 0 = 0$; $E(C) = 0$.

Value update:
$V(A) = 1.0 + 0.1 \times 1.5 \times 1 = 1.0 + 0.15 = \mathbf{1.15}$
$V(B) = 0.5 + 0.1 \times 1.5 \times 0 = \mathbf{0.5}$
$V(C) = 0.2 + 0.1 \times 1.5 \times 0 = \mathbf{0.2}$

**Step 2: t = 1, state B, $R_2 = 0$, next state C.**

$$\delta_1 = R_2 + \gamma V(C) - V(B) = 0 + 1 \times 0.2 - 0.5 = -0.3$$

Trace update:
$E(A) = 0.5 \times 1 = 0.5$ (decayed)
$E(B) = 0.5 \times 0 + 1 = 1$ (boosted)
$E(C) = 0.5 \times 0 = 0$

Value update:
$V(A) = 1.15 + 0.1 \times (-0.3) \times 0.5 = 1.15 - 0.015 = \mathbf{1.135}$
$V(B) = 0.5 + 0.1 \times (-0.3) \times 1 = 0.5 - 0.03 = \mathbf{0.47}$
$V(C) = 0.2 + 0.1 \times (-0.3) \times 0 = \mathbf{0.2}$

**Step 3: t = 2, state C, $R_3 = 1$, terminal (next $V = 0$).**

$$\delta_2 = R_3 + \gamma \times 0 - V(C) = 1 - 0.2 = 0.8$$

Trace update:
$E(A) = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25$
$E(B) = 0.5 \times 1 = 0.5$
$E(C) = 0.5 \times 0 + 1 = 1$

Value update:
$V(A) = 1.135 + 0.1 \times 0.8 \times 0.25 = 1.135 + 0.02 = \mathbf{1.155}$
$V(B) = 0.47 + 0.1 \times 0.8 \times 0.5 = 0.47 + 0.04 = \mathbf{0.51}$
$V(C) = 0.2 + 0.1 \times 0.8 \times 1 = 0.2 + 0.08 = \mathbf{0.28}$

**Final values after the episode:**
$$V(A) = 1.155, \quad V(B) = 0.51, \quad V(C) = 0.28$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the indicator function $\mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$**: Many students incorrectly add 1 to *all* states at every time step. Trace is incremented **only for the visited state**.
> 2. **Confusing the order of decay and boost**: In the recursive form, decay the *previous* trace by $\gamma\lambda$ **first**, then add the indicator. Reverse order gives wrong values.
> 3. **Mixing up $\lambda$ and $\gamma$**: $\gamma$ discounts *rewards*; $\lambda$ decays *traces*. Both appear in the same product, but they have different semantic roles.
> 4. **Forgetting the bootstrap at episode end**: At a terminal state, the next-state value is **0**, not $V(S_{t+1})$.
> 5. **Off-line vs on-line**: The forwardŌĆōbackward equivalence is **strictly true** only in the offline, every-visit, tabular, MC case. Online, accumulating-trace TD(╬╗) is *not* perfectly equivalent to the ╬╗-return ŌĆö this is why **True Online TD(╬╗)** exists.
> 6. **Sarsa(╬╗) vs TD(╬╗)**: For action-value methods, eligibility is on **(state, action)** pairs, and the trace update uses $\mathbf{1}(S_t=s, A_t=a)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-page rapid revision for the day before KTU Module-3 exam:**

- Ō£ģ Eligibility traces **bridge** TD(0) and Monte Carlo along the $\lambda$ parameter.
- Ō£ģ The **forward view** (theoretical) uses the **╬╗-return** $G_t^\lambda$ ŌĆö a geometric average of n-step returns.
- Ō£ģ The **backward view** (algorithmic) maintains a **trace vector** and updates it with **decay + boost**.
- Ō£ģ Three operations per step: compute $\delta_t$, update trace $E_t = \gamma\lambda E_{t-1} + \mathbf{1}$, update value $V \leftarrow V + \alpha \delta_t E$.
- Ō£ģ $\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow$ **TD(0)**; $\lambda = 1 \Rightarrow$ **Monte Carlo**; $0 < \lambda < 1 \Rightarrow$ **TD(╬╗)**.
- Ō£ģ Higher $\lambda$ means **longer effective horizon** of credit assignment.
- Ō£ģ **Accumulating traces** can grow unbounded; **replacing traces** are capped at 1; **Dutch traces** combine the best of both.
- Ō£ģ The **forwardŌĆōbackward equivalence** is an offline result; online equivalence requires **True Online TD(╬╗)**.
- Ō£ģ **Sarsa(╬╗)** extends eligibility traces to action values $Q(s,a)$; **Watkins's Q(╬╗)** handles off-policy traces via resetting.
- Ō£ģ Convergence of tabular TD(╬╗) requires **$\lambda \in [0, 1)$** and standard RobbinsŌĆōMonro step-size conditions.
- Ō£ģ Eligibility traces are a **computational trick** (backward view) that *implements* the **theoretical ideal** (forward view's ╬╗-return).
- Ō£ģ Always remember: **decay old traces, boost current trace, spread the TD error**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Function Approximation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT868 - INTRODUCTION TO REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linear Function Approximation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ­¤ōś Module 4 ŌĆö Linear Function Approximation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Function Approximation (LFA):** A class of value-estimation methods in Reinforcement Learning (RL) where the value function (state-value $v_\pi(s)$ or action-value $q_\pi(s,a)$) is represented as a **linear combination** of a fixed feature vector $x(s) \in \mathbb{R}^n$, weighted by a learnable parameter vector $w \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

Mathematically, the approximate value function is given by:

$$\hat{v}(s, w) \triangleq w^{T}\,x(s) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i\,x_i(s)$$

where:
- $\hat{v}(s, w)$ ŌĆö Approximation of the true value of state $s$.
- $w = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)^{T}$ ŌĆö **Weight vector** (the only learnable parameters).
- $x(s) = (x_1(s), x_2(s), \dots, x_n(s))^{T}$ ŌĆö **Feature vector** of state $s$ (fixed, hand-engineered or constructed).

The goal is to adjust the weights $w$ so that $\hat{v}(s,w)$ is as close as possible to the true return $v_\pi(s)$ for every state $s$.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition ­¤¦Ā

Imagine you are tasting a new **biryani** from a street stall and want to predict its "spice score" (0ŌĆō10). You mentally weigh factors like:

| Factor | Weight (Importance) | Raw Score (0ŌĆō10) | Weighted Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aroma | 0.5 | 7 | 3.5 |
| Masala Heat | 0.3 | 8 | 2.4 |
| Salt Balance | 0.2 | 6 | 1.2 |

$$\text{Spice Score} = (0.5)(7) + (0.3)(8) + (0.2)(6) = 7.1$$

This is exactly **Linear Function Approximation**:
- The **factors** (aroma, heat, salt) = features $x_i(s)$.
- The **importance** you assign = weights $w_i$.
- The **predicted spice score** = $\hat{v}(s, w)$.
- The **learning process** = adjusting weights after every tasting to reduce the gap between prediction and the real taste.

So in RL: **"We are the critic, the weight vector is our memory of which features matter, and the feature vector is what we observe."**

---

### 1.3 Why We Need Function Approximation?

In tabular RL, we store one value per state in a lookup table. This fails when:

- The state space is **large or continuous** (e.g., self-driving cars, Go, robotics).
- We have never seen a particular state before ŌĆö we need to **generalize** from similar states.

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization** = the ability to estimate the value of a *new* state based on its similarity to states already encountered. Linear FA provides this by sharing parameters across states via overlapping features.

### 1.4 Key Physical / Conceptual Constants

| Symbol | Name | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | Step-size (learning rate) | $0 < \alpha \leq 1$ (often $0.01$ ŌĆō $0.5$) |
| $\gamma$ | Discount factor | $0 \leq \gamma < 1$ |
| $\mu(s)$ | On-policy distribution over states | $\sum_s \mu(s) = 1$ |
| $n$ | Number of features | small for LFA ($< 1000$ typically) |
| $\lambda$ | Eligibility-trace decay | $0 \leq \lambda \leq 1$ |

---

### 1.5 KTU 2024 Course Outcomes Mapped

| CO Code | Outcome | Relevance to this topic |
|---|---|---|
| **CO4** | Apply function approximation techniques to large-scale RL problems | Directly mapped |
| **CO5** | Analyze convergence properties and limitations of linear methods | Directly mapped |
| **CO3** | Implement policy evaluation/control algorithms | Foundation for this module |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Linear Architecture ŌĆö Anatomy

In linear function approximation, we assume a **linear architecture**:

$$\hat{v}(s, w) \;=\; w^{T}\,x(s)$$

The partial derivative with respect to any single weight $w_i$ is beautifully simple:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{v}(s, w)}{\partial w_i} \;=\; x_i(s)$$

This is the **single most important property** of linear FA. It means the gradient is independent of $w$, which is why linear methods have strong theoretical convergence guarantees.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Intuition:** Each weight $w_i$ controls the slope of the value function along the $i^{th}$ feature dimension. If $x_i(s)$ increases, $\hat{v}$ scales linearly with $w_i$.

---

## 2.2 The Objective ŌĆö Mean Squared Value Error (MSVE)

We need a measure of how "wrong" our approximation is. The standard objective is the **Mean Squared Value Error**:

$$\overline{VE}(w) \;\triangleq\; \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \mu(s)\Bigl[v_\pi(s) - \hat{v}(s, w)\Bigr]^{2}$$

where:
- $\mu(s)$ ŌĆö the **on-policy distribution** under policy $\pi$ (under $\pi$, the fraction of time spent in $s$). For the episodic case, this is the normalized episode-time.
- $v_\pi(s)$ ŌĆö true value of state $s$ under $\pi$.
- $\hat{v}(s, w)$ ŌĆö our current linear approximation.

For on-policy training, the optimal weight vector $w_{TD}$ that minimizes $\overline{VE}$ satisfies the **normal equations**:

$$b \;\triangleq\; \mathbb{E}\bigl[x_t\,G_t\bigr] \;\in\; \mathbb{R}^{n}$$

$$A \;\triangleq\; \mathbb{E}\bigl[x_t\,(x_t - \gamma\,x_{t+1})^{T}\bigr] \;\in\; \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

$$w_{TD} \;=\; A^{-1}\,b$$

These two quantities ($A$ and $b$) are central to almost every result in linear FA.

---

## 2.3 Gradient and Semi-Gradient Methods

### 2.3.1 True Stochastic Gradient Descent (Monte-Carlo Target)

If $G_t$ is a sample of $v_\pi(S_t)$, then:

$$\nabla \overline{VE}(w) \;=\; -2\,\mu(s)\,\bigl[v_\pi(s) - \hat{v}(s, w)\bigr]\,\nabla_w \hat{v}(s, w)$$

Using $\nabla_w \hat{v}(s, w) = x(s)$, a sample update becomes:

$$w_{t+1} \;\leftarrow\; w_t \;+\; \alpha\,\bigl[G_t - \hat{v}(S_t, w_t)\bigr]\,x(S_t)$$

This is **Monte-Carlo (Gradient)** ŌĆö it is a true gradient method because the target $G_t$ is *unbiased* for $v_\pi(S_t)$.

### 2.3.2 Semi-Gradient Methods (Bootstrapped Targets)

When we use TD targets $U_t \triangleq R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{v}(S_{t+1}, w)$, the target itself depends on $w$, so the true gradient is corrupted. We ignore the dependency on $w$ of the target and take only the gradient with respect to $w$ through the *current* estimate. Hence the name **semi-gradient**.

The TD(0) update is:

$$w_{t+1} \;\leftarrow\; w_t \;+\; \alpha\,\bigl[R_{t+1} + \gamma\,\hat{v}(S_{t+1}, w_t) - \hat{v}(S_t, w_t)\bigr]\,x(S_t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Semi-gradient methods do not optimize any well-defined objective on-policy**, but they often converge faster and still reach a near-optimal fixed point $w_{TD}$.

---

## 2.4 Linear Methods ŌĆö Detailed Update Rules

### 2.4.1 MC Gradient (Prediction)

$$w \;\leftarrow\; w \;+\; \alpha\,\bigl[G_t - \hat{v}(S_t, w)\bigr]\,x(S_t)$$

- Bias: **Unbiased**.
- Converges to **global optimum** of $\overline{VE}$.

### 2.4.2 Semi-Gradient TD(0) (Prediction)

$$w \;\leftarrow\; w \;+\; \alpha\,\delta_t\,x(S_t)$$

where the **TD error** is:

$$\delta_t \;\triangleq\; R_{t+1} + \gamma\,\hat{v}(S_{t+1}, w) - \hat{v}(S_t, w)$$

- Bias: **Biased** during learning.
- Converges to $w_{TD}$ (a fixed point of TD(0)).

### 2.4.3 n-step Semi-Gradient TD

For $n$-step return:

$$G_{t:t+n} \;\triangleq\; R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n}\,\hat{v}(S_{t+n}, w)$$

$$w \;\leftarrow\; w \;+\; \alpha\,\bigl[G_{t:t+n} - \hat{v}(S_t, w)\bigr]\,x(S_t)$$

- Bias: Depends on $n$. Larger $n \Rightarrow$ less bias, more variance.

### 2.4.4 TD(╬╗) ŌĆö Semi-Gradient with Eligibility Traces

Define the **eligibility trace**:

$$z_{-1} \;=\; 0, \qquad z_t \;=\; \gamma\,\lambda\,z_{t-1} \;+\; x(S_t)$$

Update:

$$\delta_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma\,\hat{v}(S_{t+1}, w) - \hat{v}(S_t, w)$$

$$w \;\leftarrow\; w \;+\; \alpha\,\delta_t\,z_t$$

This unifies MC ($n = T$, $\lambda = 1$) and TD(0) ($n = 1$, $\lambda = 0$).

---

## 2.5 Least-Squares TD (LSTD) ŌĆö Batch / Data-Efficient

LSTD forms $A$ and $b$ directly from data and solves in closed form:

$$A_t \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{t-1} x_k\,(x_k - \gamma\,x_{k+1})^{T} \;+\; \varepsilon I$$

$$b_t \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{t-1} x_k\,R_{k+1}$$

$$w_t \;=\; A_t^{-1}\,b_t$$

- **Pros:** Best asymptotic MSE, no step-size tuning, sample-efficient.
- **Cons:** $O(n^2)$ memory and $O(n^3)$ per step for inversion.

**Recursive LSTD (RLSTD)** maintains $\hat{A}^{-1}$ via ShermanŌĆōMorrison:

$$\hat{A}_t^{-1} \;\leftarrow\; \hat{A}_{t-1}^{-1} \;+\; \frac{\hat{A}_{t-1}^{-1}\,x_t\,(x_t - \gamma x_{t+1})^{T}\,\hat{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}{1 + (x_t - \gamma x_{t+1})^{T}\,\hat{A}_{t-1}^{-1}\,x_t}$$

Reduces to $O(n^2)$ per step.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet ­¤ōØ

| # | Equation / Rule | Use Case | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{v}(s,w) = w^{T} x(s)$ | Approximation | Linear in both $w$ and $x$ |
| 2 | $\nabla_w \hat{v}(s,w) = x(s)$ | Gradient | Independent of $w$ |
| 3 | $\overline{VE}(w) = \sum_s \mu(s)\,[v_\pi(s) - \hat{v}(s,w)]^{2}$ | Objective | $\mu$ = on-policy state dist. |
| 4 | MC update: $w \leftarrow w + \alpha[G_t - \hat{v}]x$ | Prediction | Unbiased; $O(n)$ per step |
| 5 | TD(0): $w \leftarrow w + \alpha\,\delta_t\,x(S_t)$ | Prediction | Semi-gradient; biased |
| 6 | TD($\lambda$): $w \leftarrow w + \alpha\,\delta_t\,z_t$ | Prediction | Eligibility traces |
| 7 | LSTD: $w = A^{-1} b$ | Batch | $O(n^3)$ solve; $O(n^2)$ memory |
| 8 | RLSTD ShermanŌĆōMorrison | Online LSTD | $O(n^2)$ per step |
| 9 | $\overline{VE}(w_{TD}) \leq \dfrac{1}{1-\gamma}\,\min_w \overline{VE}(w)$ | Bound | Semi-gradient TD fixed point |
| 10 | $\hat{v}(s,w) = \sum_i w_i\,x_i(s)$ | Scalars | Use $\mid$ for absolute value |

---

## 2.7 Convergence Properties (Critical for KTU Viva!)

| Method | Convergence | Bias at Fixed Point |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient MC | Yes (global optimum of $\overline{VE}$) | Unbiased |
| Semi-gradient TD(0) | Yes (linear FA only) | $O(\alpha)$; fixed point $w_{TD}$ with bounded MSVE |
| Semi-gradient TD($\lambda$) | Yes | Bounded; lower than TD(0) for $\lambda \to 1$ |
| LSTD | Yes (exact) | $w_{TD}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The bound for semi-gradient TD(0):
> $$\overline{VE}(w_{TD}) \;\leq\; \frac{1}{1-\gamma}\,\min_{w}\,\overline{VE}(w)$$
> This says: the fixed point is at most a factor $\tfrac{1}{1-\gamma}$ worse than the best linear approximation. As $\gamma \to 1$, the bound grows ŌĆö the algorithm becomes "less forgiving" in far-sighted problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation ŌĆö Gradient of the MSVE

We want to compute $\nabla_w \overline{VE}(w)$ step by step.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Expand the squared error term:**

$$\overline{VE}(w) = \sum_{s} \mu(s)\bigl[v_\pi(s) - \hat{v}(s,w)\bigr]^{2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Use the chain rule. Let $e(s) = v_\pi(s) - \hat{v}(s,w)$. Then:**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial w}\bigl[v_\pi(s) - \hat{v}(s,w)\bigr]^{2} = 2\bigl[v_\pi(s) - \hat{v}(s,w)\bigr]\cdot\frac{\partial}{\partial w}\bigl[-\hat{v}(s,w)\bigr]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply linearity:** Since $\hat{v}(s,w) = w^{T} x(s)$,

$$\frac{\partial \hat{v}(s,w)}{\partial w} = x(s)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Combine:**

$$\nabla_w \overline{VE}(w) = 2 \sum_{s} \mu(s)\bigl[v_\pi(s) - \hat{v}(s,w)\bigr]\cdot\bigl(-x(s)\bigr)$$

$$\nabla_w \overline{VE}(w) = -2 \sum_{s} \mu(s)\bigl[v_\pi(s) - \hat{v}(s,w)\bigr]\,x(s)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Take one sample (state $S_t$ drawn under $\mu$) and use $G_t \approx v_\pi(S_t)$:**

$$\nabla_w \overline{VE}(w) \approx -2\bigl[G_t - \hat{v}(S_t,w)\bigr]\,x(S_t)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Stochastic gradient descent update (dropping the factor 2 into $\alpha$):**

$$w_{t+1} = w_t + \alpha\bigl[G_t - \hat{v}(S_t, w_t)\bigr]\,x(S_t)$$

This is the **Gradient Monte-Carlo** update for linear FA. Ō£ģ

---

## 3.2 Derivation ŌĆö Semi-Gradient TD(0) Update

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the TD error $\delta_t$:**

$$\delta_t \triangleq R_{t+1} + \gamma\,\hat{v}(S_{t+1}, w) - \hat{v}(S_t, w)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Notice that $\delta_t = 0$ when $\hat{v}$ equals the true value.** So minimizing squared TD error $\delta_t^{2}$ is a *reasonable* surrogate for $\overline{VE}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the gradient of $\delta_t^{2}$ with respect to $w$, but ONLY through the third term** $\hat{v}(S_t, w)$ (this is the "semi-gradient"):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial w}\,\delta_t^{2} \;\approx\; -2\,\delta_t\,\frac{\partial}{\partial w}\hat{v}(S_t, w) = -2\,\delta_t\,x(S_t)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Gradient descent update:**

$$w_{t+1} = w_t + \alpha\,\delta_t\,x(S_t)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Expand $\delta_t$ explicitly:**

$$w_{t+1} = w_t + \alpha\bigl[R_{t+1} + \gamma\,w_t^{T}x(S_{t+1}) - w_t^{T}x(S_t)\bigr]\,x(S_t)$$

This is the famous **linear semi-gradient TD(0) update**. Ō£ģ

---

## 3.3 Derivation ŌĆö LSTD Normal Equations

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convergence condition.** Linear semi-gradient TD(0) converges to a fixed point $w_{TD}$ where:

$$\mathbb{E}\bigl[\delta_t\,x(S_t)\bigr] = 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the linear form** $\hat{v}(s,w) = w^{T}x(s)$:

$$\mathbb{E}\bigl[\bigl(R_{t+1} + \gamma\,w^{T}x_{t+1} - w^{T}x_t\bigr)x_t\bigr] = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Distribute:**

$$\mathbb{E}\bigl[R_{t+1}\,x_t\bigr] + \gamma\,\mathbb{E}\bigl[x_{t+1}\,x_t^{T}\bigr]\,w - \mathbb{E}\bigl[x_t\,x_t^{T}\bigr]\,w = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rearrange:**

$$\mathbb{E}\bigl[x_t\,x_t^{T}\bigr]\,w \;-\; \gamma\,\mathbb{E}\bigl[x_{t+1}\,x_t^{T}\bigr]\,w \;=\; \mathbb{E}\bigl[R_{t+1}\,x_t\bigr]$$

$$\Bigl(\mathbb{E}[x_t x_t^{T}] - \gamma\,\mathbb{E}[x_{t+1}x_t^{T}]\Bigr)\,w = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} x_t]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Identify $A$ and $b$:**

$$A \triangleq \mathbb{E}\bigl[x_t\,(x_t - \gamma\,x_{t+1})^{T}\bigr]$$

$$b \triangleq \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1}\,x_t]$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Solve the linear system:**

$$w_{TD} \;=\; A^{-1}\,b$$

This is the **closed-form LSTD solution**. Ō£ģ

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, $A$ is approximated by the empirical sum over observed transitions, plus a small ridge term $\varepsilon I$ for numerical stability (since $A$ may be singular if features are redundant).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example ­¤Ä»

**Problem:** A 4-state chain, states $\{A, B, C, D\}$, left-right with terminal $A$. Discount $\gamma = 1$. Feature vectors:
- $x(A) = (1, 0)^{T}$
- $x(B) = (0, 1)^{T}$
- $x(C) = (1, 1)^{T}$
- $x(D) = (0, 0)^{T}$

True values (under uniform random policy): $v_\pi(A)=0, \;v_\pi(B)=-1, \;v_\pi(C)=-2, \;v_\pi(D)=-2$.

Starting weights $w_0 = (0, 0)^{T}$, $\alpha = 0.1$.

**Step 1:** With $w_0=0$, $\hat{v}(s) = 0$ for all $s$. TD errors on a sample transition from $C \to B$ (reward $R=-1$):

$$\delta = R + \gamma\,\hat{v}(B) - \hat{v}(C) = -1 + 0 - 0 = -1$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Update using $x(C) = (1,1)^T$:**

$$w_1 = w_0 + \alpha\,\delta\,x(C) = (0,0) + 0.1(-1)(1,1)^{T} = (-0.1, -0.1)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Recompute value estimates:**

$\hat{v}(A) = -0.1(1) + -0.1(0) = -0.1$
$\hat{v}(B) = -0.1(0) + -0.1(1) = -0.1$
$\hat{v}(C) = -0.1(1) + -0.1(1) = -0.2$
$\hat{v}(D) = -0.1(0) + -0.1(0) = 0$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Iterating** continues until convergence. Eventually $w$ approximates the best linear fit: true $v_\pi$ cannot be matched (D's value $-2$ is impossible with a 2-dim linear model!), so we converge to a *projection* that minimizes $\overline{VE}$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example ŌĆö LSTD on 1-D Random Walk

Consider the classic 1000-state Random Walk (Sutton & Barto, Example 9.1). States $s = 1, \dots, 1000$ are linearly spaced. We use 10 *basis functions*:

$$x_i(s) = \mathbb{1}\bigl(s \in \text{bucket}_i\bigr)$$

Coarse-coded into 10 overlapping intervals. We sample many transitions, build empirical $A$ and $b$ matrices ($n = 10$), then solve $w = A^{-1} b$.

The expected MSVE after running LSTD is around **0.55** (vs. **1.0** for tabular Monte-Carlo on the same samples, and **~0.6** for accumulating-traces TD).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Linear FA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    env["State S_t from Environment"] --> fe["Feature Extractor x(S_t)"]
    fe --> fv["Feature Vector x in R^n"]
    fv --> dot["Weighted Sum: w^T x"]
    wgt["Weight Vector w in R^n"] --> dot
    dot --> est["Estimated Value v_hat S_t, w"]
    errsub["True Target: R_t+1 + gamma * v_hat S_t+1"] --> errcalc
    est --> errcalc["Error / TD delta: R_t+1 + gamma * v_hat_next - v_hat"]
    errcalc --> grad["Gradient Signal: delta * x S_t"]
    grad --> upd["Update: w = w + alpha * delta * x S_t"]
    upd --> wgt
    nxt["Next State S_t+1"] --> fe
```

## 4.2 Update Loop Flow (Temporal View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Time t: observe S_t"] --> B["Compute v_hat S_t, w"]
    B --> C["Take action, get R_t+1, S_t+1"]
    C --> D["Compute v_hat S_t+1, w"]
    D --> E["delta = R_t+1 + gamma * v_hat S_t+1 - v_hat S_t"]
    E --> F["w = w + alpha * delta * x S_t"]
    F --> G["Time t+1: observe S_t+1"]
    G --> A
```

## 4.3 Family of Linear Methods ŌĆö Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["Linear Function Approximation Methods"]
    root --> gr["Gradient Methods True Gradient"]
    root --> sg["Semi-Gradient Methods Bootstrapped"]

    gr --> mc["Gradient MC"]
    gr --> ls["Least Squares LSMC / Bellman Residual"]

    sg --> td0["Semi-Gradient TD0"]
    sg --> nstep["n-step Semi-Gradient TD"]
    sg --> tdl["Semi-Gradient TD lambda"]
    sg --> lstd["Least Squares TD LSTD"]
    sg --> rlstd["Recursive LSTD"]

    lstd --> iht["In-sample LSTD"]
    sg --> q["Semi-Gradient Q-learning SARSA"]
    sg --> dq["Semi-Gradient Expected SARSA"]
```

## 4.4 StateŌĆōFeatureŌĆōWeight Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["Discrete / Continuous State s"] --> phi["Mapping Phi: s in S to x s in R^n"]
    phi --> X["Feature Vector x s"]
    X --> M["Matrix Multiply with w"]
    W["Weight Vector w in R^n"] --> M
    M --> V["Scalar Value Estimate v_hat"]
```

## 4.5 Comparison: Tabular vs Linear Function Approximation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["Tabular: One weight per state"] --> O1["Memory: O to the |S|"]
    T2["No generalization"] --> O2
    L1["Linear FA: One weight per feature"] --> O3["Memory: O to the n where n at most |S|"]
    L2["Shares weights across similar states"] --> O4
    L3["Smooth interpolation between states"] --> O5["Quality depends on feature choice"]
    O2 --> con["Pros: Exact, Simple"]
    O4 --> con
    O5 --> lim["Cons: Bias if features poor"]
    O1 --> con2["Cons: Scalability, No Generalization"]
    O3 --> con2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
