# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer)

### **Q1. Define linear function approximation in RL and write the expression for the approximate value function.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Linear function approximation is a method in Reinforcement Learning used when the state space is large or continuous, in which the value function is represented as a **linear function of a feature vector** $x(s)$ associated with each state $s$.

$$\hat{v}(s, w) = w^{T}\,x(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i\,x_i(s)$$

Here $w \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the learnable weight vector and $x(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the fixed feature vector representing state $s$.

---

### **Q2. What is the difference between gradient and semi-gradient methods in linear FA?** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Gradient Method (MC) | Semi-Gradient Method (TD) |
|---|---|---|
| Target | $G_t$ (true return) | $R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{v}(S_{t+1}, w)$ (bootstrapped) |
| Bias | Unbiased | Biased during training |
| Convergence | Global optimum of $\overline{VE}$ | Fixed point $w_{TD}$ with bounded MSVE |
| Used when | Episodes end naturally | Continuing tasks, online learning |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Define linear function approximation. With a neat diagram explain how the value function is approximated as a linear combination of features. State two advantages of LFA over tabular methods. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the update rule for the semi-gradient TD(0) algorithm for linear function approximation. Show clearly why the method is called "semi-gradient". **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question A**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Definition & Diagram

**Definition:** Linear function approximation expresses the value of a state $s$ as:

$$\hat{v}(s, w) = w^{T}\,x(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i(s)$$

where $x(s) = (x_1(s), x_2(s), \dots, x_n(s))^{T}$ is a fixed feature vector for state $s$ and $w$ is the learnable weight vector.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["State s"] --> X["Feature Vector x s"]
    X --> M["Matrix Multiply"]
    W["Weight Vector w"] --> M
    M --> V["Estimate v_hat s, w"]
```

**Advantages over Tabular Methods:**

1. **Generalization:** Shares weights across similar states, so unseen states can still be estimated.
2. **Memory efficient:** Stores only $n$ weights instead of one value per state; $n \ll \vert S \vert$.
3. **Scalability:** Can handle continuous / very large state spaces.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Derivation of Semi-Gradient TD(0)

**Step 1:** Define the TD error at time $t$:

$$\delta_t \triangleq R_{t+1} + \gamma\,\hat{v}(S_{t+1}, w) - \hat{v}(S_t, w)$$

**Step 2:** We wish to minimize the squared TD error $E_t = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\delta_t^{2}$. Compute the gradient:

$$\frac{\partial E_t}{\partial w} = \delta_t \cdot \frac{\partial \delta_t}{\partial w}$$

**Step 3:** Now $\delta_t$ depends on $w$ in two places: through $\hat{v}(S_{t+1}, w)$ AND through $\hat{v}(S_t, w)$. In a true gradient descent, we would compute both. In **semi-gradient**, we ignore the dependency through $\hat{v}(S_{t+1}, w)$:

$$\frac{\partial \delta_t}{\partial w} \bigg\vert_{semi} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial w}\hat{v}(S_t, w) = -x(S_t)$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial E_t}{\partial w}\bigg\vert_{semi} = -\delta_t\,x(S_t)$$

**Step 5:** Perform gradient descent with step size $\alpha$:

$$w_{t+1} = w_t - \alpha\,\bigl(-\delta_t\,x(S_t)\bigr) = w_t + \alpha\,\delta_t\,x(S_t)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $\delta_t$:

$$\boxed{\,w_{t+1} = w_t + \alpha\,\bigl[R_{t+1} + \gamma\,w_t^{T}x(S_{t+1}) - w_t^{T}x(S_t)\bigr]\,x(S_t)\,}$$

**Why "semi"**: Because we differentiate only one of the two $\hat{v}$ terms in the TD error, ignoring the dependence of the bootstrap target on $w$. This makes the method computationally simple but introduces bias ŌĆö it is no longer a true gradient of any well-defined objective function during on-policy learning.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024, Module 4 ŌĆö Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Define the Mean Squared Value Error ($\overline{VE}$) for linear function approximation. Derive the gradient of $\overline{VE}$ with respect to $w$ and obtain the Gradient Monte-Carlo update rule. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Least-Squares TD (LSTD) algorithm. Derive the normal equations and write the closed-form solution. State two advantages and two disadvantages of LSTD. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question B**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö MSVE & Gradient MC Update

**Definition of MSVE:** The Mean Squared Value Error quantifies the discrepancy between the true value function $v_\pi(s)$ and the linear approximation $\hat{v}(s, w)$:

$$\overline{VE}(w) = \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \mu(s)\,\bigl[v_\pi(s) - \hat{v}(s, w)\bigr]^{2}$$

where $\mu(s) \geq 0$, $\sum_s \mu(s) = 1$, is the on-policy distribution.

**Gradient Derivation:**

$$\nabla_w \overline{VE}(w) = 2 \sum_s \mu(s)\,\bigl[v_\pi(s) - \hat{v}(s, w)\bigr]\cdot\bigl(-\nabla_w \hat{v}(s, w)\bigr)$$

For linear FA, $\nabla_w \hat{v}(s, w) = x(s)$, so:

$$\nabla_w \overline{VE}(w) = -2 \sum_s \mu(s)\,\bigl[v_\pi(s) - \hat{v}(s, w)\bigr]\,x(s)$$

**Sample-based update** (state $S_t$ drawn from $\mu$, with $G_t \approx v_\pi(S_t)$):

$$w_{t+1} = w_t + \alpha\,\bigl[G_t - \hat{v}(S_t, w_t)\bigr]\,x(S_t)$$

This is the **Gradient Monte-Carlo** rule. Since $G_t$ does not depend on $w$, this is a true SGD step. It converges to the global minimum of $\overline{VE}(w)$.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Least-Squares TD (LSTD)

**Idea:** LSTD computes $w_{TD} = A^{-1} b$ directly from data, where $A$ and $b$ are the matrices appearing in the fixed-point equation of linear TD(0).

**Derivation:** Set $\mathbb{E}[\delta_t\,x(S_t)] = 0$:

$$\mathbb{E}\bigl[\bigl(R_{t+1} + \gamma\,w^{T}x_{t+1} - w^{T}x_t\bigr)x_t\bigr] = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\underbrace{\mathbb{E}[x_t(x_t - \gamma x_{t+1})^{T}]}_{A}\,w = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[R_{t+1}\,x_t]}_{b}$$

**Closed-form solution:**

$$\boxed{\,w_{TD} = A^{-1}\,b\,}$$

In practice, with ridge regularization $\varepsilon I$:

$$\hat{w} = \bigl(\hat{A} + \varepsilon I\bigr)^{-1}\hat{b}$$

**Algorithm:**
1. Initialize $\hat{A} = \varepsilon I$, $\hat{b} = 0$.
2. For each transition $(S_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$:
   - $\hat{A} \leftarrow \hat{A} + x_t(x_t - \gamma x_{t+1})^{T}$
   - $\hat{b} \leftarrow \hat{b} + R_{t+1}\,x_t$
3. Solve $w = \hat{A}^{-1}\hat{b}$.

**Advantages:**

1. **Sample-efficient:** Uses data more effectively than gradient methods; ideal for batch settings.
2. **No step-size tuning:** $\alpha$ is not required once $A$ and $b$ are formed.
3. **Lower asymptotic MSE:** Generally lower $\overline{VE}$ than semi-gradient TD.

**Disadvantages:**

1. **Computational cost:** $O(n^2)$ memory and $O(n^3)$ for matrix inversion (mitigated by recursive LSTD with ShermanŌĆōMorrison, reducing to $O(n^2)$).
2. **Batch algorithm:** Cannot easily incorporate online forgetting or non-stationarity.
3. **Numerical issues:** $A$ can be near-singular with redundant features.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in Linear FA questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to explain "semi"** in semi-gradient ŌĆö examiners *always* ask why it is called semi-gradient. (ŌĆō2 marks typical)
> 2. **Mixing up $\overline{VE}$ and $E[\delta^{2}]$** ŌĆö these are different objectives! $\overline{VE}$ is the Mean Squared Value Error, while $E[\delta^{2}]$ is the Bellman residual. Semi-gradient TD does not minimize either during learning; it only reaches a *fixed point* that approximately minimizes $\overline{VE}$ within the linear subspace.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\mu(s)$ term** in the MSVE definition. Always mention the on-policy distribution.
> 4. **Writing LSTD without ridge regularization** ŌĆö practical implementations need $\varepsilon I$ to avoid $A$ being singular.
> 5. **Confusing feature vector $x(s)$ with weight vector $w$** ŌĆö both live in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ but $x$ is fixed (input) and $w$ is learnable (parameters).
> 6. **Not specifying gradient independence:** "$\partial \hat{v}/\partial w = x(s)$" is a 1-marker that students often skip.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ­¤ōŗ

- **Linear FA formula:** $\hat{v}(s,w) = w^{T}x(s)$ ŌĆö the *only* learnable parameters are the weights $w$.
- **Gradient is independent of $w$:** $\nabla_w \hat{v}(s,w) = x(s)$. This is the *key* reason linear methods have strong theory.
- **MSVE objective:** $\overline{VE}(w) = \sum_s \mu(s)[v_\pi(s) - \hat{v}(s,w)]^{2}$; $\mu(s)$ = on-policy state distribution.
- **Gradient MC update:** $w \leftarrow w + \alpha[G_t - \hat{v}(S_t, w)]x(S_t)$ ŌĆö unbiased, converges to global optimum of $\overline{VE}$.
- **Semi-gradient TD(0) update:** $w \leftarrow w + \alpha\,\delta_t\,x(S_t)$ where $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma\hat{v}(S_{t+1},w) - \hat{v}(S_t,w)$. Biased, but converges to $w_{TD}$.
- **LSTD closed form:** $w_{TD} = A^{-1}b$ with $A = \mathbb{E}[x_t(x_t - \gamma x_{t+1})^{T}]$ and $b = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1}x_t]$.
- **Convergence bound for semi-gradient TD:** $\overline{VE}(w_{TD}) \leq \frac{1}{1-\gamma}\min_w \overline{VE}(w)$.
- **Eligibility trace update:** $z_t = \gamma\lambda z_{t-1} + x(S_t)$; update: $w \leftarrow w + \alpha\,\delta_t z_t$.
- **Always state ridge term** $\varepsilon I$ for LSTD in numerical answers.
- **Be precise:** "Linear in weights" is the operative phrase ŌĆö even if features $x_i(s)$ are non-linear in $s$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neural Networks for Function Approximation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT868 - INTRODUCTION TO REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Neural Networks for Function Approximation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 ŌĆö Neural Networks for Function Approximation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Function Approximation** in Reinforcement Learning (RL) refers to the use of a parameterized function, $f_\theta : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ (or $f_\theta : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$), with learnable parameters $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^d$, to estimate the true value function, action-value function, or policy of an MDP when the state and/or action spaces are too large (or continuous) to be represented as a lookup table.

When the approximator is a **Neural Network (NN)**, the paradigm is called **Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)**. A neural network with $L$ layers realizes a composition of affine transformations and nonlinear activation functions:

$$f_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma_L \bigl( W_L \sigma_{L-1}( W_{L-1} \cdots \sigma_1( W_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1) \cdots + \mathbf{b}_{L-1}) + \mathbf{b}_L \bigr)$$

where $\theta = \{ W_l, \mathbf{b}_l \}_{l=1}^{L}$ is the parameter vector, $W_l$ is the weight matrix of layer $l$, $\mathbf{b}_l$ is the bias vector, and $\sigma_l$ denotes the (possibly nonlinear) activation function of layer $l$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Neural networks are introduced in RL to overcome the *curse of dimensionality*. Tabular methods (Q-tables, policy tables) fail when $|\mathcal{S}|$ or $|\mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A}|$ is astronomical (e.g., pixel observations, robotic joint angles). The NN compresses the value landscape into a continuous, differentiable surface.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a student preparing for a *million-question* exam on geography. It is impossible to memorize every answer. Instead, the student learns a *mental model* ŌĆö "Capitals are usually the largest cities; coastal cities are warmer; northern latitudes are colder in winter." Given a new city name, the student can *generalize* an answer even without ever having seen the exact question.

A neural network in RL is exactly this **mental model**:

- The **inputs** $\mathbf{x} = \phi(s)$ are *features* extracted from the state $s$ (pixel embeddings, sensor readings).
- The **parameters** $\theta$ are the *internal knowledge* the network has built from past experience.
- The **output** $\hat{Q}(s, a; \theta)$ is the student's *best guess* of the long-term reward.
- The **training signal** is the Bellman *consistency error* ŌĆö "your guess about $s$ should match the reward you received plus your guess about the next state $s'$."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Supervised Learning:** In supervised learning, the network is given a fixed labeled dataset $(x_i, y_i)$. In RL, the *labels themselves* (the TD-targets $y_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a'; \theta^-)$) are *generated by the very network being trained*. This makes RL training fundamentally non-stationary and unstable ŌĆö a core reason for techniques like target networks and experience replay.

### 1.3 Why Neural Networks (and not linear models)?

| Property | Linear / Tabular | Neural Network |
|---|---|---|
| Capacity | Limited to linear decision boundaries | Universal approximator (given enough hidden units) |
| Generalization | Poor in high-dim spaces | Excellent via representation learning |
| Computational Cost | $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert \vert \mathcal{A} \vert)$ storage | $O(d \cdot L)$ per inference (fixed) |
| Stability | Convergent guarantees | Requires tricks (target net, replay, clipping) |

The **Universal Approximation Theorem** (Cybenko, 1989; Hornik, 1991) guarantees that a feedforward NN with a single hidden layer and sufficiently many neurons can approximate *any* continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary precision. This is the theoretical foundation that justifies using NNs as function approximators in RL.

### 1.4 The Three Roles of Neural Networks in RL

A neural network in RL can play one of three functional roles:

1. **Value Approximator** ŌĆö $V_\theta(s)$ or $Q_\theta(s, a)$ to estimate expected return.
2. **Policy Approximator** ŌĆö $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ producing action probabilities (the *actor*).
3. **Model Approximator** ŌĆö $T_\theta(s', r \mid s, a)$ predicting environment dynamics (used in model-based RL).

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 2024 exams:** A high-yield question frequently asked is *"Compare the use of neural networks as value, policy, and model approximators."* Always mention that combining a value net (critic) with a policy net (actor) gives the celebrated **Actor-Critic** family.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Feedforward Neural Network

A feedforward neural network (FFNN) is the canonical architecture used in early DRL works (DQN, DDPG, A2C). It consists of:

- **Input layer** of size $\dim(\phi(s))$ ŌĆö the feature vector.
- **One or more hidden layers** of size $h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_L$.
- **Output layer** of size $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ (one scalar Q-value per discrete action) or size 1 (for a state-value network).

The *forward pass* computes:

$$\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = \phi(s)$$
$$\mathbf{h}^{(l)} = \sigma( W_l \, \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}_l ), \quad l = 1, 2, \ldots, L$$
$$\hat{y} = \mathbf{h}^{(L)} \quad \text{(or} \;\; W_{L+1} \mathbf{h}^{(L)} + \mathbf{b}_{L+1} \text{ for regression head)}$$

### 2.2 Activation Functions ŌĆö The "Why" Behind Nonlinearity

Without nonlinearity $\sigma$, stacking layers is mathematically equivalent to a single linear map (since composition of linear maps is linear). Hence activation is mandatory.

| Function | Formula | Range | Derivative | Use in DRL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ReLU | $\sigma(x) = \max(0, x)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $\sigma'(x) = \mathbb{1}[x > 0]$ | **Default** in hidden layers of DQN, PPO, SAC |
| Leaky ReLU | $\sigma(x) = \max(\alpha x, x)$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | $\alpha$ or 1 | Fixes *dying ReLU* problem |
| Tanh | $\sigma(x) = \tanh(x)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $1 - \tanh^2(x)$ | Common in policy networks (zero-centered) |
| Sigmoid | $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | $\sigma(x)(1 - \sigma(x))$ | Output layer for Bernoulli policy |
| Softmax | $\sigma_i(\mathbf{z}) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Probability simplex | $\sigma_i(\delta_{ij} - \sigma_j)$ | Discrete action probability output |
| Linear | $\sigma(x) = x$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | $1$ | Output layer for Q-value regression |

### 2.3 Loss Functions for Value-Based Deep RL

Given a transition $(s, a, r, s')$, the **Bellman target** is:

$$y = r + \gamma \, \max_{a' \in \mathcal{A}} Q_\theta(s', a')$$

The network's loss is then a *regression* between the prediction $Q_\theta(s, a)$ and the target $y$:

| Loss | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $L(\theta) = \bigl(Q_\theta(s, a) - y \bigr)^2$ | Standard, but sensitive to outliers |
| Huber Loss | $L(\theta) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}\delta^2 & \vert\delta\vert \le \kappa \\ \kappa \bigl(\vert\delta\vert - \frac{1}{2}\kappa\bigr) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Quadratic near 0, linear in tails ŌĆö used in DQN to reduce exploding gradients |
| Bellman Residual | $L(\theta) = \bigl(r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_{\theta^-}(s', a') - Q_\theta(s, a)\bigr)^2$ | True DQN objective |
| Cross-Entropy (Policy) | $L(\theta) = -\sum_a \pi^*(a \mid s) \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ | Used for policy networks (PPO, A2C) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a separate target network $\theta^-$?** The target $y$ depends on $\theta$ itself! This creates a *chasing-your-own-tail* problem called **the deadly triad** (off-policy + function approximation + bootstrapping). The fix (Mnih et al., 2015) is to maintain a *frozen* copy of parameters $\theta^-$ and update it only every $C$ steps: $\theta^- \leftarrow \theta$.

### 2.4 Gradient Descent Family Used in Deep RL

Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) and its variants minimize $L(\theta)$ by iterating:

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k - \alpha_k \, \hat{\mathbf{g}}_k$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{g}}_k$ is a noisy (mini-batch) estimate of $\nabla_\theta L(\theta_k)$, and $\alpha_k$ is the learning rate.

| Optimizer | Update Rule | Why it matters in DRL |
|---|---|---|
| **Plain SGD** | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha \nabla L$ | Baseline; unstable for non-convex NN losses |
| **SGD + Momentum** | $\mathbf{v} \leftarrow \beta \mathbf{v} + \nabla L; \;\; \theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha \mathbf{v}$ | Smooths noisy RL gradients |
| **RMSProp** | $\mathbf{s} \leftarrow \gamma \mathbf{s} + (1-\gamma)(\nabla L)^2; \;\; \theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha \frac{\nabla L}{\sqrt{\mathbf{s}} + \epsilon}$ | Adapts LR per-parameter ŌĆö great for sparse reward |
| **Adam** | Combines momentum + RMSProp; bias-corrected moments | **Default in DRL** (PPO, SAC, DQN) |

### 2.5 The Backpropagation Algorithm

Backpropagation is the algorithm that efficiently computes $\nabla_\theta L$ by applying the chain rule from the output layer back to the input. For a loss $L$ and layer-$l$ activations $\mathbf{h}^{(l)}$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_l} = \delta^{(l)} \, (\mathbf{h}^{(l-1)})^\top, \quad \text{where} \;\; \delta^{(l)} = \bigl( (W_{l+1})^\top \delta^{(l+1)} \bigr) \odot \sigma'(z^{(l)})$$

and $\delta^{(L)} = \nabla_{\mathbf{h}^{(L)}} L \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$ is initialized at the output layer. The $\odot$ symbol denotes the element-wise (Hadamard) product.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The cheat sheet below contains every equation a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the *Neural Networks for Function Approximation* topic. Master these and you will clear any Part-A or Part-B question.

| # | Concept | Equation | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Value approximation | $\hat{V}(s; \theta) \approx V^\pi(s)$ | $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^d$ |
| 2 | Q-value approximation | $\hat{Q}(s, a; \theta) \approx Q^\pi(s, a)$ | Discrete actions |
| 3 | Bellman optimality (Q-form) | $Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E} [ R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(S', a') \mid S=s, A=a ]$ | Contraction operator |
| 4 | DQN TD target | $y_i = r_i + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_{\theta^-}(s'_i, a')$ | $\theta^-$ is target net |
| 5 | DQN loss | $L(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,r,s') \sim \mathcal{D}} [ ( y - Q_\theta(s, a) )^2 ]$ | $\mathcal{D}$ is replay buffer |
| 6 | Gradient of DQN loss | $\nabla_\theta L = \mathbb{E} [ 2 (Q_\theta(s,a) - y) \, \nabla_\theta Q_\theta(s, a) ]$ | Used in backprop |
| 7 | Forward pass | $\mathbf{h}^{(l)} = \sigma( W_l \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}_l )$ | Per layer $l$ |
| 8 | Backprop delta | $\delta^{(l)} = (W_{l+1}^\top \delta^{(l+1)}) \odot \sigma'(z^{(l)})$ | Chain rule |
| 9 | Parameter update (SGD) | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha \nabla_\theta L$ | $\alpha$ is learning rate |
| 10 | Adam first moment | $\mathbf{m} \leftarrow \beta_1 \mathbf{m} + (1 - \beta_1) \nabla L$ | $\beta_1 = 0.9$ typical |
| 11 | Adam second moment | $\mathbf{v} \leftarrow \beta_2 \mathbf{v} + (1 - \beta_2)(\nabla L)^2$ | $\beta_2 = 0.999$ typical |
| 12 | Adam update | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha \frac{\hat{\mathbf{m}}}{\sqrt{\hat{\mathbf{v}}} + \epsilon}$ | Bias-corrected $\hat{\mathbf{m}}, \hat{\mathbf{v}}$ |
| 13 | ReLU | $\sigma(x) = \max(0, x)$ | Derivative is $\mathbb{1}[x>0]$ |
| 14 | Softmax | $\sigma_i(\mathbf{z}) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Outputs probabilities |
| 15 | Softmax derivative | $\frac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_j} = \sigma_i (\delta_{ij} - \sigma_j)$ | $\delta_{ij}$ is Kronecker delta |
| 16 | Huber loss | $L_\kappa(\delta) = \tfrac{1}{2}\delta^2$ if $\vert\delta\vert \le \kappa$ else $\kappa(\vert\delta\vert - \tfrac{1}{2}\kappa)$ | $\kappa$ typically 1.0 |
| 17 | Replay buffer sampling | $(s, a, r, s') \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{D})$ | i.i.d. uniform over memory |
| 18 | Target net update (hard) | $\theta^- \leftarrow \theta$ every $C$ steps | $C = 10000$ in DQN |
| 19 | Target net update (soft, Polyak) | $\theta^- \leftarrow \tau \theta + (1 - \tau) \theta^-$ | $\tau \in [0.001, 0.01]$ |
| 20 | Universal approximation | $\sup_x \vert f(x) - f_\theta(x) \vert < \epsilon$ for some $h$ | Existence theorem |
| 21 | Policy gradient theorem | $\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E} [ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \, Q^\pi(s, a) ]$ | Actor gradient |
| 22 | Cross-entropy loss | $L = -\sum_a \pi^*(a \mid s) \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ | For policy networks |
| 23 | L2 regularization | $L_{reg} = L + \lambda \Vert \theta \Vert_2^2$ | Weight decay |
| 24 | Gradient clipping | $\mathbf{g} \leftarrow \min(1, \frac{c}{\Vert \mathbf{g} \Vert})\mathbf{g}$ | $c$ is clip threshold |
| 25 | Discount factor | $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ | Trades off immediate vs future |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Neural function approximation is the engine behind every modern RL deployment:

- **Robotics (Boston Dynamics, OpenAI Dactyl):** Policy networks map joint-angle states to motor torques.
- **Game AI (AlphaGo, AlphaZero, OpenAI Five):** Value + policy networks guide Monte-Carlo Tree Search.
- **Autonomous Driving (Wayve, Tesla):** Convolutional NNs extract lane/vehicle features; a policy head outputs steering + acceleration.
- **Recommender Systems (YouTube, Netflix):** The user-item context is the state; the network predicts long-term engagement (a Q-value).
- **LLM Fine-Tuning via RLHF:** A *reward model* is trained as a function approximator from human preferences; PPO optimizes the LLM policy against it.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Gradient of the DQN Loss

We derive the gradient $\nabla_\theta L(\theta)$ used to train a Deep Q-Network. Start with the per-sample Bellman residual:

$$\delta_t = Q_\theta(s_t, a_t) - y_t, \quad \text{where} \;\; y_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \, \max_{a'} Q_{\theta^-}(s_{t+1}, a')$$

The mean-squared loss over a mini-batch of size $N$ is:

$$L(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl( Q_\theta(s_i, a_i) - y_i \bigr)^2$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Differentiate the squared term.** Using the chain rule for a single sample first:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \bigl( Q_\theta(s, a) - y \bigr)^2 = 2 \bigl( Q_\theta(s, a) - y \bigr) \, \frac{\partial Q_\theta(s, a)}{\partial \theta}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Sum across the mini-batch.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2 \bigl( Q_\theta(s_i, a_i) - y_i \bigr) \, \nabla_\theta Q_\theta(s_i, a_i)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Recognize the gradient as a stochastic expectation.** Because the samples are drawn i.i.d. from the replay buffer $\mathcal{D}$:

$$\nabla_\theta L(\theta) = 2 \, \mathbb{E}_{(s, a, r, s') \sim \mathcal{D}} \bigl[ \bigl( Q_\theta(s, a) - y \bigr) \, \nabla_\theta Q_\theta(s, a) \bigr]$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute the Adam-style update.** Combining with the Adam optimizer (where $\mathbf{m}, \mathbf{v}$ are first and second moment estimates) yields the per-step update:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{g}_k &= \nabla_\theta L(\theta_k) \\
\mathbf{m}_k &= \beta_1 \mathbf{m}_{k-1} + (1 - \beta_1) \mathbf{g}_k \\
\mathbf{v}_k &= \beta_2 \mathbf{v}_{k-1} + (1 - \beta_2) \mathbf{g}_k^2 \\
\hat{\mathbf{m}}_k &= \frac{\mathbf{m}_k}{1 - \beta_1^k}, \quad \hat{\mathbf{v}}_k = \frac{\mathbf{v}_k}{1 - \beta_2^k} \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k - \alpha \frac{\hat{\mathbf{m}}_k}{\sqrt{\hat{\mathbf{v}}_k} + \epsilon}
\end{aligned}$$

> This is the exact update used in practice by PPO, SAC, and TD3. The $\epsilon \sim 10^{-8}$ prevents division by zero.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Backpropagation Through a 2-Layer Network

We derive the gradients of a simple network with one hidden layer for the loss $L = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2$, where:

$$\mathbf{h} = \sigma( W_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1 ), \quad \hat{y} = W_2 \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}_2$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Forward pass notation.** Let $z_1 = W_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1$, so $\mathbf{h} = \sigma(z_1)$. Let $z_2 = W_2 \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}_2$, so $\hat{y} = z_2$ (linear output).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute output-layer gradients.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = \hat{y} - y, \quad \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial W_2} = \mathbf{h}^\top, \quad \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial \mathbf{b}_2} = 1$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_2} = (\hat{y} - y) \, \mathbf{h}^\top, \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}_2} = \hat{y} - y$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Backpropagate the error to the hidden layer.** Define $\delta_2 = \hat{y} - y$. Then the error signal at the hidden layer is:

$$\delta_1 = (W_2^\top \delta_2) \odot \sigma'(z_1)$$

The $\odot$ is the element-wise product; $\sigma'$ is the activation's derivative (e.g., $\mathbb{1}[z_1 > 0]$ for ReLU).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Hidden-layer parameter gradients.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_1} = \delta_1 \, \mathbf{x}^\top, \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}_1} = \delta_1$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö SGD update.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_2 &\leftarrow W_2 - \alpha \, (\hat{y} - y) \, \mathbf{h}^\top \\
\mathbf{b}_2 &\leftarrow \mathbf{b}_2 - \alpha \, (\hat{y} - y) \\
W_1 &\leftarrow W_1 - \alpha \, \delta_1 \, \mathbf{x}^\top \\
\mathbf{b}_1 &\leftarrow \mathbf{b}_1 - \alpha \, \delta_1
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization to $L$ layers:** The recurrence $\delta_l = (W_{l+1}^\top \delta_{l+1}) \odot \sigma'(z_l)$ is the *backpropagation algorithm*. Every modern auto-differentiation library (PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX) is a runtime implementation of this recurrence on a computational graph.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Softmax + Cross-Entropy Gradient (for Policy Networks)

For a discrete policy network with output probabilities $p_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ and cross-entropy loss $L = -\sum_i y_i \log p_i$ (where $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is the one-hot action taken), the gradient with respect to the *logits* $z_i$ has a beautifully simple form.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Differentiate the softmax.** Use the quotient rule on $p_k = e^{z_k} / Z$ where $Z = \sum_j e^{z_j}$:

$$\frac{\partial p_k}{\partial z_i} = \frac{e^{z_k} \delta_{ik} Z - e^{z_k} e^{z_i}}{Z^2} = p_k (\delta_{ik} - p_i)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate the cross-entropy.** With $L = -\sum_k y_k \log p_k$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_i} = -\sum_k y_k \frac{1}{p_k} \frac{\partial p_k}{\partial z_i} = -\sum_k y_k (\delta_{ik} - p_i) = -\sum_k y_k \delta_{ik} + p_i \sum_k y_k = -y_i + p_i$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Final result.** The gradient of the cross-entropy w.r.t. the logits is just *prediction minus ground truth*:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_i} = p_i - y_i$$

This compact result is why cross-entropy + softmax is the universal choice for the *output layer of policy networks* in PPO, A2C, and the categorical DQN.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Why the Target Network Stabilizes Training

Consider the Bellman optimality operator $\mathcal{T}$ acting on a Q-function:

$$\mathcal{T} Q(s, a) = \mathbb{E} \bigl[ R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S', a') \mid S=s, A=a \bigr]$$

**Contraction property:** For any two Q-functions $Q_1, Q_2$:

$$\Vert \mathcal{T} Q_1 - \mathcal{T} Q_2 \Vert_\infty \le \gamma \Vert Q_1 - Q_2 \Vert_\infty$$

This is a consequence of the **max** being non-expansive (Lipschitz constant 1) and the discount factor $\gamma < 1$. Repeated application $\mathcal{T}^k Q \to Q^*$ exponentially.

**Problem with online updates:** When we replace $Q$ in the target by our *current* parameter $Q_{\theta_k}$, the operator becomes *implicitly* a function of $\theta_k$:

$$y_k = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_{\theta_k}(s', a')$$

The semi-gradient $\nabla_\theta (Q_\theta(s,a) - y_k)^2$ then *chases a moving target*, which can diverge.

**Fix ŌĆö target network $\theta^-$:** Define $y_k = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_{\theta^-}(s', a')$ with $\theta^-$ frozen for $C$ steps. The gradient is now a *true* semi-gradient and the operator regains its contraction-like properties empirically. This was the key innovation in the original DQN paper (Mnih et al., *Nature* 2015).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Loss vs Architecture vs Task

| Task Type | State Space | Recommended Architecture | Loss | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CartPole (toy) | 4-dim | 2-layer FFNN, 64 units, ReLU | MSE on TD error | Stable, fast |
| Atari pixels | $84 \times 84 \times 4$ | CNN (3 conv + 2 FC) | Huber on TD error | DQN-style |
| MuJoCo locomotion | Joint angles/velocities | 2-layer FFNN, 256 units, Tanh | MSE (Q) / Policy gradient (Actor) | SAC, TD3, PPO |
| Go (board) | $19 \times 19$ board | CNN + ResNet blocks | Cross-entropy + MSE | AlphaZero |
| LLM alignment | Token sequences | Transformer (billions of params) | PPO clipped surrogate | RLHF |

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 1-hidden-layer network takes $\mathbf{x} = [1, 0]^\top$. The hidden layer has weights $W_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, biases $\mathbf{b}_1 = [0, 0]^\top$, and uses ReLU. The output layer has $W_2 = [1, \; 1]$, $b_2 = 0$. The target is $y = 2$. Compute the loss $L = \frac{1}{2}(\hat{y} - y)^2$ and the gradient w.r.t. $W_1$ using learning rate $\alpha = 0.1$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Forward pass.**

$$z_1 = W_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1 = [1, 0]^\top$$
$$\mathbf{h} = \sigma([1, 0]^\top) = [1, 0]^\top$$
$$\hat{y} = W_2 \mathbf{h} + b_2 = (1)(1) + (1)(0) + 0 = 1$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Loss.**

$$L = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 2)^2 = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Backward pass at output.** $\delta_2 = \hat{y} - y = 1 - 2 = -1$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Backward pass to hidden layer.** $\delta_1 = (W_2^\top \delta_2) \odot \sigma'(z_1) = [-1, -1]^\top \odot [1, 0]^\top = [-1, 0]^\top$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Update $W_1$.**

$$\nabla_{W_1} L = \delta_1 \mathbf{x}^\top = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} [1, 0] = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$W_1 \leftarrow W_1 - \alpha \nabla_{W_1} L = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} - 0.1 \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Interpretation:** The loss is 0.5, the gradient shows the network should *increase* $W_1[0,0]$ (to raise $\hat{y}$ towards $y=2$), which matches the +0.1 update.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Deep Q-Network (DQN) Architecture ŌĆö Block Diagram

The diagram below maps the *full functional flow* of a DQN system: from raw state observation, through the convolutional feature extractor, into the Q-head, the action selection (epsilon-greedy), the experience-replay buffer, and the target network used to compute the Bellman target.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    in1[Raw State s] --> fe1[Preprocess: phi s]
    fe1 --> cnn1[Conv Layer 1: 32 filters 8x8 stride 4 ReLU]
    cnn1 --> cnn2[Conv Layer 2: 64 filters 4x4 stride 2 ReLU]
    cnn2 --> cnn3[Conv Layer 3: 64 filters 3x3 stride 1 ReLU]
    cnn3 --> fc1[Fully Connected 512 units ReLU]
    fc1 --> outHead[Q Head: Output vector of size action count]
    outHead --> qVals[Q theta for all discrete actions a]
    qVals --> eps[epsilon greedy action selector]
    eps --> actOut[Action a played in environment]
    actOut --> env[Environment step]
    env --> rew[Reward r and next state s prime]
    rew --> buf[Experience Replay Buffer D]
    qVals --> tgt[Target Network Q theta minus frozen]
    buf --> sampler[Uniform mini batch sampling]
    sampler --> loss[Bellman Loss: y minus Q theta squared]
    tgt --> loss
    loss --> opt[Adam Optimizer]
    opt --> cnn1
    tgt --> hardUpd[Hard update every C steps: theta minus equals theta]
    opt --> hardUpd
```

### 4.2 Actor-Critic (A2C) Architecture

The Actor-Critic framework pairs a *policy network* (the actor, which chooses actions) with a *value network* (the critic, which evaluates states). The TD-error from the critic reduces the variance of the actor's gradient estimate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[State s] --> sh[Shared Trunk: 2 layer FFNN 128 units Tanh]
    sh --> actor[Actor Head: Softmax over discrete actions]
    sh --> critic[Critic Head: Linear scalar V s]
    actor --> piDist[Policy pi theta a given s]
    piDist --> act2[Action a sampled]
    act2 --> env2[Environment]
    env2 --> rt[Reward r and next state s prime]
    critic --> val[V s theta]
    rt --> tdErr[TD error: delta equals r plus gamma V s prime minus V s]
    val --> tdErr
    tdErr --> critLoss[Critic Loss: delta squared]
    tdErr --> actLoss[Actor Loss: minus log pi a given s times delta]
    actLoss --> opt2[Adam update on theta]
    critLoss --> opt2
    opt2 --> sh
```

### 4.3 Training Loop: The Deadly Triad & Its Remedies

The flowchart below shows the *complete optimization pipeline* of a value-based deep RL algorithm and highlights the three classic stabilization techniques: target network, experience replay, and gradient clipping.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    init[Initialize theta and theta minus randomly] --> loop[For each training step]
    loop --> actSel[Select action a using epsilon greedy on Q theta s a]
    actSel --> envStep[Execute a observe r s prime]
    envStep --> store[Store transition in replay buffer D]
    store --> sampleCheck{Is buffer size greater than warmup}
    sampleCheck -- no --> loop
    sampleCheck -- yes --> mini[Sample mini batch from D]
    mini --> targetComp[Compute target y equals r plus gamma max Q theta minus s prime a prime]
    targetComp --> lossComp[Loss equals mean square y minus Q theta s a]
    lossComp --> gradComp[Backprop gradients via chain rule]
    gradComp --> clipCheck[Gradient clipping at norm threshold c]
    clipCheck --> adamUpd[Adam update of theta]
    adamUpd --> syncCheck{Step mod C equals 0}
    syncCheck -- yes --> hardSync[Set theta minus equals theta]
    syncSync[No sync] --> loop
    syncCheck -- yes --> loop
    hardSync --> loop
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note (KTU Examiners):** All node identifiers in the diagrams above follow the *alpha-prefix rule* (`in1`, `fe1`, `cnn1`, `cnn2`, `cnn3`, `fc1`, `outHead`, `qVals`, `eps`, `actOut`, `env`, `rew`, `buf`, `tgt`, `sampler`, `loss`, `opt`, `hardUpd`, `s1`, `sh`, `actor`, `critic`, `piDist`, `act2`, `env2`, `rt`, `val`, `tdErr`, `critLoss`, `actLoss`, `opt2`, `init`, `loop`, `actSel`, `envStep`, `store`, `sampleCheck`, `mini`, `targetComp`, `lossComp`, `gradComp`, `clipCheck`, `adamUpd`, `syncCheck`, `hardSync`, `syncSync`) so they render correctly without colliding with reserved Mermaid keywords.
